title: MCCT's "A Grand Night for Singing"
description:
A Grand Night for Singing offers a collection of the greatest love songs in music theatre history given to us by the incomparable writing duo, Richard Rodgers and Oscar Hammerstein II. Featuring songs from the musicals Oklahoma!, The Sound of Music, Carousel, The King and I, South Pacific, Cinderella and many more! With a modern setting and perspective, MCCT's production will reveal how relevant Oscar Hammerstein II's words still are and how Richard Rodger's music will always make audiences' imaginations soar.

Tickets at http://mcct.ludus.com

title: New Music Ensemble – David Dzubay, director; Caroline KyungA Ahn, guest composer; Kyung Sun Lee, violin; Eric Kim, cello
description: Enjoy this performance from almost anywhere in the world viaLIVE@jacobs!Repertoire  Jieun Ok: Domino (2025, premiere)  Beomseok Yoo: Vacant Skies (2025, premiere)  Yie Eun Chun: Under Construction (2025, premiere)Caroline KyungA Ahn: Frac-tured (2025, premiere)David Dzubay: Prismatic Variations (2025, premiere)About the DirectorDavid Dzubay's compositions have been performed in the United States, Europe, Canada, Mexico, and Asia by the symphony orchestras of Aspen, Atlanta, Baltimore, Boston, Cincinnati, Detroit, Fort Wayne, Guangxi, Guiyang, Honolulu, Kansas City, Louisville, Memphis, Minnesota, Oregon, Oakland, St. Louis and Vancouver; the American Composers Orchestra, National Symphonies of Ireland and Mexico, New World Symphony, National Repertory Orchestra, and New York Youth Symphony; and ensembles including the Grossman Ensemble, Le Nouvel Ensemble Moderne, Onix, Manhattan and St. Louis Brass Quintets, Voices of Change, the Alexander, Orion and Pacifica string quartets, the League/ISCM, Earplay, and the San Francisco Contemporary Music Players. His music has been championed by conductors including James DePreist, George Hanson, Keith Lockhart, David Loebel, Michael Morgan, Eiji Oue, Richard Pittman, Iván del Prado, Mark Russell Smith, Lawrence Leighton Smith, Michael Stern, Carl Topilow, David Wiley, Kirk Trevor, Thomas Wilkins, and David Zinman. Honors include two Fromm Music Foundation commissions; Guggenheim, MacDowell, Yaddo, Copland House, and Djerassi fellowships; the Kuhmo Chamber Music Festival Composition Competition, Indianapolis Chamber Orchestra Composition Competition, Utah Arts Festival Commission, William Revelli and Walter Beeler Memorial Prizes, Wayne Peterson Prize; and grants from the Aaron Copland Fund for Music for two portrait CDs. Currently professor of music and director of the New Music Ensemble at the Indiana University Jacobs School of Music in Bloomington, Dzubay was previously on the faculty of the University of North Texas in Denton. Since 2011, he has taught composition for three weeks each summer at the Brevard Music Center. From 1995 to 1998, he served as composer-consultant to the Minnesota Orchestra and during the 2005-06 season, he was Meet the Composer/American Symphony Orchestra League Music Alive Composer-in-Residence with the Green Bay Symphony Orchestra. Dzubay has conducted at the Tanglewood, Aspen, and June in Buffalo festivals as well as the National Symphony of Columbia, Grossman Ensemble, League of Composers Orchestra, Pittsburgh New Music Ensemble, Music from China, and Voices of Change.

title: Monthly Birding: Wetlands and Waterfowl
description: Spend a morning at our Carl Ziegler Wetlands with expert birder Gary Langell for a waterfowl birding event! We’ll use binoculars and spotting scopes to observe ducks, geese, and other wetland species as they move through this vibrant habitat. In March of 2025, local eBird reports at the “Dolan Wetlands” included a diverse array of waterfowl, including Northern Shovelers, Gadwall, Green‑winged Teal, American Wigeon, Hooded Mergansers, and more! Come with your questions and curiosity; Gary is amazing at pointing out key traits for species identification! Carl Ziegler Wetlands is not open to the public.

title: 2nd Weekend Work Day Trail Building
description:
Knobstone Hiking Trail Association of Indiana Meetup
Would you to spend a day in some beautiful woods with a delightful group of people building a connecting section of Indiana’s premier long distance hiking trail? Come join us as we continue work on the Tecumseh Trail extension portion of the KHT in MMSF. We will be building the new trail including clipping green briar.
Please bring a lunch and water. We will have large hand tools, but please bring any small tools you have such as clippers or a pruning saw. And a backpack is handy to carry everything in. Instructions will be provided for those who need them. We finish about 3:30.
Meet at the Hatfield Ridge Road gate. The gate is locked but we have a key. Go to the big sign on Rosenbaum/Catholic Cemetry Rd.
Join us on our website at http://www.knobstonehikingtrail.org/, on Facebook at [www.facebook.com/groups/2255586117953484](http://www.facebook.com/groups/2255586117953484), and Substack [https://khta.substack.com/](https://khta.substack.com/)

title: Growlers Off Road Adventure Ride
description:
Suitable for Cross, MTB or Gravel Bikes
These rides feature gravel roads, single and double track trails, and just enough pavement to string the aforementioned together into fun, woodsy adventure loops. Rides begin at 6PM through the Fall and Winter months as weather allows and typically last about two hours. Lights are mandatory as we enter the Fall and Winter seasons.
Routes are suitable for mountain bike or gravel bike, with some more turned to one or the other. Rides begin at different locations, primarily associated with Yellowwood State Forest, Brown County State Park, and the Hoosier National Forest in Monroe, Lawrence, and Brown Counties. Specific routes may be dictated by weather conditions, but starting locations, route maps, and gps file links will be posted here in advance.
Expect a lot of trees and few cars
Non-members should sign up for a free trial membership, good for up to three rides, and which includes electronically signing a liability waiver, prior to participation

location: SIDNEY AND LOIS ESKENAZI MUSEUM OF ART - Room Number: Check in on the first floor
description: In this workshop, we begin by looking closely at artwork—asking what we see, think, feel, and wonder. Together, we'll explore the artist's possible message and its social meaning, then turn those reflections into our own creative responses.  Whether you're a writer or simply curious to try something new, you'll find inspiration here and a welcoming community to share the journey.  About the Exhibition   Step into Mulyana: Vital Ecosystems─an imaginative, immersive installation that transforms the museum into a vibrant, crocheted, underwater ecosystem. Inspired by Indonesia's spectacular coral reefs, artist Mulyana (b. 1984, Indonesia) invites you to explore a handcrafted seascape where art and ecology meet—sparking reflection on beauty, fragility, and our shared responsibility to protect the ocean.  What to Bringyour favorite pencil (no pens).  a notebook or paper. Clipboards are available to borrow.  a phone or camera to take a picture of the art for later reference.  Know Before You GoArrive early, check in, and get ready for an on-time start.Readwhere to find us, where to park, accessibility, and museum guidelines.About Your Facilitator  Dedaimia Whitney earned an MFA in fiction from Indiana University under the direction of Scott Russell Sanders. She taught creative writing at Franklin College for many years. She has served the museum as a docent since 2019. Her favorite works of art are those that are both visually stimulating and thought-provoking.
source: Eskenazi Museum of Art, IU Arts & Humanities Institute

title: Color Drawing and Painting Workshop
location: SIDNEY AND LOIS ESKENAZI MUSEUM OF ART - Room Number: Check in with Guest Services on the first floor
description: Discover the joy of drawing in an inspiring setting! Begin by sketching directly from artwork in the collection to sharpen your eye and strengthen your skills. Then, move into the studio to learn about color theory and add color to your artwork.Strengthen fundamental drawing skills  Explore color theory in action  Experiment with color media  Expand your creative expression in a hands-on way  Reserve your spot. If this page says, "Registration has closed" that means the waitlist is full.SuppliesBring your favorite drawing pencil (e.g. 4B), a sketchbook or drawing paper, and a phone or device to photograph artwork (without flash) as we sketch. Additional supplies are provided.  Know Before You GoArrive early to find parking, check in, and get your drawing supplies ready.  At 1:55 p.m. any unclaimed reservations will be released to guests who are standing by waiting for a spot.  Where to find us, where to park, accessibility, and museum guidelines (https://artmuseum.indiana.edu/visit/your-visit.html).  This workshop is recommended for ages 12 and up. Anyone under 18, who is not an IU student, must be accompanied by an adult.  When You ArriveCheck in with Guest Services at the information desk on the first floor, even if you come often and even if you are late. We appreciate having an accurate attendance count.  Store all backpacks and large bags before entering the galleries. A small handbag may be carried at your side.  Get your supplies ready. Attach an 18x24" piece of newsprint to a drawing board.  Cancellations  This workshop often has a waitlist. Please use the cancellation link included with your confirmation email if your plans change.About the Instructor  John Cheek has taught credit and non-credit classes for eight years in portrait painting, landscape painting, palette knife painting, life drawing, beginning drawing, and advanced drawing, as well as in design. He volunteers his time and expertise to facilitate drawing workshops at the Eskenazi Museum of Art.
source: Eskenazi Museum of Art, IU Arts & Humanities Institute

title: DC Global Leadership Forum, Fall 2026: Applications Due!
description: Why you should applyThe D.C. Global Leadership Forum gives Indiana University students a unique opportunity to work and learn in Washington, D.C., gaining direct exposure to government agencies, global institutions, think tanks, and policy leaders. If you want a career in international affairs, public policy, global business, or related fields — this puts you at the heart of decision-making. The program offers flexible terms (Fall, Spring, or Summer) so you can pick what fits your degree path and timeline.Fall ProgramEnroll in four IU/HLS courses taught by IU faculty and practitioner instructors based in D.C.Earn IU credit while living in Washington, D.C.Combine coursework with internships, practicums, or other professional engagements.Courses offered currently include:INTL-I426/I524 – Applied Policy Skills for DCINTL-I300/500 – Policy Writing and Global AffairsINTL-I300/500 – U.S. Foreign PolicyINTL-I310/510 – International SecurityOptional: SGIS-X373: Practicum Experience OR SGIS-S 300: Diplomacy LabOne credit DC Preparation course

title: Exhibit: From the Pampas to Indiana: Discovering Tango in Two Special Collections
location: William & Gayle Cook Music Library - Room Number: Lobby
description: Highlighting recent donations from Pablo Aslan (Fall 2024) and Kristin Wendland (May 2025), this exhibit brings together complementary perspectives that celebrate the passion, history, and enduring legacy of this beloved musical tradition.  On view from February 25 through March 15, 2026, the exhibit invites visitors to explore the sounds, stories, and cultural richness of tango up close. This exhibit is part of the LAMC Tango Festival and is presented in celebration of the Latin American Music Center's 65th Anniversary.  This exhibit is a collaboration of Emma Dederick (LAMC Librarian and Curator of Special Collections) in partnership with Music Librarianship students and LAMC Technical Services Assistants, Lauren O'Connor, curator (MLS/MA Musicology student); Luz Ramirez (undergraduate student in Violin Performance); and Lucy Rissmeyer (MLS/MA Musicology student).  Come experience the rhythm, artistry, and spirit of tango.  The exhibit can be viewed during the Cook Music Library open hours. For the Library's hours, visit: https://libraries.indiana.edu/cook-music-library (https://libraries.indiana.edu/cook-music-library)

title: Moon Over Buffalo - Auditions
location: Brown County Playhouse, 70 S. Van Buren St, Nashville, IN, 47448, United States
description:
Audition Notice:
Moon Over Buffalo
by Ken Ludwig
The Brown County Playhouse and Director Steve Booker will hold auditions at the Brown County Playhouse on March 16 and 17 from 6:30pm – 8:30pm. You can arrive anytime up to 8:00. Actors will read from audition sides, which will be provided when you arrive.
Important Dates: Production dates are May 29, 30, 31, and June 5, 6. Rehearsals will typically be Monday, Tuesday, and Thursday evenings, beginning March 30, in Nashville.
Show Overview: It is the summer of 1953, and George and Charlotte Hay, former Broadway stars, have taken their run-down touring company to Buffalo, New York. In Buffalo they have the intention of running “Cyrano de Bergerac” and “Private Lives” in repertory, all the while grumbling about missed Hollywood opportunities. But on-stage harmony is compromised when George performs an off-stage infidelity, impregnating the company’s ingenue. When Charlotte learns of this, she prepares to run away with the family lawyer, sending lightweight George on a grief-stricken drinking binge. Meanwhile, it  turns out that Frank Capra is headed to town on a talent scouting mission looking to hire the couple for his swashbuckling Scarlet Pimpernel epic. As a result, the Hay family — including scornful, deaf mother-in-law Ethel, determinedly practical daughter Rosalind, and dashing actor Paul, Rosalind’s ex-boyfriend — must work overtime to get sloppy drunk George into his Cyrano hat and nose…. or is it his Elyot Chase smoking jacket? With the entrance of Rosalind’s fiancé, anxious TV weatherman Howard — one man with two mistaken identities — and Richard Maynard, the wealthy lawyer hoping to lure Charlotte away to his mansion — the confusion only intensifies.
Character Overview: The ages shown below are the script suggestions. However, the director encourages anyone interested to audition for the show, even if your age doesn’t match up with certain roles.

George Hay, a traveling actor, age 50s
Charlotte Hay, George’s wife and actress in his company, age 50s
Ethel, Charlotte’s nearly deaf mother, Age 70s
Rosalind, The Hays’ daughter, who left the stage to lead a “normal” life, Age 20s – 30s
Howard, A TV weatherman and Rosalind’s fiancé, Age 20s – 30s
Eileen, An actress in the company who was George’s ‘one-night stand”, Age 20s
Paul, Stage manager for the company and Rosalind’s ex-fiancé, Age 20s – 30s
Richard, A love-sick lawyer who is courting Charlotte, Age 50s

title: CANCELED: Common Threads
location: SIDNEY AND LOIS ESKENAZI MUSEUM OF ART - Room Number: Roehm Family Art-making Event
description: Looking for a welcoming space to crochet, knit, embroider, quilt, and explore other fiber arts? Interested in sharing your experiences and connecting with others while learning new skills? Or even just want a place to vent while you work on your scarf you've been crocheting for 2 years? Look no further; join us for Common Threads!  Common Threads is a free program open to the public and offers a welcoming environment to share, unwind, and learn! You can bring your own fiber art to work on while working alongside others who enjoy working in fiber and textiles. Or you can try learning something new; we welcome all skill levels! Either way, you're sure to have a good time.  This group meets on the 1st and 3rd Tuesday of each month from 3–4:30 p.m., excluding holidays and museum closures. The group will meet in the Roehm Family Art-making Studio, located inside the Simpson Center for Education on the 2nd floor of the Eskenazi Museum of Art.
source: Eskenazi Museum of Art, IU Arts & Humanities Institute, IU La Casa Latino Cultural Center

title: Mindful Makers
description:
Join us for Mindful Makers, a weekly event built around being creative, meeting new people and feeling more confident in yourself and your wellness. Each week has a theme - come for one or come for all! We're here to have fun and make cool things.
January 21: Semester Vision Boards
Picture your new semester! Create a vision board that will motivate you to achieve this semester. Learn some tips on how to set great goals to help you achieve that vision.

January 28: Meditation and Aromatherapy
Ever been curious about aromatherapy and how it works? Here's your opportunity to learn more! While you're here, we'll also practice deep breathing techniques, progressive relaxation and guided visualization, and other simple techniques that don't require any special skills or equipment

February 4: Crochet the Stress Away
Whether you're a crochet expert or have never picked up a hook in your life, we have a place for you! Join us, learn basic crochet stitches, and talk about ways to have fun on campus and in the community.

February 11: Origami Ornaments and Overcoming
Sometimes life throws us curve balls. In this Mindful Makers, we'll go through the process of folding origami hearts or cranes, and using those to make beautiful ornaments that reflect challenges we've overcome. These cranes carry hope on their wings!

February 18: Air Clay Emotions
What does your angry monster look like? What about your joy? Use air clay to sculpt mini-monsters representing both supportive and challenging emotions, and learn ways of taming your more wild ones. Remember - not all monsters are scary. Sometimes they're pretty great!

February 25: Love Letters to Your Body and Cookie Decorating
Sometimes we need reminders of how great our bodies can be. In this week's MM, you'll learn simple bookbinding skills to make an accordion book, and fill those pages with what we like about ourselves and our bodies. Join our nutritionist, Katie Shepherd, for cookie decorating while you're here!

March 4: Watercolor Your Values
One of the most powerful motivators we have is our personal values. Paint a values portrait, and discuss how you can tie your goals into your values to keep yourself moving forward.

March 11: Sleep Vision Boards
Getting a good night's sleep is critical towards functioning at your best - but truthfully, most of us don't get nearly the quantity or quality of sleep we need. Create a sleep vision board as you learn ways to improve your sleep habits and why sleep is so important!

March 25: Create Your Own Journal
And we do mean from scratch! Learn simple Japanese stitchwork bookbinding to make your own book, decorate it to suit your tastes, and create a journal you can use however you like! (And if you're new to journaling, we can work on that as well!)

April 1: Perler Beads and Mindfulness
Being mindful doesn't take anything special. Sometimes it can be found in the smallest of things - like the little perler beads! Make yourself an ornament while discovering ways mindfulness can be a part of the everyday things you already do!

April 8: Gaming with Friends
Do you know that the oldest known game board is 9000 years old? We've been playing games for a long time! Well, not us personally. We're just going to play for a couple of hours. Come and join us!

April 15: Resinate
No, that's not a typo. Learn the basics of resin crafting and make your own ornaments or magnets. While here, share your favorite hobbies and why they matter to you - maybe you'll inspire someone else!

April 22: Affirmation Jars
At the end of the semester, sometimes we need reminders of what motivates us, makes us special, or just what matters to us. Create your own affirmation jar and fill it up with all those good things. When you're feeling low, pull one out and give yourself a boost!

April 29: Keep Growing
Whether you're looking forward to graduation, a summer off, or one filled with activity, summer is a great time for growth. Share your upcoming opportunities as you decorate a pot and select a new plant to nurture. Like plants, we need care to keep growing, so share your ideas for how you're going to care for yourself!

Hosted by: Health Promotion

Additional Information can be found at: https://beinvolved.indiana.edu/event/11977504

title: Çanakkale Martyrs’ Commemoration and Ramadan Iftar Gathering
description:
The Turkish Student Association at Indiana University is organizing a Çanakkale Martyrs’ Commemoration and Ramadan Iftar Gathering on March 18. This event aims to honor the historical significance of the Gallipoli Campaign (1915), a pivotal moment in World War I that carries deep meaning not only for Türkiye but also for many nations around the world.
Çanakkale represents stories of sacrifice, resilience, and humanity that transcend national boundaries. The campaign involved soldiers from different countries, and over time it has become a symbol of remembrance, mutual respect, and reconciliation. Through this commemoration, we aim to reflect on these shared historical narratives and promote intercultural understanding within the IU community.
In addition to the commemoration, we will host an iftar dinner, marking the breaking of the fast during the holy month of Ramadan. Iftar is a time of reflection, gratitude, and community, and we welcome students, faculty, and community members of all backgrounds to share this meaningful cultural tradition together.

Hosted by: Turkish Student Association at Indiana University

Additional Information can be found at: https://beinvolved.indiana.edu/event/12302054

title: Open Mic Night at The Comedy Attic
location: The Comedy Attic,123 S Walnut St, Bloomington, IN 47408, USA
description:
Every Wednesday at 8PM is Comedy Attic Open Mic Night. Bloomington has one of the best local comedy scenes in the country, and it all starts with our open mic night!

Each comedian is asked to do no more than five minutes of completely original material. While we do not care about foul language in your set, we do not allow racist, homophobic, transphobic or rape/female bashing jokes. If you have to ask if it's racist, homophobic, transphobic or a rape joke, it probably is.

Comedians that wish to participate are asked to e-mail comedyattic@gmail.com mailto:comedyattic@gmail.com . Newcomers and any participants who are not paid professionals will be required to bring audience members to see them. This is non-negotiable. So if you plan on signing up, be sure to invite as many friends as you can. Good luck!

title: Senior Movie Series: Poms
location: Brown County Playhouse, 70 S. Van Buren St, Nashville, IN, 47448, United States
description:
Meredith Funeral Home, in partnership with the historic Brown County Playhouse, is excited to
offer a Free Monthly Movie Series for those aged 60 and over. Enjoy a classic film with friends, free
popcorn, and a soda—all in the comfort of the historic Brown County Playhouse!

When:
MAR 19, 2026 – Poms
APR 16, 2026 – Book Club
May 21, 2026 – Senior Moment
Time: 2:00 p.m. Doors open at 1:00 p.m.
Where: Historic Brown County Playhouse, 70 S Van Buren St. in Nashville, IN
No ticket is necessary, and admission is free for those 60 and over. For those under 60, admission is just
$7.

This initiative is part of the Meredith family’s ongoing commitment to giving back to the community. Bill
and Jamie Meredith, along with their dedicated staff, are excited to create a monthly opportunity for
seniors to connect with others and enjoy a time of fellowship.
Celebrating 50 years of exceptional customer service, Meredith Funeral Home remains locally
owned and operated. Throughout these years, they have earned the trust of the community by
providing compassionate, dignified services at an affordable price.
We invite you to join us for an afternoon of movies, community, and memories. We look forward to
seeing you at the Brown County Playhouse Senior Movie Series!

—————————————-

POMS is an uplifting comedy about a woman (played by Keaton) who moves into a retirement community and starts a cheerleading squad with her fellow residents, including Pam Grier and Jacki Weaver.

title: Wilderness First Aid Courses
description:
Our Wilderness First Aid (WFA) courses will give you the skills needed to respond to medical emergencies when help is far away.
This is a 2-day/16-hour course that features extensive hands-on learning opportunities. Students will walk away with a 2-year WFA certification from Stonehearth Open Learning Opportunities (SOLO), which is one of the oldest wilderness medicine organizations in the world.
Students may also add on CPR modules and/or re-certify for their SOLO Wilderness First Responder (WFR) certification.
Courses start at $275. Cost includes course materials and lunch on Saturday and Sunday. Overnight lodging from Friday–Sunday may be available for participants on a first-come, first-serve basis.
Register for the course here.

Rachel Lichtman Saturday, March 21st at Blockhouse Bar (205 S College) $15 dollars | 21+ Pre-show grooviness: 7pm Screening: 8pm From fondue parties to cocktail lounges to art galleries to cinemas, Programme 4: Make the Scene is an analog, local broadcast television happening designed to be collectively experienced in person, an immersive, wall-to-wall carpeted presentation that transports you to an aspirational world of tomorrow, today. With creator Rachel Lichtman, special guest stars, Rhodes pianos, complimentary matchbooks and more. Escape to a groovy, aspirational world of Programme 4 with Special Guest Stars: Mike Adams as your Master of Ceremonies, Jared Cheek playing soft sounds & Mystic Moods, and Stephen Deusner in-conversation with Programme 4 creator Rachel Lichtman . The happening vibes start "After 6" with pre-show grooviness before the feature presentation at 8pm. Make the Scene will include a feature-length video presentation of Programme 4 , plus creator Rachel Lichtman, special guest stars, talk show-style Q&A’s, Rhodes pianos, and more. Larger productions will evoke broadcast network upfronts of the 60’s and 70’s, a surreal sales presentation featuring a cavalcade of stars and the satirical UHF television and AM radio of Golden Sands. Rachel Lichtman is a visual artist, comedy writer, and conjurer of groovy worlds. Her work has featured a cavalcade of notable actors, comics and musicians, and she has produced, directed and written documentaries, live stage shows, full-length music shows, art installations, title sequences and over four billion music videos. She is currently producing more deep-shag Programme 4 magic and figuring out how to install those lucite stairs that light up when you walk on them. More information about Programme 4: https://www.programme4.tv/

title: ‘Sky Dance’ & Twilight Hike
description: As spring approaches, a flurry of activity begins in the wetlands, including migration, courtship, and rising waters. The chorus of amphibians during this twilight hike will be the soundtrack as we search the sky for the woodcock’s famous mating ritual, the ‘sky dance.’ Join expert naturalist Cathy Meyer and Sycamore Land Trust for an evening exploration of the trails and boardwalk at our iconic Beanblossom Bottoms Nature Preserve. Attendance is limited and registration is required.

title: Understanding Autism
location: First Christian Church Bloomington 205 E Kirkwood
description: A great deal of misinformation has been spread – particularly by our current President and Secretary of Health and Human Services – about the reasons for the rapid rise in diagnosed cases of autism spectrum disorder over the past half-century. Professors Emeritus Tim Londergan and Steve Vigdor will review the expansion of diagnostic criteria for the neurodevelopmental condition(s) known as autism, and how these diagnostic changes have contributed to the rise. They will also show that the totality of epidemiological studies establish no link between autism and vaccines and at most a weak association (not necessarily causal) with prenatal Tylenol usage. Extensive twin studies have established a dominant role for genetics and a minor role for environmental conditions in contributing to autism. Ongoing brain imaging studies of people on the autism spectrum are revealing atypical connectivity issues that are associated with some autistic behaviors.

title: Spring 2026 Colloquium Series with Paulo Ramos
description: The Center for Latin American and Caribbean Studies is proud to present the continuation of its Colloquium Series for Spring 2026.  We welcome Paulo Ramos, former postdoc at the Center for Research on Race & Ethnicity in Society, current visiting scholar of Ostrom Workshop.  Paulo's research project Bodies of Water ("Corpos d'água") explores environmental racism and Black women's relationships to water. Part of his work offers artistic performance that delves into conceptual realms through poetry and corporeal expression, and he has published a volume of poetry also entitled Bodies of Water. Paulo's performance invites a sensory experience while reflecting on environmental racism and transcending racial and class boundaries. Focusing on the connection between water and Black women, Paulo's work highlights the draining of dreams and lives from Black bodies by the state, a situation that dates back to colonial Brazil. The situation Paulo examines constitutes an ongoing cycle of violence deeply rooted in the legacy of slavery and entrenched within institutional frameworks.     The CLACS Spring 2026 Colloquium meets Mondays from February through April, with faculty and guest speakers engaging Latin America and the Caribbean through research on history, culture, language, politics, and the arts. Check the CLACS Calendar (https://events.iu.edu/clacs/) for details.
source: IU Hamilton Lugar School, IU La Casa Latino Cultural Center

title: It's On Us: Bystander Intervention Workshop
description:
The It’s On Us: Bystander Intervention Workshop is a 90-minute, audience-driven bystander intervention program that teaches students the skills needed to help in situations involving alcohol and drug use that could lead to a sexual assault. Students practice helping in real-life scenarios and discuss how alcohol and drug use influences a person’s ability to give and get consent to engage in sexual activity with another person.

It's On Us is required for all first-year and transfer undergraduate students. You must attend one session to fulfill the requirement.

Instructions:

Those who arrive more than 5 minutes after the start time will NOT be allowed into the session and will need to attend a different session

If this is your assigned workshop but you cannot attend it in its entirety, please RSVP for a different workshop.

title: ACC's Student Development Workshop Series presents: "I am perfectly FINE!: How do you balance productivity and self-care?"
location: Asian Culture Center (807 E 10th street, Bloomington, IN)
description: For this workshop, the common myth of productivity and self-efficacy will be discussed to help attendees find out how to feel good about their work and projects. Plus, there will be a discussion (or self-discussion) about finding their own definition of rest and rejuvenation. Hye Ree earned her Doctor of Psychology (Psy.D.) degree in Clinical Psychology with a focus on traumatic stress psychology from Adler University in Chicago. Hye Ree serves as an embedded counselor at the ACC. All Indiana University events, groups, activities, and educational, cultural, and historical observances welcome all IU students and other members of the Indiana University community.For more information, view this event in BeInvolved (https://beinvolved.indiana.edu/event/12168296).

title: Breaking Tradition: America's 250th Birthday!
location: LOWELL E. BAIER HALL/IU MAURER SCHOOL OF LAW - Room Number: 125
description: Professor Randy Kozel of the University of Notre Dame Law School, joined by Professor William Popkin, will explore how we, as law students, lawyers, and judges, interpret our Constitution in light of its history and tradition. What relationship does the history and traditions approach to constitutional interpretation have to other interpretive methods? How does this system of interpretation influence the shaping of American law today? Join us to celebrate America's 250th birthday!  BuffaLouie's will be provided for all attendees

title: It's On Us: Bystander Intervention Workshop
description:
The It’s On Us: Bystander Intervention Workshop is a 90-minute, audience-driven bystander intervention program that teaches students the skills needed to help in situations involving alcohol and drug use that could lead to a sexual assault. Students practice helping in real-life scenarios and discuss how alcohol and drug use influences a person’s ability to give and get consent to engage in sexual activity with another person.

It's On Us is required for all first-year and transfer undergraduate students. You must attend one session to fulfill the requirement.

Instructions:

Those who arrive more than 5 minutes after the start time will NOT be allowed into the session and will need to attend a different session

If this is your assigned workshop but you cannot attend it in its entirety, please RSVP for a different workshop.

All Indiana University events, groups, activities, educational, cultural, and historical observances welcome all IU students and other members of the Indiana University community.

title: Philharmonic Orchestra – Jeffery Meyer, conductor; Jenny Bahk, cello soloist
description: Enjoy this performance from almost anywhere in the world viaLIVE@jacobs!Repertoire  Dvořák: Cello Concerto in B Minor, Op. 104 (1895)Rachmaninoff: Symphonic Dances, Op. 45 (1940)About the Conductor  Distinguished for insightful performances, visionary programming, and wide-ranging collaborations, conductor Jeffery Meyer has captivated audiences around the globe. He is professor of orchestral conducting at the Indiana University Jacobs School of Music as well as artistic partner with the Northwest Sinfonietta, one of the northwest United States' most vibrantly creative ensembles. Artistic director of the St. Petersburg Chamber Philharmonic for two decades, his work with the orchestra was noted for its breadth and innovation. His programming has earned three ASCAP Awards for Adventurous Programming and two Vytautas Marijošius Memorial Awards. A champion of contemporary music, Meyer imagines the orchestra experience as a forum for story, emotion, and civic dialogue. His projects have paired symphonic works with multimedia visionaries and diverse cultural collaborators. His recordings span repertoire from baroque to the present; his most recent, Illuminations: Music of Narong Prangcharoen, was released by Albany Records in 2023. Recent highlights include a multimedia Rite of Spring and Petrushka with the Thailand Philharmonic; Carlos Simon's Graffiti with renowned graffiti artists; a theatrical symphonic concert developed in collaboration with Daniel Bernard Roumain and Marc Bamuthi Joseph; Laura Kaminsky's Piano Concerto with Ursula Oppens (Cedille Records); a semistaged production of Nkeiru Okoye's Invitation to a Die-In; and multiple collaborations with the American Composers Orchestra's EarShot program. Recent and upcoming engagements include the City Chamber Orchestra of Hong Kong, Los Angeles Ballet, Xalapa Symphony, Orquesta Juvenil Universitaria Eduardo Mata, American Composers Orchestra, Texas Festival Orchestra at Round Top, and returns to the Grossman Ensemble, Sichuan Symphony, and Thailand Philharmonic. Passionate about working with young musicians and music education, Meyer is an active adjudicator, guest clinician, and master class teacher. Prior to his appointment at the Jacobs School, he led acclaimed orchestral programs at Arizona State University and Ithaca College. He has given master classes throughout the United States, Canada, Europe, and Asia. Meyer holds degrees in piano and composition and earned his Doctor of Musical Arts in Piano Performance with Gilbert Kalish at Stony Brook University.

title: Center for Survey Research Consultations
description:
Make an appointment now! https://iub.libcal.com/appointments/csr

You have the option to schedule on Zoom or in-person.

The Center for Survey Research consults with faculty, staff, and students across a wide range of substantive disciplines spanning business, education, the social sciences, and the health sciences. We are happy to assist with any qualitative or quantitative research project by providing consultation on:
research design
questionnaire development and review
sample design
online interface and reporting portal development
database and data collection instrument programming including Qualtrics online survey software
usability testing
sample management including high-volume email campaigns
data collection using online, telephone, mail, and face-to-face administration
qualitative methods (semi-structured interviews, focus groups, observations, etc.)
data processing
data analysis

Takes place in the Wells Library Scholars' Commons, first floor of the east tower. No question too big or too small!

title: Eiteljorg Artist In Residence Visiting Lecture: JJ Otero
location: SIDNEY AND LOIS ESKENAZI MUSEUM OF ART
description: 1–2 p.m. Artist talk, Learn more about JJ's artistic journey, creative philosophy, and evolving practice, followed by an open Q+A session, Martin Commons, second floor2–4 p.m. Enjoy hands-on artist programming and a rare look at selected objects from the museum's collections.    JJ Otero (Navajo/Hopi) brings a unique perspective to contemporary Native art. After early success as an award-winning musician and later as a painter and cradleboard maker, he shifted his artistic focus to jewelry and metalsmithing. Since 2018, his work has been featured at prominent Native art markets, including the Heard Museum Art Market, SEEDS Art Market, and the Santa Fe Indian Market.  Guided by the belief that"This moment is perfect and there's nothing lacking,"JJ's approach to texture, stamping, and form demonstrates both technical rigor and an evolving artistic voice.All of these activities are free and open to the public. This program is co-organized by the IU First Nations Educational and Cultural Center and the Eskenazi Museum of Art, with support from the Eiteljorg Museum of American Indians and Western Art.
source: Eskenazi Museum of Art, IU First Nations Center

title: Ostrom Workshop | Research Series: Yaron Weissberg
location: The Ostrom Workshop, 513 N PARK AVE - Room Number: Tocqueville
description: Bio  Yaron Weissberg is a Postdoctoral Researcher at the Research Group for International Politics and Conflict Research. He holds a Bachelor's degree in Political Economy from the University of Lucerne and a Master's degree in Comparative and International Studies from ETH Zürich and the University of Zürich. He completed his Ph.D. at ETH Zrich's International Conflict Research Group, focusing on the intersection of natural resources and conflict. His research examines how the transition to cleaner forms of energy can reduce risks associated with resource-based conflicts, while also exploring new risks that might emerge.Title  Digging Out of Trouble? The Development Potential of Green Minerals in Conflict-Affected RegionsAbstract  The global transition to renewable energy requires substantial increases in green technology mineral (GTM) extraction, including lithium, cobalt, and graphite. While mineral wealth may fuel conflict, resources can also provide employment during violence and revenues for post-conflict reconstruction. This paper examines whether GTMs exhibit such stabilizing characteristics in conflict-affected regions. We argue that GTMs differ fundamentally from "traditional" conflict resources. Unlike oil and diamonds, GTMs lack concentrated value to generate large revenues for armed actors during conflict. Furthermore, their bulky nature requires substantial infrastructure investments, creating local economic spillovers that benefit the local economy and population more broadly. However, GTM extraction remains highly vulnerable to large-scale conflict due to infrastructure dependence and insufficient revenues for firms to justify continued operations under extreme insecurity. Using PRIO-GRID data and UCDP conflict measures for the post-Cold War period, we analyze spatial variation in mining activity and nighttime light emissions across conflict intensities. We find that GTM sites demonstrate economic resilience during localized violence but experience severe disruption during civil wars, with substantial variation by extraction mode. These findings clarify conditions under which resource extraction economically stabilizes rather than destabilizes conflict-affected regions, contributing to our understanding of the green energy transition's security implications.About the Ostrom Workshop Research Series  Learn about innovative research by Graduate Students and Visiting Scholars as they advance the mission of the Ostrom Workshop, building upon the theme of governance to understand and address major societal problems.

title: Grantsmanship 201: Writing an Effective Grant for Artists & Humanists
Ostrom Workshop | Research Series: Jorge Canabal
location: The Ostrom Workshop, 513 N PARK AVE - Room Number: Toqueville
description: Bio  Jorge Acevedo Canabal, MD, is a visiting scholar at the Ostrom Workshop and co-Principal Investigator of the "Team Building for the Cyber-Epidemiology Center" project, which receives funding from an IU 2030 Emerging Frontiers Grant. His past relevant work involves clinical medicine and research focused on novel pathogens and vulnerable populations, disaster response, healthcare crises response, and cybersecurity in healthcare.Title  Counting the Invisible: Expansion and Application of Technological Hazards' Classification to Document Human Harm in Healthcare, Public Health, and BeyondAbstract  Despite growing reliance on digital technologies and connected devices to improve public safety, public health, and healthcare delivery, there exists an identified but not comprehensively described gap in the classification and documentation of failure modes that can result in human harm. This gap is notable precisely because external cause documentation frameworks in vital statistics and clinical settings are not new: existing tools successfully capture harm attributable to natural disasters such as hurricanes, floods, and earthquakes. It is in the specific domain of technological hazards involving digital systems that the most significant classificatory gaps remain. These failure modes can be broadly described as accidental, adversarial, or as the result of cascading effects of failures in the infrastructure upon which the system of interest relies.  During this talk, the presenter will discuss (1) how adoption and adaptation of existing frameworks and tools from several fields of study, including epidemiology, health informatics, and patient safety science, can support the development of cross-disciplinary functional definitions; (2) how adaptation of Ostrom's IAD framework can be useful for systematically identifying, at different levels of observation, relevant feedback loops that can inform targeted responses; and (3) challenges that must be addressed for the development and implementation of applied research that improves current understanding of technological failures' relationship to human harm, and that can inform further development of technologies and interventions that mitigate risk.About the Ostrom Workshop Research Series  Learn about innovative research by Graduate Students and Visiting Scholars as they advance the mission of the Ostrom Workshop, building upon the theme of governance to understand and address major societal problems.

title: 21JPSI/IKS **SPECIAL EVENT** "China's Weaponization of Trade: Korea, Japan, and the Need for Collective Resilience" (Victor Cha (Georgetown/CSIS))
location: Global & International Studies Building - Room Number: GA 0001 (Shreve Auditorium)
description: Public event featuring a public talk and discussion/Q&A with Dr. Victor Cha (https://www.csis.org/people/victor-cha) (Georgetown/CSIS; former NSC Director for Asia) based on his new book: China's Weaponization of Trade: Resistance through Collective Resilience (https://cup.columbia.edu/book/chinas-weaponization-of-trade/9780231564205/) (Columbia University Press, 2026).Co-hosted/co-organized by 21JPSI and IKS. Co-sponsored by the Department of Political Science.About the book:  In recent years, China and the United States have each turned economic interdependence into an instrument of coercion, using their dominant positions in international trade to push states and firms to comply with their political goals. What is distinctive about this form of economic pressure, and how can other countries fight back?  This groundbreaking book explores the weaponization of economic interdependence and its implications for the international order through a wealth of new and original data on China's economic statecraft. Victor D. Cha, Ellen Kim, and Andy Lim examine how and in what ways the United States and China have deployed economic coercion, focusing on China's extensive use of this tactic over the past three decades. They analyze a vast data set that includes more than 600 cases of China's economic bullying of states, companies, and individuals in North America, Asia, and Europe. Cha, Kim, and Lim propose a multilateral strategy of "collective resilience" to counter intimidation, showing how targeted states can band together, leverage trading relationships, and threaten retaliation. Synthesizing new insights from unique trade data with international security expertise, this timely book sheds new light on how China exercises economic power—and it provides a playbook to deter bullies and rebalance the global order.About the speaker:Dr. Victor Cha is president of the Geopolitics and Foreign Policy Department and Korea chair at the Center for Strategic and International Studies (CSIS). He is also a distinguished university professor and professor of government at Georgetown University. From 2021 to 2025, he was appointed by the Biden administration to serve on the Defense Policy Board in an advisory role to the secretary of defense. From 2004 to 2007, he served on the National Security Council (NSC), where he was responsible for Japan, Korea, Australia, New Zealand, and Pacific Island nations. Dr. Cha was the U.S. deputy head of delegation at the Six Party Talks and received two outstanding service commendations during his tenure at the NSC. He is the author of nine books, including the award-winning Alignment Despite Antagonism: The United States–Korea–Japan Security Triangle (Stanford University Press, 1999), which won the 2000 Ohira Book Prize, and The Impossible State: North Korea, Past and Future (Ecco, 2012), selected by Foreign Affairs as a "Best Book on the Asia-Pacific for 2012." His other books are Nuclear North Korea: A Debate on Engagement Strategies (Columbia University Press, 2003); Beyond the Final Score: The Politics of Sport in Asia (Columbia University Press, 2009); Powerplay: Origins of the American Alliance System in Asia (Princeton University Press, 2018); Korea: A New History of South and North (Yale University Press, 2023); The Black Box: Demystifying the Study of Korean Unification and North Korea (Columbia University Press, 2024); North Korea's Sea-Based WMD Capability (Bloomsbury, 2025); and China's Weaponization of Trade: Resistance through Collective Resilience (Columbia University Press, 2026). Dr. Cha is a two-time Fulbright scholar and a former Olin fellow at Harvard University, as well as a former Hoover, Stanford Center for International Security and Cooperation, and Koret fellow at Stanford University. He currently serves on the editorial boards of ten academic journals and is coeditor of the Contemporary Asia book series at Columbia University Press. He serves on the boards of the National Endowment for Democracy and the Korea Society and is a senior fellow at the George W. Bush Institute. Dr. Cha received his PhD, MIA, and BA from Columbia University and a BA with honors from Oxford University.Co-sponsored by 21JPSI, the Institute for Korean Studies, and the East Asia and the World speaker series. Unless otherwise noted, all 21JPSI events are 100% in-person.
source: IU Hamilton Lugar School, IU Kelley School of Business

title: Addressing the Curse Talk
description:
This event’s purpose is to address the generational trauma that the black community
undergoes with the “me vs me” tactic that we tend to take when it comes to mental health that
continues to be passed down. The growth is to help manipulate and address the myth of having
to “suck it up” and not be accused of being “so angry” when we do open up. The end goal is to
allow black students to see that they have a community on campus that indeed believes and
supports them when it comes to mental health.

title: Open Mic Night at The Comedy Attic
location: The Comedy Attic,123 S Walnut St, Bloomington, IN 47408, USA
description:
Every Wednesday at 8PM is Comedy Attic Open Mic Night. Bloomington has one of the best local comedy scenes in the country, and it all starts with our open mic night!

Each comedian is asked to do no more than five minutes of completely original material. While we do not care about foul language in your set, we do not allow racist, homophobic, transphobic or rape/female bashing jokes. If you have to ask if it's racist, homophobic, transphobic or a rape joke, it probably is.

Comedians that wish to participate are asked to e-mail comedyattic@gmail.com mailto:comedyattic@gmail.com . Newcomers and any participants who are not paid professionals will be required to bring audience members to see them. This is non-negotiable. So if you plan on signing up, be sure to invite as many friends as you can. Good luck!

title: The Philadelphia Orchestra
description: The world-renowned Philadelphia Orchestra strives to share the transformative power of music with the widest possible audience and to create joy, connection, and excitement through music in the Philadelphia region, across the country, and around the world. For their Bloomington performance, they'll be led by principal guest conductor Marin Alsop, a pioneering force in classical music and the first woman to serve as the head of major orchestras in the United States, South America, Austria, and Great Britain.     Alsop will lead a program filled with brilliance and heart: Beethoven's Leonore Overture No. 3, a powerful tale of courage and triumph; Brahms's Variations on a Theme of Joseph Haydn, full of wit and wonder; and Schumann's radiant Symphony No. 2, music that soars with hope and resilience. Whether you're a longtime fan or new to orchestral music, this concert promises to captivate.

title: A Conversation on Legal Trends in Music & AI with Dan Amin and Robert Meitus
location: LOWELL E. BAIER HALL/IU MAURER SCHOOL OF LAW - Room Number: 123
description: The Indiana University Maurer School of Law is pleased to participate once again in AlgoRhythms 2026, the third annual conference presented in collaboration with the Indiana University Jacobs School of Music exploring the intersection of music and artificial intelligence.  As a part of the larger program, Maurer will host a featured conversation titled A Conversation on Legal Trends in Music & AI. For this conversation, we are delighted to welcome Dan Amin '06, Assistant Chief Counsel at Lucasfilm. Mr. Amin will be joined by Robert Meitus, Partner at Meitus Strohm and Maurer Adjunct Professor.Dan Amin (Assistant Chief Counsel, Lucasfilm)  Dan oversees legal support for Lucasfilm's technology initiatives and leads legal and business affairs for Industrial Light & Magic (ILM), ILMxLAB, Skywalker Sound, and Technoprops. Since joining in 2014, he has guided ILM's global expansion, including the launch of studios in Australia and India and the development of StageCraft virtual production services. A founding leadership team member of ILMxLAB, he serves as primary counsel for its AR, VR, and immersive projects. Previously, Dan was in-house counsel at PBS, managing technology transactions and regulatory matters, and co-authoring the Supreme Court amicus brief in FCC v. Fox (2012). He holds degrees from Carnegie Mellon and Indiana University Maurer School of Law.  See a Full schedule of AlgoRhythm events here: https://blogs.iu.edu/jsominnovation/algorhythms-the-world-of-music-and-ai/ (https://blogs.iu.edu/jsominnovation/algorhythms-the-world-of-music-and-ai/)  Sign up to attend: https://forms.office.com/r/4QgGSdDfzp (https://nam12.safelinks.protection.outlook.com/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fforms.office.com%2Fr%2F4QgGSdDfzp&data=05%7C02%7Clawrooms%40iu.edu%7C8b6a058bd1c34953a71b08de756a77de%7C1113be34aed14d00ab4bcdd02510be91%7C1%7C0%7C639077297324940997%7CUnknown%7CTWFpbGZsb3d8eyJFbXB0eU1hcGkiOnRydWUsIlYiOiIwLjAuMDAwMCIsIlAiOiJXaW4zMiIsIkFOIjoiTWFpbCIsIldUIjoyfQ%3D%3D%7C0%7C%7C%7C&sdata=HnoaNgpgT3GEgAh93kCPUBxACwthBA0nAYIWALmjz7k%3D&reserved=0)

title: AlgoRhythms: AI, Music, and Human-Centered Creativity
location: Moot Courtroom, Baier Hall,211 S Indiana Ave, Bloomington, IN 47405, USA
description:
Indiana University Jacobs School of Music, partnered with the Maurer School of Law, IU Innovates, Music Tectonics, and the AI+Digital Futures Team, presents AlgoRhythms 2026, March 26–28 in Bloomington and online. This multi-day conference brings together artists, scholars, technologists, educators, and industry professionals to explore how artificial intelligence is transforming music creation, ownership, and the future of creative work.

Thursday, March 26
12:00 PM — Legal Trends in Music & AI
A discussion examining current legal developments shaping AI’s role in music.
Moot Courtroom, Maurer School of Law | Streaming online

1:30 PM — Legal Challenges and Opportunities in Music & AI
A panel exploring emerging legal questions around authorship, rights, and industry impact.
Moot Courtroom, Maurer School of Law | Streaming online

4:00 PM — Who Owns the Muse? Authenticity, Agency & AI in Music Creation
A conversation on creativity, authorship, and artistic identity in the age of AI.
Location TBA | Streaming online

Sign up here https://forms.office.com/pages/responsepage.aspx?id=NL4TEdGuAE2rS83QJRC-kdRT0NpCm_pFldrMPyyiXzVUMFRGSUg0V0lHV1BTTkJBSTVLOUFLUlRVMS4u&route=shorturl to attend either the in-person or virtual panel discussions.

title: Mag-Tagalog Tayo
location: Asian Culture Center (807 E 10th street, Bloomington, IN)
description: Come spend your Thursdays at the ACC practicing your Tagalog and celebrating Filipino culture! Every Thursday from 4:00-5:00 PM, we'll host a variety of language-immersive events—(included but not limited to) language table, speech practice, Tagalog tutoring, cooking workshops—to introduce the aspects that Filipino culture has to offer. Tagalog na Tayo! is open to everyone, whether you're a beginner, fluent speaker, or simply curious about Filipino culture.  All Indiana University events, groups, activities, and educational, cultural, and historical observances welcome all IU students and other members of the Indiana University community.For more information, view this event in BeInvolved (https://beinvolved.indiana.edu/event/11993690).

title: ACC Book Club features "To Save and Destroy" by Viet Nguyen
location: Asian Culture Center (807 E 10th street, Bloomington, IN)
description: What does it mean to be an "other"? How can writing shift the boundaries between insider and outsider for both author and audience? All are welcome to join the ACC in discussing these questions over dinner through Viet Thanh Nguyen's essay collection To Save and to Destroy: Writing as an Other. Originally delivered as the Norton Lecture series at Harvard in 2023-24, the six chapters span Nguyen's reflections on war, solidarity, and power, drawing from sources ranging from classics of American literature to Nguyen's own lived experience as a refugee. Digital copies of the eBook are freely available to access through IU Libraries, so all attendees are welcome to read ahead! Pizza will be served at each meeting. For more information, please reach out to ACC volunteer and book club moderator Cheryl Nong, PhD student in social psychology, at acc@iu.edu. The next book club meeting is on March 26, 2026.  All Indiana University events, groups, activities, and educational, cultural, and historical observances welcome all IU students and other members of the Indiana University community.For more information, view this event in BeInvolved (https://beinvolved.indiana.edu/event/11950464).
source: IU Asian Culture Center, IU beINvolved Student Orgs

title: Global Competencies Skills Development Series: From Indiana to Ambassador—Lessons from a Global Career
location: GLOBAL & INTERNATIONAL STUDIES BUILDING - Room Number: 1060
description: Lead with relevance to the audience. Make the Foreign Service connection explicit.  Here's a tighter, more targeted version:Join Ambassador Maria Brewer for an off-the-record dinner with Hamilton Lugar School students. Dinner will be provided.  A two-time U.S. Ambassador and Indiana native, Ambassador Brewer built her career in the U.S. Foreign Service, leading missions in Africa and South Asia and managing complex diplomatic negotiations.  If you are exploring a career in the Foreign Service or international policy, this is a rare opportunity for candid access. Hear how assignments are chosen, how reputations are built, and how leaders navigate pressure overseas.  Come with real questions. Leave with clarity about what this path demands and what it offers.
source: IU Arts & Humanities Institute, IU Hamilton Lugar School

title: Environmental Commission Outreach and Education Working Group Meeting
You (probably) Haven't Seen This Before
description: Dive deep into the archival collections of IU Libraries Moving Image Archive through a new monthly screening series of short films! Each month, rare and unique archival films will be presented focusing on underrepresented narratives and underrepresented motion-picture practices that push on and expand the spectrum of what is considered "cinema."  In an effort to keep audiences open-minded and with limited pre-conceptions of these rare works of celluloid, descriptions of films to be shown will not be shared prior to screenings. Join us based on announced themes and the expectation that the works you see will fall outside of Hollywood tropes, outside of mainstream commercial expectations, and outside of conventional filmmaking practices. Trust us, you (probably) haven't seen these films before!  This program is curated by Essence London and hosted by the IU Libraries Moving Image Archive Screening Room.  Please remember to register below.

title: You (probably) Haven't Seen This Before with IULMIA
description: For the past four years, the IU Libraries Moving Image Archive has hosted a regular screening series showcasing rare archival shorts that highlight underrepresented narratives and experimental motion-picture practices—works that expand the very idea of "cinema."On Thursday, March 26th at 5:30 p.m., the BFCA and IULMIA will present a collaborative edition of You (probably) Haven't Seen This Before, curated by Publications & Programming Associate Essence London. The event will be held in IULMIA's Screening Room and will last under an hour! Refreshments will be served following the screening.

description:
Join us for the Student Involvement and Leadership Center’s Bridgework Series, a monthly leadership and organizational development series designed to help students strengthen their team-building skills, understand group dynamics, and become agents of change within their organizations and communities. Each session will center around a key aspect of how groups work—from communication and collaboration to culture-building and creating sustainable impact—through interactive activities and real-world leadership scenarios.
This 8-event series (spanning the academic year) provides an engaging space for students to connect across campus, learn the foundations of effective teamwork, and explore how they can contribute to meaningful change.
Whether you're leading a student org, supporting a team, or just getting started, Bridgework offers practical tools and powerful insights to help you build better bridges—between people, ideas, and action.

📅 March 26th, 2026⏰ 6:00 PM – 8:00 PM📍 IMU Frangipani Room
💡 Strong teams don’t happen by chance—they’re built with intention. In "Culture of Connection," the penultimate event in the Bridgework Series, we’ll continue our exploration of the Group Dynamics domain of the Student Leadership Competencies, equipping you with the strategies 🔗 to foster trust, create inclusive environments, and strengthen the relational fabric of your organization.
🔥 A thriving team culture is the foundation of success. Let’s dive into how to cultivate connection and collaboration that lasts!

title: Paying For It
description: $8.00 - Tickets for the 7 PM screening of PAYING FOR IT 2025 • Canada • 85 minutes • dir. Sook-Yin Lee Thursday, March 26th at Backspace Gallery ( 112 W 6th St ) $ 8 | All Ages Showtime: 7pm TRAILER Synopsis : Paying For It is a live-action adaptation of acclaimed alternative-cartoonist Chester Brown’s best-selling graphic novel. In the late 90s, Chester and Sonny are a long-term, committed, romantic couple. When Sonny wants to redefine their relationship, Chester, an introverted cartoonist, starts sleeping with sex workers and discovers a new kind of intimacy in the process. In a kind of double-act of portraiture, Sook-Yin Lee, Chester Brown’s real-life ex, adapts his graphic novel in a compassionate and unique movie. The result is a singular work of cinematic autofiction that is alive, funny and moving. The handling of sex and intimacy is deft, narrative and necessary. Nothing is hidden, but at the same time, never gratuitous. The humor is grounded and smart. Paying For It is an intelligent and affecting work about love, sex and non-monogamy for adults. The story is relevant and appeals to audiences to reconsider hetero-normative lifestyles.

title: Take Shelter w/ filmmaker | Transfixed: Michael Shannon on Screen, Jorgensen Guest Filmmaker Series
location: IU Cinema,East 7th Street, Bloomington, IN, USA
description:
When Curtis LaForche (Michael Shannon) begins having nightmares of an encroaching, apocalyptic storm, he refrains from telling his wife, Samantha (Jessica Chastain). To protect her and their six-year-old Deaf daughter Hannah (Tova Stewart), Curtis starts focusing his anxiety and money into the obsessive building of a storm shelter. While Hannah's healthcare and special needs education has resulted in financial struggle, Curtis' seemingly inexplicable behavior concerns Samantha and provokes intolerance among co-workers, friends and neighbors. However, the resulting strain on his marriage and tension within the community doesn't compare to Curtis' private fear of what his disturbing dreams may truly signify. [121 min; drama, thriller; English]

A Q&A with actor Michael Shannon and Joshua Coonrod (Assistant Teaching Professor of English and Communication, Missouri University of Science and Technology) will follow the screening.

title: The Hidden World of Lichens with Gillian Harris
location: The Laura Hare Nature Preserve at Downey Hill
description: Discover the Hidden World of Lichens, led by naturalist and artist Gillian Harris! Lichens, which are a unique partnership between fungi and algae or cyanobacteria, are more than just “mossy stuff” on trees and rocks; they’re fascinating indicators of air quality, habitat diversity, and ecosystem health. As we hike, we’ll observe these often-overlooked organisms in their many forms and learn about their biology, where they live, and why they matter.

title: 21JPSI/IKS: "From Manners to Rules: Advocating for Legalism in South Korea and Japan" (Prof. Celeste Arrington, GWU)
location: GLOBAL & INTERNATIONAL STUDIES BUILDING - Room Number: Room 1112
description: This public event is open to all students, faculty, staff, and members of the community. Please join us! Professor Arrington's prepared remarks will be followed by a discussion/audience Q&A. Co-hosted with HLS' Institute for Korean Studies, and co-sponsored by the Department of Political Science and the Maurer School of Law's Stewart Center on the Global Legal Profession.About the talk:  Persons with disabilities have gained new rights and access in the past few decades in Japan and South Korea. Laws and regulations related to disabled persons have become more detailed and enforceable, and policymaking processes increasingly include persons with disabilities. Celeste Arrington unpacks how the demand for law is driving these advances in disability rights. By advocating for legislative changes and creatively using them, including through the courts, activists and lawyers are reshaping regulatory style and forging new opportunities for political engagement. The legalistic turn in disability-related policy marks a significant change in Japan and Korea, where officials relied on nonbinding guidelines, bureaucratic discretion, and vague laws. This talk draws on Arrington's new book, From Manners to Rules: Advocating for Legalism in South Korea and Japan (https://www.cambridge.org/core/books/from-manners-to-rules/F68A14FC5E643B2FE1DF3708EDAD4D79) (Cambridge, 2025).About the speaker:                      Professor Celeste Arrington (https://elliott.gwu.edu/celeste-arrington) specializes in comparative politics, with a regional focus on the Koreas and Japan. Her research interests include law and social change, governance, civil society, social movements, policy-making processes, lawyers, the media and politics, and qualitative methods. She is also interested in the international relations and security of Northeast Asia and transnational activism.                                        Professor Arrington is director of the GW Institute for Korean Studies (GWIKS) and co-director of the East Asia National Resources Center. She is also a Visiting Research Scholar at Princeton's School of Public and International Affairs and a faculty affiliate of the Global Japan Lab. Arrington received a Ph.D. from the University of California, Berkeley, an MPhil from the University of Cambridge, and an A.B. summa cum laude from Princeton University. She has been a fellow at Harvard, the Institute for Advanced Study in Princeton, the Law and Public Affairs Program at Princeton, and the Ludwig-Maximilians-University in Munich. GW's Office of the Vice President for Research awarded her the 2021 Early Career Research Scholar Award. Her article with Claudia Kim won the 2023 Asian Law and Society Association's distinguished article award.                  This event is made possible thanks to generous financial support from the Japan Foundation. All 21JPSI events are 100% in-person unless otherwise noted.
source: IU Hamilton Lugar School, IU Maurer School of Law

title: Kathy Pherson Speaker Event
description:
Katherine Hibbs Pherson, Chief Executive Officer of Globalytica and the VEXAS Global Foundation, teaches advanced analytic techniques and critical thinking skills to analysts in government, academia, and the private sector and consults to the government on planning, security, and analysis projects. She and the late Randy Pherson are co-authors of Critical Thinking for Strategic Intelligence, 3rd ed. (2021). Ms. Pherson currently serves as the Vice President of the International Association for Intelligence Education (IAFIE). As the past vice chair of the Intelligence and National Security Alliance’s (INSA) Security Policy Reform Council (2010-2024), she served as INSA’s representative to the National Industrial Security Program Policy Advisory Committee (NISPPAC). She has since 2018 supported the US Government’s Trusted Workforce 2.0 (TW 2.0) transformation initiative. Ms. Pherson in 2000 completed a 27-year career with CIA in intelligence and security analysis and resource management. Her leadership in security led to the adoption of a risk management methodology, strengthening and implementation of overseas security countermeasures, and improvements in dealing with unsolicited contacts.

Hosted by: Intelligence and Security Club

Additional Information can be found at: https://beinvolved.indiana.edu/event/11981959

title: EASC Colloquium: Shang Wei
description: Professor Wei Shang specializes in pre-modern Chinese literature and culture, especially the fiction and drama of the Ming and Qing dynasties. His research interests also include print culture, book history and intellectual history of the same period. His book “Rulin Waishi" and Cultural Transformation in Late Imperial China (2003) addresses the role of Confucian ritualism and fiction in shaping the intellectual and cultural changes of the eighteenth century. His other publications are concerned with Jin Ping Mei Cihua (The Plum in the Golden Vase), late Ming culture, fiction commentary, and medieval poetry, including Writing on Landmarks: From Yellow Crane Tower to Phoenix Pavilion (2020). He is the editor and co-editor of several volumes in both English and Chinese, and a contributor to The Cambridge History of Chinese Literature (2010).

title: Burke Lecture Series: Dr. Sampada Aranke
location: SIDNEY AND LOIS ESKENAZI MUSEUM OF ART - Room Number: Martin Commons
description: This talk will consider the ways that famed Black American conceptual artist David Hammons cites, leverages, and operationalizes white, western canons to probe the generative limits of art historical discourse and canonization. The artist's intentional engagement with the practices and legacies of artists whose oeuvres have formed an avant-garde western canon like Duchamp, Klein, Matta-Clark, is not about mere adoration, but in fact a strategic assessment, if not deformation, of art historical discourses. Operating like a trojan horse, Hammons has seamlessly created opportunities for alignment and association, making it so that named collectors of Duchamp and museums that collect Matta-Clark have incomplete collections if they do not own a work by Hammons. This project will work alongside two key Hammons works, The Holy Bible: Old Testament (2002) and Days End (2021) to consider how the artist breaks disciplinary canons in order to make radically renewed associations.     Lecture will be held until 5 pm followed by questions. Reception to follow.Sponsored by the Robert E. & Avis Tarrant Burke Lecture Fund.

title: Space Cybersecurity Digital Badge
description: Date: March 27 to June 5, 2026 (not meeting on Good Friday)Tuition: $3,995*Duration: 10 week courseDelivery: Online sessions Fridays from 4 to 5 p.m. ET  Cyberattacks, ranging from hacktivists to organized crime networks and even nation states, are targeting vulnerable networks and are frequently successful in stealing funds as well as valuable intellectual property.  At the same time, there is a booming demand across both the public and private sectors for practitioners with this training, with more than 700,000 openings nationwide for cybersecurity professionals. There is a particular need for those with an expertise in space cybersecurity given the reliance on space-based infrastructure for everything from weather forecasting and satellite telecommunications to broadband internet.Since Russia's cyberattacks on space-based services (https://www.cigionline.org/publications/the-first-space-cyber-war-and-the-need-for-new-regimes-and-policies/?_gl=1*19p6xc0*_ga*MTY4ODYwMTMzNC4xNzQzNDI2Mjk5*_ga_61CH0D2DQW*MTc0MzY5MTM3Ni4yMi4xLjE3NDM2OTIxNDQuNjAuMC4w*_gcl_aw*R0NMLjE3NDMwMTI0MzMuQ2owS0NRandxSW1fQmhEbkFSSXNBS0JZY211dXhkT0M4ZWwza0tFMzUzSEpOTm5sOEJ6TThuUFRTRjRISEtYV0ZIRmExZThuTVBoQVN5d2FBbm5IRUFMd193Y0I.*_gcl_au*NjEyMjMwMTQ2LjE3NDM2OTEzNzc.) provided by commercial US space companies during its war on Ukraine, the cybersecurity of space systems has become high on the agenda, spurring discussions at the White House and in Congress, and a $700 million budget request by the Space Force (https://www.darkreading.com/cloud-security/us-space-force-wants-700m-cybersecurity) for this purpose alone. At the same time, a fast-growing market has emerged (https://www.inc.com/heather-wilde/harness-power-of-space-cybersecurity-for-growth-opportunity.html), with demand for professionals and firms.  Participants in the Space Cybersecurity Digital Badge program will gain in-depth understanding of the various cyber threats to space systems as well as techniques, tools, and best practices to address those threats. At the end of the program, participants will have the capability to develop and implement effective strategies for managing cyber risks to space systems.  After successful completion of an assurance of learning (assessment) at the conclusion of the program, participants will earn the Kelley School of Business Space Cybersecurity Digital Badge. Participants may also earn 3 credit hours toward the 12 credit hour Kelley School of Business Cybersecurity Management Graduate Certificate. The Cybersecurity Management Graduate Certificate (https://kelley.iu.edu/programs/executive-education-degrees-certificates/online-certificates/cybersecurity-management/index.html) may, in turn, be applied to the IU MS in Cybersecurity Risk Management (https://cyberrisk.iu.edu/?_gl=1*1833cq*_gcl_au*MTU3NDIzMTYyNy4xNzQ3MjMzNjYx*_ga*MTc4NDY5NDU2My4xNzMzNjM2NDc4*_ga_61CH0D2DQW*czE3NDcyMzA4OTkkbzIwJGcxJHQxNzQ3MjM2Mjc2JGo2MCRsMCRoMCRkMFdsRmYxSkwzaUs1bnoyYmNvdmVyY09qMlFscDFNV1R1QQ..).  CISA designation: Your participation in this program and completion of all course requirements earns you 12.0 CPE credits that can go towards the annual credits you need to maintain your CISA designation.  * Space ISAC members receive exclusive discounts of up to 50% based on membership tier. To learn about and obtain the discount, please reach out to Jade Maloney at jade@spaceisac.org (mailto:jade@spaceisac.org).  * GI/VA: We proudly welcome active-duty service members and veterans to the space cybersecurity program. Recognizing that GI Bill and VA education benefits do not apply to this program, IU is pleased to offer a Service Members & Veterans Scholarship amounting to 30% of the tuition. To receive the scholarship, please enter the code GI/VA30 during registration. Proof of military or veteran status should be provided by the start date of the program.

title: Jorgensen Guest Filmmaker Presents: Michael Shannon, Brett Neveu, and "Eric LaRue" | Transfixed: Michael Shannon on Screen, Jorgensen Guest Filmmaker Series
location: IU Cinema,East 7th Street, Bloomington, IN, USA
description:
In Michael Shannon’s directorial debut—adapted from Brett Neveu’s 2002 renowned stage play—a mother struggles to cope in the aftermath of a shocking crime at the hands of her son. Janice (Judy Greer) is struggling, moving through life as if in a haze and unable to let go of her anger and frustration following her son's violent and shocking crimes. While her husband (Alexander Skarsgård) has found refuge at a new church, Janice finds it hard to seek solace in her faith despite her pastor’s pleas to heal her wounds by meeting with the mothers of her son’s victims. As Janice ponders her role in the community, she questions whether there is any established path to healing from an experience such as hers. Eric LaRue asks audiences to witness the frayed emotional ripples violent acts can engender. [119 min; drama; English]

Join award-winning writer Brett Neveu and award-winning actor/director Michael Shannon for an intimate and wide-ranging conversation on the art of collaboration and all points between. Please note this program will encompass a brief onstage conversation, followed by the film and then a post-film Q&A.

title: The DaVinci Code- Live on Stage
location: Waldron Auditorium,112 South Walnut Street, Bloomington, IN 47404, USA
description:
Constellation Stage & Screen presents “The DaVinci Code- Live on Stage,” on stage March 26 - April 12 at the Waldron Auditorium.

Dan Brown’s international best-seller comes to life on stage in this thrilling, high-stakes adventure! When the curator of the Louvre is found murdered under mysterious circumstances, symbologist Robert Langdon and cryptologist Sophie Neveu are thrust into an investigation that quickly unravels into a deadly conspiracy. As they follow a trail of cryptic puzzles and ancient codes, they discover a secret that could change the course of history. Packed with pulse-pounding suspense and stunning visuals, The Da Vinci Code is an exhilarating journey into the unknown. Recommended for ages 13+.

See Constellation’s website for more information, including a detailed performance schedule. Learn more and find tickets at seeconstellation.org/mainstage/da-vinci-code/ https://seeconstellation.org/mainstage/da-vinci-code/ https://seeconstellation.org/mainstage/da-vinci-code/

location: Blockhouse Bar, 205 S. College Avenue, Bloomington, Indiana, 47404, United States
description:
Friday March 27th at 8:00 pm
*doors open at 7:00 pm
Where: The BlockHouse Bar 21+, 205 S College Ave
Sliding fee $10 – $25, Pay at the door

About Making Movies
Making Movies (3/25/26 – 3/27/26) A dynamic band that blends Afro-Latino rhythms and rock ’n’ roll with African-derived percussion and styles like rumba, merengue, mambo, and cumbia. Founded by Enrique Chi—a Panamanian songwriter, musician, filmmaker, and cultural producer—the Latin Grammy-nominated band serves as the primary platform for Chi’s creative mission. Armed with electric guitars and a mejoranera, a folkloric guitar native to Panamá, the band interweaves styles as divergent as cumbia and blues, salsa, soul, and rock’n’roll––all boiled down into their own original sound.

location: IU Cinema,East 7th Street, Bloomington, IN, USA
description: Michael Shannon and Ashley Judd give searing performances in this skin-crawling exercise in fear and paranoia from director William Friedkin. Oklahoma waitress Aggie (Judd) lives a largely solitary life in a rundown motel. She's haunted by a tragic past and hounded by her ex-con ex-husband (Harry Connick, Jr.). When she sparks a romance with an eccentric drifter, Peter Evans (Shannon), she starts to feel hopeful again—until Peter reveals he is a victim of government experimentation. The couple begins to obsess over the nightmarish idea that they are infected by insects. Shannon reprises the role he originated in playwright Tracy Letts' original London production of the same name. [102 min; drama, thriller, horror; English]
