description: SATURDAY, MARCH 28All events in Sweeney Hall, IU Jacobs School of Music| Zoom and YouTube Live  Note: Additional events are on Thursday and Friday. See Website for details. CONFERENCE WEBSITE (https://blogs.iu.edu/jsominnovation/algorhythms-the-world-of-music-and-ai/) | Sign Up To Attend > (https://forms.office.com/r/4QgGSdDfzp) | See Who the Participants Are > (https://blogs.iu.edu/jsominnovation/2026-summit-participants/) | Read our Press Release > (https://blogs.iu.edu/jsominnovation/2026/02/03/algorhythms-2026-a-summit-on-ai-music-and-human-centered-creativity/)9:00 a.m.Business Transformation in the Music AI WorldModerated by Tristra Newyear Yeager (Music Tectonics)    - Matt Henninger (Moises)    - Marcus Lawrence (Zinstrel)    - Steve Stewart (SongHub)    - 4th Panelist will be announced soon10:30 a.m. Emerging Tools and Creative Pathways in Music & AIModerated by Michael Sammis (Professor of Practice in Music Business, IU Jacobs School of Music)    - Tracy Maddux (beatBread)    - Ethan Castro (EDGE Sound)    - Joe Berman (SongHub)    - Olivia Jones (MIDiA Research)12:15 p.m.Keynote AddressDmitri Vietze (Founder, RPS and Music Tectonics)2:00 p.m.Creativity in AI: Artistic Migration & New TerritoriesModerated by Ed Dallis-Comentale (Asst, VP for Research in Arts & Humanities, Indiana University)    - Caleb Weintraub (Eskenazi School, IU)    - Ivica Bukvic (KIX Lab, IU)    - Raina Murnak (U Miami)    - Jason Palamara (IU Indianapolis)4:00 p.m.Creativity in AI: Understanding The Known & UnknownModerator: Marcus Lawrence (Zinstrel)    - Tom Collins (U Miami)    - Tristra Newyear Yeager (Music Tectonics)    - Timirah James (Artist, AI Music researcher)    -  Andy West (IU Jacobs School of Music)    The summit provides an opportunity to wrestle with questions about authorship, ownership, environmental impact, cultural disruption, business transformation, and the meaning of creativity itself.  Primary partners for this project are the Maurer School of Law, IU Innovates, Music Tectonics, and the AI+Digital Futures Team.  AlgoRhythms is generously supported by Frank Graves, whose contributions to the Jacobs School of Music and Indiana University are deeply appreciated. Additional funding is made possible through the generous support of the Office of the Vice President for Research in Arts & Humanities, IU Innovates, the Bloomington Office of the Chancellor, the Maurer School of Law, and Sweetwater Sound.
source: IU Jacobs School of Music, IU Kelley School of Business

title: Declaration of Independence Curator Talks
description: The Declaration of Independence: The Motives, The Moment is an exhibition exploring the journey taken by the nation's founders. How colonial leaders go from loyal subjects of the British empire to risking everything by becoming revolutionaries? Take an insightful tour of the exhibition from the curator, with up close looks at Revolutionary War era correspondence, books and manuscripts that influenced the revolutionaries, and media that spread the word about the declaration.  Light refreshments will be offered. Come learn something new about the nation's founding and have a snack in Indiana University's rare book library!  Tours are offered at noon, 1 p.m., and 2 p.m. RSVP requested for planning of refreshments and staffing.

title: Dramatic Entanglements: Ecologies and Encounters on the Premodern Stage
description: A two-day symposium exploring eco-social entanglements on the premodern stage. Featuring short talks and performances by invited scholars, faculty, and students, the event examines how early drama stages environmental, social, and political encounters—and what those overlapping ecologies can tell us now.  Sponsored by the College Arts and Humanities Institute and the Environmental Futures Initiative.
source: IU Arts & Humanities Institute, IU Theatre & Dance

title: Pre-PA Club Phlebotomy Class
description:
The Pre-PA club is conducting a phlebotomy course, led by Nicholas Perrotta, where he will teach pre-PA and other IU students valuable venipuncture skills. Upon completion of the course, the students will receive a phlebotomy certification lasting up to two years.
The training length is 2 days, Saturday, March 28th and Sunday, March 29th. On Sunday, clinic usually wraps up around 2:30pm, with an optional discussion afterward about strategies, fielding questions about job/volunteer opportunities as a phlebotomist, etc. This is a challenging and rewarding clinic that will be of great value students as they continue their health career.
"Nick is a very experienced instructor (20+ years) who has not only been teaching university students but also several hospitals’ research staff and county public health departments and he has a diverse research background."
Note: Certification is valid anywhere in the United States for two years from certification date with the exception of: California, Washington State, Nevada, Louisiana (there are a different in-state training/licensing, so if you need it there, contact appropriate agency and find out about requirements and exemptions); students have also drawn blood in other countries. There are a number of places around the country including the Midwest, and your Bloomington campus where students have gained employment after clinic.

Hosted by: Pre-Physician Assistant Club at IU

Additional Information can be found at: https://beinvolved.indiana.edu/event/11912208

title: AlgoRhythms: AI, Music, and Human-Centered Creativity
location: Sweeney Hall, IU Jacobs School of Music,1201 E 3rd St, Bloomington, IN 47405, USA
description:
Indiana University Jacobs School of Music, partnered with the Maurer School of Law, IU Innovates, Music Tectonics, and the AI+Digital Futures Team, presents AlgoRhythms 2026, March 26–28 in Bloomington and online. This multi-day conference brings together artists, scholars, technologists, educators, and industry professionals to explore how artificial intelligence is transforming music creation, ownership, and the future of creative work.

Saturday, March 28
All events held in Sweeney Hall at the Indiana University Jacobs School of Music | Also streaming online

9:00 AM — Business Transformation in the Music AI World
A panel exploring how AI is reshaping the music industry, from creation to distribution and business strategy.

10:30 AM — Emerging Tools and Creative Pathways in Music & AI
A conversation highlighting new technologies and evolving creative workflows for musicians and industry professionals.

12:15 PM — Keynote Address
A featured keynote examining innovation and future directions at the intersection of music and technology.

2:00 PM — Creativity in AI: Artistic Migration & New Territories
A discussion on how AI is expanding artistic practice and opening new creative possibilities.

4:00 PM — Creativity in AI: Understanding the Known & Unknown
A closing panel reflecting on current developments in AI music and the questions still unfolding for artists and audiences.

Sign up here https://forms.office.com/pages/responsepage.aspx?id=NL4TEdGuAE2rS83QJRC-kdRT0NpCm_pFldrMPyyiXzVUMFRGSUg0V0lHV1BTTkJBSTVLOUFLUlRVMS4u&route=shorturl to attend either the in-person or virtual panel discussions.

title: SILC Leadership Compass Series - "Group Dynamics"
description:
Join us for the Student Involvement and Leadership Center's Leadership Compass Series, a monthly leadership development series designed to help students grow across the 8 Student Leadership Competency domains. Each session will focus on one core leadership area—ranging from self-awareness to civic responsibility—through interactive workshops and hands-on activities. This 8-workshop series (spanning the academic year) provides a dynamic space for students to build confidence, sharpen their skills, and connect with peers who are passionate about personal and community growth.
Whether you're new to leadership or already involved on campus, the SILC Leadership Compass offers practical tools and meaningful conversations to elevate your leadership journey—one domain at a time.

***Lunch is provided for participants from 12:00pm-1:00pm on all dates***

📅 March 28th, 2026⏰ 10:00 AM – 3:00 PM📍 IMU Club Hub
💡 Teams thrive when dynamics are intentional—but how do leaders foster collaboration and navigate challenges effectively? In "Group Dynamics," the next event in the Leadership Compass Series, we’ll explore the Group Dynamics domain of the Student Leadership Competencies, equipping you with the tools 🔄 to build trust, enhance teamwork, and lead groups through both strengths and struggles with confidence.
🔥 Great teams don’t just work together—they grow together. Let’s refine how you lead and support your team!

location: Fiber Arts Center, College Mall, Bloomington, 3038 East Third Street, Bloomington, IN, 47401, United States
description:
Saturday, March 28th 12:30 – 4:30 PM
Curious about learning brioche knitting? Come join us for this one-day introductory class! Participants will learn two-color brioche knitting in both flat and in-the-round, including a few common increase and decrease stitches found in brioche patterns. Students are expected to know intermediate/advanced knitting techniques such as knitting in the round, slipped stitches, and how to use a cable needle.

$55 – BSWG members
$65 – non-BSWG student members (includes one-year guild student membership)
$85 – non-BSWG members (includes one-year guild membership)
Class size 2-6 students

Students should bring the following: Two ~10-gram balls of DK or worsted weight yarn in 2 contrasting colors (recommended to use a light and a dark or a variegated and a solid color), size US4 or US5 circular needles or DPNs, and a cable needle.
To register, click here.

title: Intermediate/Advanced Drawing Workshop
location: SIDNEY AND LOIS ESKENAZI MUSEUM OF ART - Room Number: Check in with Guest Services on the first floor
description: Reserve your spot. If this page says, "Registration has closed" that means the waitlist is full.SuppliesBring your favorite drawing pencil (e.g. 4B), a sketchbook or drawing paper, and a phone or device to photograph artwork (without flash) as we sketch.  Know Before You GoArrive early to find parking, check in, and get your drawing supplies ready.  At 1:55 p.m. any unclaimed reservations will be released to guests who are standing by waiting for a spot.  Where to find us, where to park, accessibility, and museum guidelines (https://artmuseum.indiana.edu/visit/your-visit.html).  This workshop is recommended for ages 12 and up. Anyone under 18, who is not an IU student, must be accompanied by an adult.  When You ArriveCheck in with Guest Services at the information desk on the first floor, even if you come often and even if you are late. We appreciate having an accurate attendance count.  Store all backpacks and large bags before entering the galleries. A small handbag may be carried at your side.  Get your supplies ready. Attach an 18x24" piece of newsprint to a drawing board.  Cancellations  This workshop often has a waitlist. Please use the cancellation link included with your confirmation email if your plans change.About the Instructor  John Cheek has taught credit and non-credit classes for eight years in portrait painting, landscape painting, palette knife painting, life drawing, beginning drawing, and advanced drawing, as well as in design. He volunteers his time and expertise to facilitate drawing workshops at the Eskenazi Museum of Art.

description: Robert Redford captures the majesty of the Montana wilderness and the strength of the American family in this acclaimed adaptation of Norman Maclean's classic memoir. Growing up, both boys rebel against their stern minister father. While Norman channels his rebellion into writing, Paul descends a slippery path to self-destruction. Co-starring Tom Skerritt as the Reverend Maclean and Emily Lloyd as wild-hearted Jessie Burns. [123 min; drama; English]

description:
This Brazil, Indiana native is self-taught on guitar, piano, and drums influenced by the best in country, R&B, rock & roll, and blues. Expressing a raw and impulsive style of rock & roll, Henry always adds his own voice and style. His sales over the years have topped well over the million mark. This has given him the opportunity to be a support act for artists such as Stevie Ray Vaughan, Eddie Money, Chicago, and Richard Marx. Although most of his touring is in Indiana, he has appeared on the Arsenio Hall Show and Late Show With David Letterman. He also married and raised four children during this period, and successfully supported himself and his family with music since he was 17.

title: SIRAT
description: $0.00 - Tickets for the 8 PM screening of SIRAT 2025 • Morocco, France • 115 minutes • dir. Oliver Laxe Saturday, March 28th at Upland Wood Shop (354 W 11th St) FREE, but ticketed | All Ages Showtime: 8pm *Sponsored by IU Arts & Humanities Council as part of Granfalloon 2026. More info at grafalloon.indiana.edu . TRAILER Synopsis : A father (Sergi López) and his son arrive at a rave deep in the mountains of southern Morocco. They are searching for Mar — daughter and sister — who vanished months ago at one of these endless, sleepless parties. Surrounded by electronic music and a raw, unfamiliar sense of freedom, they hand out her photo again and again. Hope is fading, but they push through and follow a group of ravers heading to one last party in the desert. As they venture deeper into the burning wilderness, the journey forces them to confront their own limits.

title: Poetry in Place
location: Leonard Springs Nature Park, 4685 S Leonard Springs Rd, Bloomington, IN 47403, USA
description:
Registration fee $7/in-city, $9/non-city. Register online by March 27 at https://webtrac.bloomington.in.gov/webtrac/web/search.html?Primarycode=140015. For ages 18 yrs. and up.
Nobody can discover the world for somebody else, writes Kentucky author Wendell Berry. In this program we will tap into the inspiration the forest offers as we examine the relationship between poetry and place. As we hike through lovely Leonard Springs, participants will have the opportunity to hear excerpts from multiple nature writers, before stopping to write their own. One will have the opportunity to share their writing, or keep it private. Participants will be given a nature notebook and pencil.

title: Saving the Hoosier: The Concert
location: Pillar Arts Alliance Center,2894 E 3rd St, Bloomington, IN 47401, USA
description:
This is your chance to experience Saving the Hoosier Concert live, connecting with people who care about our Hoosier National Forest while you enjoy music up close. The live concert will feature Hoosier musicians Sarah Flint https://www.sarahflintmusic.com/ and Jeff Shew https://jeffshewcom.wordpress.com/ , and is a fundraiser to help complete the new documentary film “Saving the Hoosier: The Fight for America’s Forests https://www.womenwithguts.com/ ”. Audience members will get to see a sneak peek of scenes from the film and meet narrator Hud Mellencamp https://www.imdb.com/name/nm10348731/ . While there, enjoy some food and the opportunity to bid on some auction items. The film is being produced by Bloomington-based Women with Guts Productions https://www.womenwithguts.com/ .

With Artists for Climate Awareness https://artistsforclimateawareness.org/ as fiscal sponsor for the film, all donations are tax-deductible. Tickets for the concert are $30, and include beverages and pizza, generously donated by Pizza X https://www.pizzaxbloomington.com/ . Audience members will be invited to participate in a sing-along of the Indiana state song, "On the Banks of the Wabash"' that will be used in the film.

Besides Flint and Shew, the soundtrack for Saving the Hoosier will include music from Krista Detor https://www.kristadetor.com/ , Carrie Newcomer https://www.carrienewcomer.com/ , Charlie Jesseph https://open.spotify.com/artist/304dseTgiYoYhLzqcqrN4c and Busman’s Holiday https://busmansholiday.bandcamp.com/album/table-talk .

Saving the Hoosier follows environmental activists who, through grassroots education and lawsuits, have successfully protected the Hoosier National Forest from the US Forest Service’s plans for commercial logging and prescribed burns to benefit timber sales.

Tickets sold in advance.

See more at https://artistsforclimateawareness.org/event/saving-the-hoosier-concert/ https://artistsforclimateawareness.org/event/saving-the-hoosier-concert/

Buy tickets at https://savingthehoosier.eventbrite.com https://savingthehoosier.eventbrite.com/

title: It's On Us: Bystander Intervention Workshop
description:
The It’s On Us: Bystander Intervention Workshop is a 90-minute, audience-driven bystander intervention program that teaches students the skills needed to help in situations involving alcohol and drug use that could lead to a sexual assault. Students practice helping in real-life scenarios and discuss how alcohol and drug use influences a person’s ability to give and get consent to engage in sexual activity with another person.

It's On Us is required for all first-year and transfer undergraduate students. You must attend one session to fulfill the requirement.

Instructions:

Those who arrive more than 5 minutes after the start time will NOT be allowed into the session and will need to attend a different session

If this is your assigned workshop but you cannot attend it in its entirety, please RSVP for a different workshop.

location: LOWELL E. BAIER HALL/IU MAURER SCHOOL OF LAW - Room Number: 122
description: The "Litigating Faculty First Amendment Rights" program will explore current litigation surrounding the question of faculty First Amendment rights related to teaching and academic speech. Kilborn, plaintiff/appellant in a recent case at the Seventh Circuit, will describe how he came to challenge threats of discipline from his university in a case that broke important new ground in this federal judicial circuit for the First Amendment rights of public university faculty. Bleisch, an expert on the First Amendment and universities, will provide broader doctrinal context and discuss similar recent cases around the country. Sanders will direct questions to the two speakers and will moderate.Jason Kilborn, Professor of Law, University of Illinois Chicago School of Law  Josh Bleisch, Staff Attorney, ACLU of Indiana  Steve Sanders, IU Maurer School of Law

title: Spring 2026 Colloquium Series with Amadou Beidy Sow
description: The Center for Latin American and Caribbean Studies is proud to present the continuation of its Colloquium Series for Spring 2026.  We welcome Dr. Beidy Sow as he presents Political Communication in Latin America: A Case Study of Colombia  Abstract: Political communication plays a crucial role in democratic processes worldwide, and no country can afford to ignore it if it wants to ensure the proper functioning of its system. In Colombia, political communication reflects the complexity of the country's historical, social, and institutional dynamics, marked by a prolonged armed conflict, deep political polarization, and persistent inequalities. This article examines the evolution of political communication in Colombia, focusing on the interaction between political leaders and citizens. It highlights the use of various channels by political leaders to convey their messages. The study also draws on citizen testimonies, populist rhetoric, and the different methods used by politicians to communicate with the population and influence their choices, as well as the laws implemented to facilitate communication between leaders and their constituencies. Furthermore, it contributes to understanding issues related to peace processes, social movements, and youth participation in contemporary political communication practices. Overall, the study of political communication in Colombia will provide a better understanding of the profile of the country's political leaders and allow for an analysis of the strengths and weaknesses of Colombian democracy.   Amadou Beidy Sow is a Bamana instructor from Ségou, Mali. Dr. Sow obtained a Master's degree in Second Language Teaching from the Ecole Normale Supérieure in Bamako. After coming to the United States, he earned a Master's degree in International Studies at Ohio University followed by a Ph.D. in Education (Cultural Studies in Education) with a focus on political leadership. He has published Political Leadership in the Hand of Teachers: The Type of Leadership Teacher Politicians Display on the Political Scene in Mali, VDM Verlag (2010). Dr. Sow has presented at numerous conferences. He has experience teaching Bamanankan, French, English, and Social Studies.                             The CLACS Spring 2026 Colloquium meets Mondays from February through April, with faculty and guest speakers engaging Latin America and the Caribbean through research on history, culture, language, politics, and the arts. Check the CLACS Calendar (https://events.iu.edu/clacs/) for details.
source: IU Hamilton Lugar School, IU La Casa Latino Cultural Center

title: Taming the Beast? Creative Destruction, Free Speech and Social Media in Central Europe
description: Intricate legal challenges are posed globally by the platform economy, particularly concerning regulation, human rights, and the essential functions of the state in the 21st century. A surge of regulatory efforts at both national and international levels is apparent in response to the rapid digitalization and the proliferation of disruptive technologies. This phenomenon is exemplified by the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR), the Digital Markets Act (DMA), and the Digital Services Act (DSA) within the European Union – commonly referred to stateside as by-products of the "Brussels Effect", and counteracted – most recently – by U.S. state-led initiatives, earning this movement the name "Texas Effect" in key areas that affect the First Amendment.  Driven by this development, it makes sense to investigate the evolving relationship between technology (herein dubbed 'the Beast') and state sovereignty. As digital platforms transcend traditional boundaries, they challenge the state's ability to regulate effectively, necessitating a reevaluation of what constitutes essential state functions. This reevaluation is crucial to ensure that states can uphold their foundational roles, including the protection of human rights, maintenance of public order, even national security, and regulation of economic activities. Furthermore, the concept of 'essential state functions' in the context of the platform economy needs to be addressed. Certain functions, such as the administration of justice, national defense and security, and public health, remain the exclusive domain of the state (in the context of European integration), even amidst widespread digitalization on a European level. However, the rise of powerful digital platforms challenges the states' capacity to perform these functions effectively, prompting a need for regulatory innovations that reaffirm state authority and responsibility.  In a European context, most of Central Europe, with a post-Soviet past, is in a special situation when it comes to assessing issues of sovereignty and free expression in light of globalization and European integration. Due to the apparent tension between the inherent drivers of the digital platform ecosystem—such as innovation (as the source of 'Creative Destruction') and market expansion—and the regulatory solutions established by some states will be examined, underscoring the need for a balanced approach that leaves room for innovation while safeguarding the common good, various public interests, and human rights, at times with special focus on protected groups such as children – "in order to secure the blessings of liberty to our posterity" – a goal also echoed by the preamble of the U.S. Constitution.  Márton Sulyok is a Senior Lecturer at the Institute of Public Law at the University of Szeged (Hungary) and the Head of the Public Law Center at the MCC Law School in Budapest (Hungary). He currently sits on the Board of the European Public Law Organization and is a member of the European Group of Public Law. His research interests include constitutional law and theory, with special focus on constitutional interpretation; European human rights law with special focus on privacy and due process protections; law and technology; and the concept of constitutional identity in EU integration and the role of the EU Charter of Fundamental Rights in national constitutional jurisdictions in Europe.

title: It's On Us: Bystander Intervention Workshop
description:
The It’s On Us: Bystander Intervention Workshop is a 90-minute, audience-driven bystander intervention program that teaches students the skills needed to help in situations involving alcohol and drug use that could lead to a sexual assault. Students practice helping in real-life scenarios and discuss how alcohol and drug use influences a person’s ability to give and get consent to engage in sexual activity with another person.

It's On Us is required for all first-year and transfer undergraduate students. You must attend one session to fulfill the requirement.

Instructions:

Those who arrive more than 5 minutes after the start time will NOT be allowed into the session and will need to attend a different session

If this is your assigned workshop but you cannot attend it in its entirety, please RSVP for a different workshop.

title: Apples to Oranges? Comparing Constitutions
location: LOWELL E. BAIER HALL/IU MAURER SCHOOL OF LAW - Room Number: 122
description: Marton Sulyok, visiting researcher at the Georgetown Law Center for the Constitution, joined by Professor Timothy Waters, will compare constitutions across the globe in their treatment of the rights guaranteed by the First Amendment to the United States Constitution. What can the United States learn from other constitutions? How can our First Amendment influence other nations' respect for the rights of their citizens? Join us for a discussion of our Constitution as the 250th anniversary of our Nation approaches, and what can be learned by examining different approaches to expressive rights.  Dave's Hot Chicken will be provided for all attendees!

title: Electronic Evidence in Criminal Investigations
location: LOWELL E. BAIER HALL/IU MAURER SCHOOL OF LAW - Room Number: 125

Myers will discuss the legal nuances of electronic evidence, and Odier and Hunter will discuss and demonstrate their role in the detection and seizure of electronic evidence in criminal investigations.

title: Why Obfuscation is (still) Needed (more than ever)
location: STEPHEN L. & CONNIE J. FERGUSON INTERNATIONAL CENTER - Room Number: 421/423
description: This salon series at Indiana University features leading thinkers exploring how to build better digital and natural worlds.Beyond the Web / After Analog Speaker Series.  This talk examines data obfuscation, defined as the "production, inclusion, addition, or communication of misleading, ambiguous, of false data in an effort to evade, distract, or confuse". The concept emerged from early interventions such as TrackMeNot (2006) and AdNauseam (2014), two browser extensions designed to disrupt behavioral profiling—projects that were both widely praised and sharply criticized.  The talk revisits this work to explain why obfuscation was chosen as a strategy and why these particular designs were pursued over potentially more effective alternatives. Turning to the contemporary landscape of generative AI and large language models, where privacy threats have intensified, it asks whether new technologies create opportunities to extend the ethical and practical successes of earlier efforts while addressing their limitations.About the Speaker  A Professor of Information Science and the founding director of the Digital Life Initiative at Cornell Tech. Her research spans issues of bias, trust, security, autonomy, and accountability in digital systems, most notably, privacy as contextual integrity. Professor Nissenbaum's publications include the books Obfuscation: A User's Guide for Privacy and Protest (https://mitpress.mit.edu/books/obfuscation), with Finn Brunton (MIT Press, 2015), Values at Play in Digital Games (https://www.valuesatplay.org/vap-the-book), with Mary Flanagan (MIT Press, 2014), and Privacy in Context: Technology, Policy, and the Integrity of Social Life (https://www.sup.org/books/title/?id=8862) (Stanford, 2010). These, along with numerous research articles, have been translated into seven languages, including Polish, Chinese, and Portuguese. She received the 2014 Barwise Prize from the American Philosophical Association and the IACAP Covey Award for computing, ethics, and philosophy. Professor Nissenbaum has also contributed to privacy-enhancing free software, such as TrackMeNot (http://trackmenot.io/) (designed to prevent the profiling of web search histories) and AdNauseam (https://adnauseam.io/) (designed to counter profiling based on ad clicks). She holds a Ph.D. in philosophy from Stanford University and a B.A. (Hons) in Philosophy and Mathematics from the University of the Witwatersrand (http://www.wits.ac.za/), South Africa.

title: A Night in Argentina with Eleanor & Dario and Continuo
location: Blueberry Hill Estate, 1493 IN-56, Paoli, IN, United States
description:
Tuesday, March 31
Dinner at 5:30 / Concert at 6:30
Blueberry Hill Estate
1493 IN-56 Paoli, IN
$25 concert only / $50 Dinner & Concert
Order Tickets Here
Presented in partnership by Lotus Blossoms, Let Music Speak, and Team Peace Argentinian dinner prepared by Lost River Coop & Cafe.
About Eleanor & Dario
Eleanor Dubinsky (cello, vocals) and Darío Acosta Teich (guitar) take audiences on a trip around the world with multilingual original songs and fresh interpretations of jazz and beloved classics in Spanish, French, Portuguese. Their elegant performances transcend genre and geography, celebrating the beautiful diversity of our world. They serve as cultural ambassadors via the US Department of State Bureau of Educational and Cultural Affairs Arts Envoy program and African Regional Services.

About Continuo
Continuo is an expandable cast of classical+ musicians collaborating and creating to bring captivating performances to a variety of venues. From duo to chamber orchestra, Continuo performs a wide swath of genres, including original compositions and the storied classical cannon, contemporary works, and arrangements of traditional, pop and folk songs. Founded by Diana Flores and Jordana Greenberg in 2023, Continuo’s mission is to create, collaborate, and connect, through the indistinguishable art of music.

title: Best of Un/filmed: Short Documentaries from Russian Speaking Filmmakers | Additional Films and Guests
location: IU Cinema,1213 East 7th Street, Bloomington, IN 47405, USA
description:
Un/Filmed is an international documentary film development project and a regular partner with the Byrnes Institute at IU focused on preserving the tradition of independent documentary filmmaking among Russian-speakers, and to provide competitive workshops for participants with the opportunity to create films reflecting on pressing social issues. This program is showcasing the top six films of the 2024-2025 cohort with subtitles presented in English.

Like Mother, Like Daughter (dir. Sofia Sedlova, 2025, Russia and France): Yana—who’s unexpectedly pregnant—and her daughter Liza await their papers in a Paris suburb. Uncertainty sparks personal crises for Yana and forces Liza to grow up too fast. Their roles reverse in this small family, but together they’ll face the challenges ahead. [7 min; Russian and French with English subtitles]

We're Fine (dir. Daria Yurishcheva, 2025, Russia and France): Two old acquaintances share an apartment in Marseille by force of circumstances. The talkative Ukrainian Andrii is forced to take care of the gloomy Russian Filipp. The migrant's instability will not make Andrii lose his optimism. [16 min; Russian and French with English subtitles]

She Became a Pillar of Salt (dir. Xenia Vasilkova, 2025, Russia): A Russian filmmaker and her Ukrainian friend rebuild their lives in Armenia after fleeing their homelands. As war draws all apart, we uncover quiet echoes between them. [15 min; Russian with English subtitles]

Uneasy Living (dir. Nikita Nadezhdin, 2025, Russia): A Russian, staunchly against war, flees to Georgia after protest detentions, battling depression and scorn from traditional society while scavenging in dumpsters and aiding stray cats. Through quiet acts of kindness, he searches for life's meaning. This intimate documentary explores hope, humanity, and defiance against outdated norms. [15 min; Russian with English subtitles]

Kozlov Mitya, 81+ (dir. Yulia Ruzmanova, 2025, Russia): What matters most in relationships in your 80s: a hug, a sack of potatoes, a kind word, or simply having someone to spend New Year’s Eve with? This is a road trip with Mitya Kozlov and his dog as he visits his girlfriends, searching for an answer to this question. [17 min; Russian with English subtitles]

Memory is Me? (dir. Vlada Lodesk, 2025, Russia): To get acquainted with her deceased dad, whom she never met, a filmmaker follows the traces of him in her mother’s memories and the artifacts that are left behind. Along the way, she writes letters to her father—letters that cannot be sent. [15 min; Russian with English subtitles]
source: Bloomington Arts, IU Cinema, IU Hamilton Lugar School

The show showcased internet clips with the addition of Daniel's comedic perspective and was one of the longest-running comedy series for Comedy Central.  He can currently be heard and seen hosting his video podcast, TOSH SHOW. The show premiered garnering millions of watches and listens in the first weeks.  Daniel has performed live stand-up comedy to sold out venues all over the world including New Zealand, Australia, Montreal, and Ireland. title: It's On Us: Bystander Intervention Workshop
description:
The It’s On Us: Bystander Intervention Workshop is a 90-minute, audience-driven bystander intervention program that teaches students the skills needed to help in situations involving alcohol and drug use that could lead to a sexual assault. Students practice helping in real-life scenarios and discuss how alcohol and drug use influences a person’s ability to give and get consent to engage in sexual activity with another person.

It's On Us is required for all first-year and transfer undergraduate students. You must attend one session to fulfill the requirement.

Instructions:

Those who arrive more than 5 minutes after the start time will NOT be allowed into the session and will need to attend a different session

If this is your assigned workshop but you cannot attend it in its entirety, please RSVP for a different workshop.

title: Victoria Popritkin, soprano; Paulina Villarreal, mezzo-soprano; Shuichi Umeyama, piano
description: Repertoire  Wolf: 'Mignon-Lieder' from "Goethe-Lieder" (1889)Rachmaninoff: Six Romances, Op. 38 (1916)Guastavino: Cita (1943)Guastavino: La rosa y el sauce (1942)Guastavino: Hermano (1968)Guastavino: Pampamapa (1968)About the Faculty Artists  Mezzo-soprano Paulina Villarreal is assistant professor of music in voice at the Indiana University Jacobs School of Music. A recipient of the Yamaha Corporation Excellence in Music Education Award, she brings a dynamic career as a performer, educator, and advocate for Latin American and Iberian vocal music. Prior to joining the Jacobs School, Villarreal served on faculty at the University of Memphis. She continues on faculty at the Hawaii Performing Arts Festival and is founder and artistic director of the Cantos para el Mundo concert series in Mexico. Previous academic appointments include Centenary College of Louisiana and Millikin University. She was a graduate assistant at the University of Cincinnati College–Conservatory of Music, where she earned a doctoral degree. Villarreal has appeared with Opera Saratoga, Cincinnati Opera, Opera Fusion: New Works, the Tanglewood Music Center, Boston Pops, Cincinnati Symphony, Princeton Symphony, and Opera Western Reserve, among others prestigious institutions. Equally at home in classical, musical theater, and commercial music, she is recognized for her artistic versatility and cultural advocacy. A sought-after clinician, lecturer, and published researcher, she has presented for the National Association of Teachers of Singing, National Opera Association, College Music Society, Classical Singer Convention, and institutions across Latin America and the Caribbean. Her students have earned major roles and coveted spots in Young Artist programs and competitions in the United States and abroad. Career highlights include the title role in Carmen, the world premiere of Mango Suite with the Princeton Symphony, and Simply Sondheim with the Boston Pops. Her recitals often spotlight her research on Latin American song, zarzuela, and Mexican composer María Grever.Shuichi Umeyama is assistant professor of music and an opera coach at the Indiana University Jacobs School of Music. A sought-out collaborator by internationally prominent vocalists, pianist Umeyama is a highly seasoned concert performer. Equally at ease as a soloist and accompanist, he commands a vast repertoire in both vocal and instrumental literature. He has performed piano concertos by Brahms, Beethoven, Chopin, and Rachmaninoff, among others, and his recitals have been broadcast on Japan National Broadcasting (NHK). In the United States, Umeyama has served as coach or accompanist for San Francisco Opera, Houston Grand Opera, Santa Fe Opera, Opera Theatre of Saint Louis, Opera North, Indianapolis Opera, and others. He has been an official accompanist for the Metropolitan Opera National Laffont Competition for over 30 years. As a musical coach, he has participated in numerous operatic world premieres, including Our Town (Rorem), Jeppe (Sandstrom), Vincent (Rands), The Tale of Lady Thị Knh, What the Horse Eats (Phan), Anne Frank (Ran), Tevye's Daughters (Weiser), and The Amazing Adventures of Kavalier and Clay (Bates). In 2017, Umeyama taught voice students at Aichi University of the Arts in Japan as a resident artist. He earned Bachelor of Music and Master of Music degrees from Tokyo National University of Fine Arts and Music.

title: Hot Tuesdays: Jazz Combos – Jeremy Allen Group 2; Greg Ward Group
By Their Own Hands: The Black Filmmakers Hall of Fame exhibit
description: By Their Own Hands includes one-of-a-kind items such as Sidney Poitier's autographed shooting script for Let's Do It Again (1975), the Nicholas Brothers' tap shoes, and letters from icons like Maya Angelou and Oprah Winfrey, among others. The centerpiece is a stunning wall of 42 BFHFI inductees' handprints, a distinction comparable to receiving a star on the Hollywood Walk of Fame! Select examples of inductees' handprints include Lena Horne, Eartha Kitt, Richard Pryor, and Billy Dee Williams.  Special thanks to University Collections at McCalla for collaborating with and helping install the exhibit, and to the IU Foundation's Affinity Giving Group – Black Philanthropy Circle (BPC) for generously awarding the BFCA a grant to frame the handprints in Summer 2024.By Their Own Hands will remain on display through Spring 2026, and the BFCA will hold an educational event to re-engage the exhibit space during Black History Month. Contact us at bfca@iu.edu to request an appointment to view BFHFI collection items not curated for the exhibit.

title: Rising from the Dust: The Legacy of Michelle Materre exhibit
location: Black Film Center and Archive, 1320 E. 10th Street, Wells Library 044, Bloomington, IN, 47405 - Room Number: Phyllis Klotman Classroom 044B
description: Curated by archivists Sarah Petras and Dan Hassoun, this exhibit showcases posters, collages, photos, and realia relating to the career of pioneering film distributor Michelle Materre, including original materials from the first release of Daughters of the Dust! Stop by the BFCA office to get a look at this exhibit, which will stay up for the remainder of the 2025-2026 academic year.

title: Total African Cinema Exhibit
location: Black Film Center and Archive, 1320 E. 10th Street, Wells Library 044, Bloomington, IN, 47405 - Room Number: Exhibit/Reception Area
description: Donated by the Vieyra family in June 2021, the exhibition showcases highlights from The Paulin S. Vieyra Collection. The display case features intimate and rare items, including family portraits, Vieyra's typewriter, and a 16mm reel of Môl (1966). Five original collages by graphic designer Pouya Jahanshahi are at the center of the exhibition. Jahanshahi collaborated with Project Archivist Jason Byrne, who shaped the exhibition's themes and selected archival content. Student workers Sarah Petras, Thomas Pigott, Cree Prince, and Kudzai Zinyemba supported the project by helping process and research the materials.  The TOTAL AFRICAN CINEMA exhibit is on view in the BFCA reception area now through May 31, 2026!

research design
questionnaire development and review
sample design

database and data collection instrument programming including Qualtrics online survey software
usability testing

data processing
data analysis

title: Mindful Makers
description:
Join us for Mindful Makers, a weekly event built around being creative, meeting new people and feeling more confident in yourself and your wellness. Each week has a theme - come for one or come for all! We're here to have fun and make cool things.
January 21: Semester Vision Boards
Picture your new semester! Create a vision board that will motivate you to achieve this semester. Learn some tips on how to set great goals to help you achieve that vision.

January 28: Meditation and Aromatherapy
Ever been curious about aromatherapy and how it works? Here's your opportunity to learn more! While you're here, we'll also practice deep breathing techniques, progressive relaxation and guided visualization, and other simple techniques that don't require any special skills or equipment

February 4: Crochet the Stress Away
Whether you're a crochet expert or have never picked up a hook in your life, we have a place for you! Join us, learn basic crochet stitches, and talk about ways to have fun on campus and in the community.

February 11: Origami Ornaments and Overcoming
Sometimes life throws us curve balls. In this Mindful Makers, we'll go through the process of folding origami hearts or cranes, and using those to make beautiful ornaments that reflect challenges we've overcome. These cranes carry hope on their wings!

February 18: Air Clay Emotions
What does your angry monster look like? What about your joy? Use air clay to sculpt mini-monsters representing both supportive and challenging emotions, and learn ways of taming your more wild ones. Remember - not all monsters are scary. Sometimes they're pretty great!

February 25: Love Letters to Your Body and Cookie Decorating
Sometimes we need reminders of how great our bodies can be. In this week's MM, you'll learn simple bookbinding skills to make an accordion book, and fill those pages with what we like about ourselves and our bodies. Join our nutritionist, Katie Shepherd, for cookie decorating while you're here!

March 4: Watercolor Your Values
One of the most powerful motivators we have is our personal values. Paint a values portrait, and discuss how you can tie your goals into your values to keep yourself moving forward.

March 11: Sleep Vision Boards
Getting a good night's sleep is critical towards functioning at your best - but truthfully, most of us don't get nearly the quantity or quality of sleep we need. Create a sleep vision board as you learn ways to improve your sleep habits and why sleep is so important!

March 25: Create Your Own Journal
And we do mean from scratch! Learn simple Japanese stitchwork bookbinding to make your own book, decorate it to suit your tastes, and create a journal you can use however you like! (And if you're new to journaling, we can work on that as well!)

April 1: Perler Beads and Mindfulness
Being mindful doesn't take anything special. Sometimes it can be found in the smallest of things - like the little perler beads! Make yourself an ornament while discovering ways mindfulness can be a part of the everyday things you already do!

April 8: Gaming with Friends
Do you know that the oldest known game board is 9000 years old? We've been playing games for a long time! Well, not us personally. We're just going to play for a couple of hours. Come and join us!

April 15: Resinate
No, that's not a typo. Learn the basics of resin crafting and make your own ornaments or magnets. While here, share your favorite hobbies and why they matter to you - maybe you'll inspire someone else!

April 22: Affirmation Jars
At the end of the semester, sometimes we need reminders of what motivates us, makes us special, or just what matters to us. Create your own affirmation jar and fill it up with all those good things. When you're feeling low, pull one out and give yourself a boost!

April 29: Keep Growing
Whether you're looking forward to graduation, a summer off, or one filled with activity, summer is a great time for growth. Share your upcoming opportunities as you decorate a pot and select a new plant to nurture. Like plants, we need care to keep growing, so share your ideas for how you're going to care for yourself!

Hosted by: Health Promotion

Additional Information can be found at: https://beinvolved.indiana.edu/event/11977505

title: Open Mic Night at The Comedy Attic
location: The Comedy Attic,123 S Walnut St, Bloomington, IN 47408, USA
description:
Every Wednesday at 8PM is Comedy Attic Open Mic Night. Bloomington has one of the best local comedy scenes in the country, and it all starts with our open mic night!

Each comedian is asked to do no more than five minutes of completely original material. While we do not care about foul language in your set, we do not allow racist, homophobic, transphobic or rape/female bashing jokes. If you have to ask if it's racist, homophobic, transphobic or a rape joke, it probably is.

Comedians that wish to participate are asked to e-mail comedyattic@gmail.com mailto:comedyattic@gmail.com . Newcomers and any participants who are not paid professionals will be required to bring audience members to see them. This is non-negotiable. So if you plan on signing up, be sure to invite as many friends as you can. Good luck!

title: The Moment
description: $8.00 - Tickets for the 8 PM screening of THE MOMENT 2025 • USA • 103 minutes • dir. Aidan Zamiri Wednesday, April 1st at The Back Door (207 S College Ave) $8 | 21+ Showtime: 8 pm TRAILER Synopsis : A rising pop star navigates the complexities of fame and industry pressure while preparing for her arena tour debut. A flashy, tongue-in-cheek hyper-pop mockumentary, The Moment is Charli xcx’s creative reflection on her own meteoric success with brat . Directed and co-written by “360” music video director Aidan Zamiri in his feature debut, the film has a stylishly effortless rhythm and a uniquely self-aware, ironic sense of humor. Charli xcx plays an exaggeratedly manic version of herself, surrounded by a cast of characters that mirror both the friends and foes of the era. Hailey Gates and Alexander Skarsgård personify near-diametrically opposed influences in Charli xcx’s dynamic career; pieces in the grand scheme of brat’s enduring cultural power. Behind the satire and Charli xcx’s celebrity is an earnest and heartfelt expression of the weight of success, the price of legacy, and the fight to preserve artistic integrity. The Moment is a film for fans and newcomers alike — a lively, current, dazzling capsule of one of the most iconic artists of our time.

title: "Meet the Barbarians" Film Screening
location: HERMAN B WELLS LIBRARY - Room Number: Screening Room
description: Join us at the Wells Library screening room to watch French film Meet the Barbarians, part of the HLS Movie Night series.  When a French village accidentally welcomes the "wrong" refugee family, their assumptions get turned upside down. Director Julie Delpy's warm, funny dramedy asks who the real barbarians are when fear meets humanity.  This film was the summer 2024 box office hit in Europe. The town council of Paimpont, a small village in Brittany, has unanimously voted in favor of welcoming a family of Ukrainian refugees. However, the refugees who turn up aren't Ukrainian but Syrian, and some of the locals are not very pleased with their new neighbors. Yet the Fayad family avoids all the clichés the French villagers may have expected. So much so that, they leave one wondering: who in this little village are the true 'barbarians'? A film that gives you laughs, tears, cheers, and a good dose of Breton Sun.   Watch a trailer for the film here. (https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=6MBI076ZRU8)
source: IU Bloomington Libraries, IU Hamilton Lugar School

title: Artist Talk| Rania Matar: Where Do I Go? لوين روح؟
location: SIDNEY AND LOIS ESKENAZI MUSEUM OF ART
description: 5-6 p.m.Artist talk (2nd Floor, Martin Commons)6-7 p.m. Exhibition Visit (3rd Floor Gallery)About the Artist:  Matar was born and raised in Lebanon and moved to the U.S. in 1984. As a Lebanese-born, American/Palestinian woman and mother, Matar's cultural background, cross-cultural experience, and personal narrative inform her photography. She has dedicated her work to exploring issues of personal and collective identity, through photographs of female adolescence and womanhood. She works both in the United States where she lives and the Middle East where she is from, to focus on notions of identity and individuality all within the context of the underlying universality of these experiences.About the Curator:  Elliot Josephine Leila Reichert is an American curator, critic, and editor. She is the inaugural Curator of Contemporary Art at the Eskenazi Museum of Art at Indiana University and was a 2023-24 Propel Fellow with the Association of Art Museum Curators Foundation. She was formerly Art Editor of Newcity and Assistant Curator at the Block Museum of Art, Northwestern University. She has curated and assisted in developing critically celebrated exhibitions in the United States and abroad.    This event is part of the First Thursdays festivals showcase. (https://artsandhumanities.indiana.edu/council-programs/first-thursdays/index.html)

title: Drop-in Tours and Crafting
location: SIDNEY AND LOIS ESKENAZI MUSEUM OF ART - Room Number: Featured Exhibitions Gallery, First Floor
description: An Immersive ExperienceInspired by Indonesia's spectacular coral reefs, acclaimed fiber artist Mulyana (b. 1984, Bandung, Indonesia) invites you to wander through a handcrafted seascape where art and ecology meet.  Explore colorful coral reefs, playful fish, and fantastical sea creatures — all meticulously hand-knit and crocheted.  Pause to reflect on the delicate balance between human wellbeing and the vitality of our oceans.  Chat with a Museum Guide to deepen your understanding.  Create Your Own Sea CreatureEnjoy a hands-on activity in the gallery.  No prior experience is needed — a volunteer can guide you step by step.  Choose from a fish, octopus, lobster, sea urchin, or crab.  All supplies are provided, so you can dive right in!  Why Visit? This exhibition is more than art — it's a living conversation about beauty, fragility, and our shared responsibility to protect the ocean. Whether you're an art lover, a family looking for a creative activity, or simply curious, Vital Ecosystems offers a memorable experience for all ages.
source: Eskenazi Museum of Art, IU Arts & Humanities Institute

title: The DaVinci Code- Live on Stage
location: Waldron Auditorium,112 South Walnut Street, Bloomington, IN 47404, USA
description:
Constellation Stage & Screen presents “The DaVinci Code- Live on Stage,” on stage March 26 - April 12 at the Waldron Auditorium.

Dan Brown’s international best-seller comes to life on stage in this thrilling, high-stakes adventure! When the curator of the Louvre is found murdered under mysterious circumstances, symbologist Robert Langdon and cryptologist Sophie Neveu are thrust into an investigation that quickly unravels into a deadly conspiracy. As they follow a trail of cryptic puzzles and ancient codes, they discover a secret that could change the course of history. Packed with pulse-pounding suspense and stunning visuals, The Da Vinci Code is an exhilarating journey into the unknown. Recommended for ages 13+.

See Constellation’s website for more information, including a detailed performance schedule. Learn more and find tickets at seeconstellation.org/mainstage/da-vinci-code/ https://seeconstellation.org/mainstage/da-vinci-code/ https://seeconstellation.org/mainstage/da-vinci-code/

title: Kelley Teaching Seminar-Oh No! I Suspect Academic Misconduct – What do I do?
location: WILLIAM J. GODFREY GRADUATE AND EXECUTIVE EDUCATION CENTER - Room Number: 3059
description: Faculty Panel:Russ Clark, Sr. Lecturer, ODT, I-Core Co-Ordinator  Todd Haugh, Assoc. Prof., Bus. Law & Ethics, Faculty Liaison to the Office of Students Conduct  John Hill, Clinical Prof., ODT, Full-time MBA Chair  Lynn Keller, Lecturer, Bus. Law & Ethics, Co-chair Academic Fairness Committee  Amy Kinser, Teaching Prof., Computer Skills (CPCS), Executive Dir. of Teaching & Learning    We have all been there. A student looks a little too long at another student's work. A student sneaks in a little help from AI. A student plagiarizes tracts from the internet with no attribution. We are obligated by the university and by ethical practice to confront suspected academic misconduct. At the same time, there are clearly determined procedures that must be followed in order to levy any form of sanctions when academic misconduct has been confirmed.  Working through the roiling waters of an academic misconduct case can be a huge drag (in both senses of the word) on faculty, so learning how to address it can save time and heartache for everyone involved. Yes, this seminar will be worth your time!  In this Kelley Teaching Seminar, we will hear examples from Kelley classrooms and learn best practices for dealing with suspicions of academic misconduct. In particular, we will learn the necessary procedures and share ideas about how to frame discussions with students when discussing suspicions of academic misconduct.

description: Curious how to turn conferences, networking, and small conversations into real career opportunities? Join Erin Huntington, retired VP of International Corporate Affairs at Eli Lilly, for a hands-on session designed to give you the insider playbook for global careers.  Erin built a career bridging government service and international corporate strategy, managing global teams and connecting policy, advocacy, and market access for one of the world's leading pharmaceutical companies. She'll share real-world strategies that helped her move from Capitol Hill to global leadership—and how you can apply them too.What you'll get out of this session:      How to approach professionals, start conversations, and make connections that matter          Insider strategies for turning networking into internships, mentors, and career opportunities          Tools to tell your story confidently in interviews and professional settings          Tips for navigating global conferences and international professional spaces      This drop-in session is open to all HLS students. Light refreshments will be provided. Don't miss the chance to get concrete strategies that you can use immediately at conferences, internships, and global career events.

title: 21JPSI/EASC: "The Role of Dialect in Contemporary Japanese Society"(Prof. Jae Takeuchi; IU)
location: GLOBAL & INTERNATIONAL STUDIES BUILDING - Room Number: 1112
description: Talk abstract: Japanese dialects continue to be used in regions across Japan, despite the promotion of Standard Japanese through education and media. Drawing on examples from Ehime Prefecture, this presentation will discuss how Japanese dialects are evolving and consider what they contribute to the linguistic landscape of contemporary Japan.Jae DiBello Takeuchi is Associate Professor of Japanese in the Department of East Asian Languages and Cultures at Indiana University. Dr. Takeuchi conducts research in Japanese sociolinguistics and applied linguistics, second language acquisition, and foreign language pedagogy. Her research focuses on the experiences of Japanese second language speakers who are long-term residents of Japan. She is particularly interested in how they navigate Japanese speech styles, such as dialects and keigo, which they encounter in local communities, workplaces, and leisure activities. At Indiana University, Dr. Takeuchi teaches courses in Japanese language and sociolinguistics. Before coming to Indiana, she taught Japanese at Clemson University and served as the director of the Languages and International Business program.  Dr. Takeuchi's 2023 book Language Ideologies and L2 Speaker Legitimacy: Native Speaker Bias in Japan examines native speaker bias and L2 speaker legitimacy. Her recent projects focus on linguistic microaggression and consider how speaker identity and markers such as accent impact L2 speakers' acceptance in Japanese communities.
source: IU Arts & Humanities Institute, IU Hamilton Lugar School

title: REEI Colloquium: "Ukraine's Alleged 'East-West' Domestic Divide: Why Has the Country Survived 2014 and 2022?" with Petro Kuzyk
location: GLOBAL & INTERNATIONAL STUDIES BUILDING - Room Number: 4067
description: Since gaining independence in 1991, Ukraine has often been regarded as a hopelessly divided society and a failing polity. However, this sticky image of Ukraine does not match the fact that the country has been able to resist both Russia's limited military and hybrid attack in 2014 and full-scale aggression in 2022. In this presentation, Dr. Kuzyk will address the issue of deluded and manipulated interpretations of Ukrainian domestic 'east-west' differences. He will argue that the alleged 'east-west' split is overstated, unstable, receding over time, and shaped by foreign misconceptions about Ukraine's societal differences as much as by internal political disputes and conflicts.  Petro Kuzyk is an Associate Professor of the International Relations Department at Ivan Franko National University of Lviv (Ukraine). His recent research has focused on Ukraine's 'east-west' cleavage and its international misinterpretations, as well as the representation of the country's wartime diplomatic efforts in domestic public discourse. During the 2025-26 academic year, Petro Kuzyk is a Fulbright Visiting Professor at the Byrnes Institute of Indiana University-Bloomington.

Beetlejuice | Additional Films and Guests
description: After Barbara and Adam Maitland (Geena Davis and Alec Baldwin) die in a car accident, they find themselves stuck haunting their country residence, unable to leave the house. When the unbearable Deetzes and their teen daughter Lydia (Winona Ryder) buy the home, the Maitlands attempt to scare them away without success. Their efforts attract Beetlejuice (Michael Keaton), a rambunctious spirit whose help quickly becomes dangerous for the Maitlands and innocent Lydia. [92 min; comedy, horror; English]This screening is in conjunction with IU Auditorium's upcoming April 10-12 performances ofBeetlejuice: The Musical.

Bloomington Community Farmers' Market
location: City of Bloomington, 401 N Morton St, Bloomington, IN 47404, USA
description:
The Bloomington Community Farmers' Market takes place rain or shine every Saturday, April through October, at Showers Common next to City Hall, 401 N. Morton St. in Bloomington.
April-September: 8 a.m.-12:30 p.m.
October: 9 a.m.-12:30 p.m.
November: 9 a.m.-12:30 p.m. at the Pavilion at Switchyard Park
The Market is a gathering place where local farmers, makers, and neighbors come together to share the foods, traditions, and connections that strengthen our community and support a reliable local food supply.
Market goers find a wide assortment of goods from the farm and field, including fruits and vegetables, eggs, meats and cheeses, honey, seasonal flowers, and annual and perennial plants. Food and beverage artisans provide a tasteful variety of drinks, pastries, and savory dishes. This weekly gathering provides opportunities for individuals to connect with their community in meaningful ways and support local farmers and small businesses.

title: Fire on the Landscape with Sam Shoaf
location: The Laura Hare Nature Preserve at Downey Hill
description: The whippoorwill, a species of concern here in Indiana, has found safe refuge in the recently burned areas of Downey Hill, but why? Join us for a guided hike through the Laura Hare Nature Preserve at Downey Hill with Sam Shoaf, Director of Operations and Burn Boss at Eco Logic, LLC, to explore the vital role of fire in Indiana’s ecosystems. Sam will share insights into how prescribed burns are planned and conducted, and how fire helps maintain healthy forests, promote native plant growth, and support wildlife diversity. Participants will walk through areas recently shaped by fire and learn how this natural process contributes to long-term habitat restoration and resilience.

Indiana University Traditional Powwow
description:
13th Annual PowwowSaturday, April 4, 202612–5 p.m.Wilkinson Hall
The Indiana University annual Traditional Powwow attracts hundreds of visitors each year and boasts a line-up of nationally known dancers and singers, along with traditional foods, crafts, and dance clothing. Performers of all ages participate, carrying on the traditions of their people as they represent them through their feathers and beadwork, their dance clothes, the songs they sing, and the styles in which they dance. The powwow is an important time when generations come together to share in dance and song, and to celebrate the diversity of contemporary Native American tribal identity.
In addition to the social dancing at IU’s Traditional Powwow, vendors offer handcrafted items such as silver and turquoise jewelry, blankets, artwork, beaded purses, and other accessories.

Hosted by: First Nations Educational and Cultural Center, Native American Student Association

Additional Information can be found at: https://beinvolved.indiana.edu/event/12311686

Writing in the Galleries: Where Do I Go?
location: SIDNEY AND LOIS ESKENAZI MUSEUM OF ART - Room Number: Check in on the first floor
description: In this workshop, we begin by looking closely at artwork—asking what we see, think, feel, and wonder. Together, we'll explore the artist's possible message and its social meaning, then turn those reflections into our own creative responses.  Whether you're a writer or simply curious to try something new, you'll find inspiration here and a welcoming community to share the journey.  About the Exhibition  Step into the world of acclaimed photographer Rania Matar (b. 1964, Lebanon), whose striking portraits document young women persisting in uncertain times. Matar's lens highlights women's creativity, strength, dignity, and resilience—offering a powerful counter-narrative to the challenges of her country and region. Read more about this featured exhibition (https://artmuseum.indiana.edu/exhibitions/upcoming/_feature-exhibitions/2026-03-05-rania-matar-where-do-i-go.html).  What to Bringyour favorite pencil (no pens).  a notebook or paper. Clipboards are available to borrow.  a phone or camera to take a picture of the art for later reference.  Know Before You GoArrive early, check in, and get ready for an on-time start.Readwhere to find us, where to park, accessibility, and museum guidelines.About Your Facilitator  Dedaimia Whitney earned an MFA in fiction from Indiana University under the direction of Scott Russell Sanders. She taught creative writing at Franklin College for many years. She has served the museum as a docent since 2019. Her favorite works of art are those that are both visually stimulating and thought-provoking.
source: Eskenazi Museum of Art, IU Arts & Humanities Institute

title: Beginner/Intermediate Drawing Workshop
location: SIDNEY AND LOIS ESKENAZI MUSEUM OF ART - Room Number: Check in with Guest Services on the first floor
description: Reserve your spot. If this page says, "Registration has closed" that means the waitlist is full.SuppliesBring your favorite drawing pencil (e.g. 4B), a sketchbook or drawing paper, and a phone or device to photograph artwork (without flash) as we sketch.  Know Before You GoArrive early to find parking, check in, and get your drawing supplies ready.  At 1:55 p.m. any unclaimed reservations will be released to guests who are standing by waiting for a spot.  Where to find us, where to park, accessibility, and museum guidelines (https://artmuseum.indiana.edu/visit/your-visit.html).  This workshop is recommended for ages 12 and up. Anyone under 18, who is not an IU student, must be accompanied by an adult.  When You ArriveCheck in with Guest Services at the information desk on the first floor, even if you come often and even if you are late. We appreciate having an accurate attendance count.  Store all backpacks and large bags before entering the galleries. A small handbag may be carried at your side.  Get your supplies ready. Attach an 18x24" piece of newsprint to a drawing board.  Cancellations  This workshop often has a waitlist. Please use the cancellation link included with your confirmation email if your plans change.About the Instructor  John Cheek has taught credit and non-credit classes for eight years in portrait painting, landscape painting, palette knife painting, life drawing, beginning drawing, and advanced drawing, as well as in design. He volunteers his time and expertise to facilitate drawing workshops at the Eskenazi Museum of Art.
source: Eskenazi Museum of Art, IU Arts & Humanities Institute

title: Chamber Orchestra – Arthur Fagen, conductor; David Holloway, horn soloist
description: Enjoy this performance from almost anywhere in the world viaLIVE@jacobs!Repertoire  Wagner: Siegfried Idyll (1870)Elizabeth Raum: Sherwood Legend for horn and orchestra (1996)  W. A. Mozart: Symphony No. 41 in C Major, K.551 ("Jupiter")About the ConductorArthur Fagen is professor of music in orchestral conducting and cochair of the Department of Orchestral Conducting at the Indiana University Jacobs School of Music, where he has been on faculty since 2008. Additionally, he has been music director of The Atlanta Opera since 2010. Fagen has conducted opera productions at the world's most prestigious opera houses and music festivals. From 1998 to 2001, he was invited regularly as guest conductor at the Vienna State Opera. On the concert podium, he has appeared with numerous internationally known orchestras. Fagen has an opera repertory of more than 100 works. He has served as principal conductor in Kassel and Brunswick, as chief conductor of the Flanders Opera of Antwerp and Ghent, as music director of the Queens Symphony Orchestra, and as a member of the conducting staff of Lyric Opera of Chicago. From 2002 to 2007, he was music director of the Dortmund Philharmonic Orchestra and the Dortmund Opera. He and the Dortmund Philharmonic were invited to the Concertgebouw in Amsterdam and the Palais de Beaux Arts in Brussels as well as to Salzburg, Beijing, and Shanghai. Fagen conducted a new production of Turandot at the Atlanta Opera in 2007, opening the season and inaugurating the new opera house, the Cobb Energy Performing Arts Center. He was first-prize winner of the Baltimore Symphony Orchestra Conductors' Competition as well as a prizewinner of the Gino Marinuzzi International Conductors' Competition in Italy. Fagen has recorded for BMG, Bayerischer Rundfunk, SFB, and WDR Cologne. He records regularly for Naxos, for which he has completed the six symphonies of Bohuslav Martinů. His Naxos recording of Martinů's piano concertos was awarded an Editor's Choice award in the March 2010 issue of Gramophone magazine.

title: The Player | City Lights Film Series
description: A classic of Hollywood satires, The Player follows film producer Griffin Mills (Tim Robbins) through a story of blackmail, murder, and moral decay. After he starts receiving death threat postcards from a mysterious screenwriter, Mills sets out to uncover the sender's identity while scheming to secure his job. [124 min; comedy, drama; English]

title: Double Exposure 2026 | IU Cinema
description: Presenting original student work, Double Exposure is an innovative and collaborative program made possible through a partnership which brings together The Media School Cinematic Arts, Jacobs School of Music's Music Scoring for Visual Media program, and IU Cinema. Each film is made, scored, and sound-designed by Indiana University students and will receive its world-premiere presentation on IU Cinema's big screen.AnchorsDirector: Rocco Jann | Music: Carson MericalAnchorsis a chaotic, farcical duel for viewership between two insane media personalities. While some films make thoughtful critiques of the current state of our broadcast news landscape,Anchorsopts for a… different approach. Absurd headlines, unhinged media personalities, whacky visuals—Anchorsseeks to make fun of the news in order to make sense of it. So, sit back, grab a friend, and strap in tight, because it's time to watch the news like you've never seen it before. (7 min)HandlebarsDirector: Dylan Dankel | Music: Niki SimerlyAging out of foster care, Rowan faces the uncertainty of independence with little more than a backpack, a trash bag of clothes, and a frayed photograph of his younger sibling Riley. Fueled by the drive to reconnect, he sets out into the night.There's a Cult in these WoodsDirector: Nolan Goode | Music: Theo MorrisI've come to the conclusion that I can never be content with one specific way of life, or way of thinking. When it comes to these things, I'm too curious of a person, a trait slowly dying from the chokehold that goliaths like organized religion and technology have put us in. That's a large reason why I made this film. (6 min)InvocationDirector: Betsy Leija | Music: Sean GiovanettiHave you ever felt that something was calling to you? That somewhere in the dark, something was waiting, promising to awaken feelings you have never known? InInvocation, Isa is caught in the haunting struggle between her religious past and a mysterious being that calls to her each night, drawing her toward forbidden desire and uneasy serenity. As her longing deepens, Isa must confront whether surrender is damnation or liberation. (5 min)Euphoric SpitDirector:Blake Schwandt |Music:Donavan WalkerSet in a charged Los Angeles summer, the film follows a young artist's journey through fleeting sexual encounters that open portals into his unfelt interior world. What begins as bodily exchange becomes a spiritual excavation—an attempt to sense the parts of himself that exist beyond sight. Through fragmented memories, warmth, and ecstatic disorientation, the film maps desire as a path toward wholeness. (5 min)Rainbow NightDirector:Catie Stanton |Music: Tianqi ZhangWhen Cassandra, a beautiful soul who has lost her way, goes home, she is faced with a choice: to follow in her mother's footsteps or to be better. This therapeutic film exhibits a combination of anger, resentment, love, loss, but most importantly, hope. Writer and director CM Stanton, composer Tianqi Zhang, and everyone else hope to take you in, transform you, and heal you.Contains suicide and mental health issues.(6 min)DevourerDirector: Goodman Murphy-Smith | Music: Tu GuA nameless teenager wanders an unfamiliar house infested by a past it cannot forget. Deep in its endless hallways, a door hides many eyes.Devoureris a sleepwalk through a contemporary teenage experience, lost in a landscape of violent videos, alienation, and technology rotting in the shadow of the Information Age.Contains implied violence against a child and brief flashing lights.(6 min)Game Theory 101Director: Gracie Runge | Music: Shmuli MyersDom and Kevin, two best friends who enjoy hosting legendary game nights in their apartment, are pushed to their breaking point by their acquaintance, Fitz, who constantly crashes out during games. Fitz is a poor sport, and every time he loses a game, he goes "nuclear." Dom and Kevin propose a challenge to Fitz: he has one last chance at game night before he is banned from game nights forever. (8 min)Nostalgia is a LiarDirector: Deo Akiode | Music: Donavan WalkerOur actions outlive us, but so will our inactions. We all live with the burden of our regrets and mistakes, but for some of us, we know our time to make amends for them is running short. In this film, we watch a desperate man try valiantly to do so: his journey reflecting themes of loss, memory, and harsh honesty. (8 min)To This OceanDirector:Polina Saburova |Music:Camilo RincónThis is an experimental multilingual audio-visual poem about home and belonging in the context of migration in the ever-changing world. How can you create a home where there isn't supposed to be one? When you leave, what stays with you of the place that you call home? Is it a smell or a touch or an image or a sound? Who or what is a home? (6 min)
source: IU Cinema, IU Jacobs School of Music

title: E5C4P3: The Music Of Journey
location: Brown County Playhouse, 70 S. Van Buren St, Nashville, IN, 47448, United States
description:
E5C4P3 was formed in June of 1993. From that year through 1999, E5C4P3 traveled nationwide and paid tribute to one of rock history’s most beloved bands, performing for tens of thousands of fans. E5C4P3 is still known today as the original and best Journey tribute band to ever exist. In 1999, E5C4P3 went on hiatus and the world was suddenly filled with multiple Journey tributes trying to fill the void that E5C4P3 left behind. In 2008, E5C4P3 was reformed to recreate the magic of a Journey concert like no one else can. The band covers the music with a note-for-note accuracy that is second to none and performs each show with the high-energy excitement of a real Journey concert as well as visually recreating the essence of early 80’s Journey! Focusing on the “Steve Perry era,” E5C4P3 covers all the hits from “Lights” and “Wheel In The Sky” to “Separate Ways” and “Be Good To Yourself,” including occasional surprises for all the die-hard Journey fans.  For all of the countless fans that have never stopped believing, they’re back!

It's On Us: Bystander Intervention Workshop
description:
The It’s On Us: Bystander Intervention Workshop is a 90-minute, audience-driven bystander intervention program that teaches students the skills needed to help in situations involving alcohol and drug use that could lead to a sexual assault. Students practice helping in real-life scenarios and discuss how alcohol and drug use influences a person’s ability to give and get consent to engage in sexual activity with another person.

It's On Us is required for all first-year and transfer undergraduate students. You must attend one session to fulfill the requirement.

Instructions:

Those who arrive more than 5 minutes after the start time will NOT be allowed into the session and will need to attend a different session

If this is your assigned workshop but you cannot attend it in its entirety, please RSVP for a different workshop.

All Indiana University events, groups, activities, educational, cultural, and historical observances welcome all IU students and other members of the Indiana University community.

Hosted by: Office for Sexual Violence Prevention and Victim Advocacy

Additional Information can be found at: https://beinvolved.indiana.edu/event/11970055

title: Spring 2026 Colloquium Series with Carlos Colmenares Gil
description: The Center for Latin American and Caribbean Studies is proud to present the continuation of its Colloquium Series for Spring 2026.  We welcome Dr. Carlos Colmenares Gil as he presents Miguel James and the "Angelitos Negros" of Venezuelan Poetry.  This presentation examines Venezuelan-Trinidadian poet Miguel James' oeuvre as critical of a national project of racial democracy in Venezuela, and of how such a project informs structures of literary reception by limiting them. In a country where both political and cultural discourse frame and construct a Venezuelan identity as a seemingly flexible mestizaje, James' work demands taking a step back from it and articulating these heterogeneous cultural and social elements otherwise. James' seemingly multiculturalist enumerations and mixture of referents move away from a radical critique; instead, they offer a counterpoint where mestizaje as a demagogic identity is displaced in favor of a process of amefricanidade, as theorized by Lélia González, in which blackness is neither monumentalized in an immemorial past, nor dissolved in a postracial present. To understand this transition from one socio-political structure of meaning to another, Stuart Hall's concept of articulation will be key to illuminating James' poetry.    Carlos Colmenares Gil studies the Spanish-Caribbean, continental Caribbean, and Brazil, specifically how the cultural expressions (literature, film, music) from these areas are immersed, or resist to be immersed, in a cosmopolitan logic which translates and creates a simplified structure of critical reception for them. His work engages with a long tradition of Latin American thought as well as continental philosophy, psychoanalysis, and anthropology, examining questions of high and low art, the idea of the popular, the hyper and translocal, and the intellectual life of minoritized subjects. He has published on social theorist Alejandro Moreno and on Afro-Venezuelan music. Currently, he is working on a book project focused on Venezuelan poetry and the critique of interiority; and a long essay centered around Igor Barreto and the destruction and re-composition of the landscape in relation to other Latin American poets. In parallel, he continues to research on Afro-Latin drums and the voices and chants that accompany them.                 The CLACS Spring 2026 Colloquium meets Mondays from February through April, with faculty and guest speakers engaging Latin America and the Caribbean through research on history, culture, language, politics, and the arts. Check the CLACS Calendar (https://events.iu.edu/clacs/) for details.
source: IU Hamilton Lugar School, IU La Casa Latino Cultural Center

title: It's On Us: Bystander Intervention Workshop
description:
The It’s On Us: Bystander Intervention Workshop is a 90-minute, audience-driven bystander intervention program that teaches students the skills needed to help in situations involving alcohol and drug use that could lead to a sexual assault. Students practice helping in real-life scenarios and discuss how alcohol and drug use influences a person’s ability to give and get consent to engage in sexual activity with another person.

It's On Us is required for all first-year and transfer undergraduate students. You must attend one session to fulfill the requirement.

Instructions:

Those who arrive more than 5 minutes after the start time will NOT be allowed into the session and will need to attend a different session

If this is your assigned workshop but you cannot attend it in its entirety, please RSVP for a different workshop.

All Indiana University events, groups, activities, educational, cultural, and historical observances welcome all IU students and other members of the Indiana University community.
