title: Tom Walsh Jazz Ensemble
description: Enjoy this performance from almost anywhere in the world viaLIVE@jacobs!About the DirectorTom Walsh is Robert J. Waller Sr. and Robert J. Waller Jr. Professor of Music (saxophone) and chair of the Jazz Studies Department at the Indiana University Jacobs School of Music. An active performer of jazz and classical music, he has presented concerts and workshops in South Africa, China, Brazil, Japan, Germany, Austria, Italy, Croatia, Slovenia, Azerbaijan, Costa Rica, and across the United States. In 2019, his beginning improvisation book Jazz Master Teaches You to Improvise was published by Shanghai Music Publishing House. Recent recordings include The Pandemic Sessions by the IU jazz faculty, Luke Gillespie's Moving Mists, Basically Baker, Vol. 2, with the Buselli-Wallarab Jazz Orchestra (Patois), Mike Hackett's New Point of View (Summit) and two CDs released on the IUMusic label: Holiday Celebration and Sylvia McNair's Romance. Premiere performances include Scott Jones's concert band arrangement of Russell Peck's The Upward Stream, Chris Rutkowski's Concerto for Alto Saxophone and Wind Ensemble, and David Baker's Concerto for Alto Saxophone and Orchestra. His latest solo CD release is Intersections (Arizona University Recordings), featuring Luke Gillespie on piano. Earlier solo CDs include New Life (RIAX) and Shaking the Pumpkin (RIAX). Other releases include the David Baker Concerto with the Czech National Symphony Orchestra on Paul Freeman Introduces David Baker, Vol. XII (Albany), Basically Baker with the Buselli/Wallarab Jazz Orchestra (Patois), and Sky Scrapings: Saxophone Music of Don Freund (AUR). Walsh has appeared at conferences of the International Association for Jazz Education, Jazz Education Network, North American Saxophone Alliance, and World Saxophone Congress. He is a Yamaha Performing Artist and a Vandoren Artist. He has taught at the Jamey Aebersold Summer Jazz Workshops since 1991. He co-leads the IU Summer Saxophone Academy with Otis Murphy and is co-director of the College Audition Preparation Workshop. Walsh earned degrees in Saxophone Performance and Jazz Studies from Indiana University, where his principal teachers were distinguished classical saxophonist Eugene Rousseau and renowned jazz educator David Baker. His other influential teachers include Mike Tracy, Pat LaBarbera, Jerry Coker, and David Liebman.

title: Common Threads
location: SIDNEY AND LOIS ESKENAZI MUSEUM OF ART - Room Number: Roehm Family Art-making Event
description: Looking for a welcoming space to crochet, knit, embroider, quilt, and explore other fiber arts? Interested in sharing your experiences and connecting with others while learning new skills? Or even just want a place to vent while you work on your scarf you've been crocheting for 2 years? Look no further; join us for Common Threads!  Common Threads is a free program open to the public and offers a welcoming environment to share, unwind, and learn! You can bring your own fiber art to work on while working alongside others who enjoy working in fiber and textiles. Or you can try learning something new; we welcome all skill levels! Either way, you're sure to have a good time.  This group meets on the 1st and 3rd Tuesday of each month from 3–4:30 p.m., excluding holidays and museum closures. The group will meet in the Roehm Family Art-making Studio, located inside the Simpson Center for Education on the 2nd floor of the Eskenazi Museum of Art.

title: Teenage Wasteland | Additional Films and Guests
description: At first glance, there's nothing especially memorable about Middletown, N.Y. Despite being one of the largest cities in Orange County, and only 70 miles north of Manhattan, most New Yorkers have never heard of it. According to former student Mike Regan, "You were always going somewhere else—going to the city, going to the Jersey Shore or going to Long Island, and everybody was always trying to get out." In the early '90s, a group of high-school students—with encouragement from an unconventional English teacher—decided to take a deeper look at their seemingly unmemorable town to produce an investigative documentary. Banding together to confront indifferent adults, corrupt politicians, and a violent criminal organization threatening their small town, what they uncovered sent shockwaves throughout their community. Teenage Wasteland is a coming-of-age story based on exclusive access to hundreds of hours of candid, humorous, and nostalgic video outtakes, and the cooperation of Fred Isseks and his intrepid students as they reckon, 30 years later, with a defining event in their lives and a remarkable story of civic courage. [110 min; documentary; English]
source: IU Cinema, IU Kelley School of Business

title: CANCELED: Ostrom Workshop | Research Series: Adriana Abreu
location: The Ostrom Workshop, 513 N PARK AVE - Room Number: Tocqueville
description: Bio  Adriana is an Amazonian environmental anthropologist focusing on resource management, traditional communities, conservation, gender equity, development, and socioenvironmental challenges in the Brazilian Amazon. She holds a B.A. in Social Sciences and an M.A. in Sociology & Anthropology from the University of Pará, Brazil. Over the past decade, she has been involved in researching socioenvironmental conflicts, development, and the resistance of rural communities in Amazonia as an activist-scholar. Adriana was previously a grantee of the Amazon Rainforest Fund, contributing to the journalist collaborative project "Quilombolas, Invisible Protectors of the Forest, in Sight of COVID-19" https://pulitzercenter.org/projects/quilombolas-invisble-protectors-forest-sight-covid-19-portuguese (https://nam12.safelinks.protection.outlook.com/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fpulitzercenter.org%2Fprojects%2Fquilombolas-invisble-protectors-forest-sight-covid-19-portuguese&data=05%7C02%7Cmackka%40iu.edu%7Ccd4eb2a425784381849508ddd389bf51%7C1113be34aed14d00ab4bcdd02510be91%7C1%7C0%7C638899310768561227%7CUnknown%7CTWFpbGZsb3d8eyJFbXB0eU1hcGkiOnRydWUsIlYiOiIwLjAuMDAwMCIsIlAiOiJXaW4zMiIsIkFOIjoiTWFpbCIsIldUIjoyfQ%3D%3D%7C0%7C%7C%7C&sdata=OEEE0nioleIGY2XjOsqO%2BNCZcPMfqzT%2B%2FXhDn5n52Bs%3D&reserved=0). Currently pursuing her Ph.D. in Anthropology at Indiana University, Adriana's research focuses on the intersection of formal rules and social norms in the arapaima fisheries community-based management. She is part of the project "Integrating Cross-scale Socio-Ecological Feedbacks in Freshwater Fisheries" co-led by Professor Eduardo Brondizio and is a member of the Center for Analysis of Socio-Ecological Landscapes (CASEL) at Indiana University.About the Ostrom Workshop Research Series  Learn about innovative research by Graduate Students and Visiting Scholars as they advance the mission of the Ostrom Workshop, building upon the theme of governance to understand and address major societal problems.

The Center for Survey Research consults with faculty, staff, and students across a wide range of substantive disciplines spanning business, education, the social sciences, and the health sciences. We are happy to assist with any qualitative or quantitative research project by providing consultation on:

questionnaire development and review
sample design

usability testing
sample management including high-volume email campaigns
data collection using online, telephone, mail, and face-to-face administration

data processing
data analysis

Takes place in the Wells Library Scholars' Commons, first floor of the east tower. No question too big or too small!

title: Ostrom Workshop | Research Series: Paulo Ramos
location: The Ostrom Workshop, 513 N PARK AVE - Room Number: Tocqueville
description: Bio  Paulo Ramos is born from the womb of his mother, Terezinha Ramos de Jesus. He is an Afro-Brazilian visual artist, performer, actor, and poet whose multidisciplinarywork spans video, performance, and poetry. His acclaimed poetry book, Corpos d'água, was published in 2023 in Brazil. And in 2026, the book, titled Bodies of Water, will be released in the United States, in English, by NP-PRESS and Counterpath Press. Ramos' compelling performance piece, presented at the 12th At First Sigh New Works Festival Indiana University and in venues across São Paulo and Tepoztlán, gave rise to the thought-provoking videoperformance Bodies of Water: Threebuckets: a river, a mangrow, and a sean. Paulo Ramos is a Ph.D. in Social Science in Gender and Black Studies in PUCSP. Pontifícia Universidade Católica de São Paulo,Paulo Ramos continues to explore and amplify the narratives of Afro-Brazilian identity.Abstract  Bodies of Water is an artistic performance, an academic confrontation grounded in the studies of Denise Ferreira da Silva (2020), constructed through a poetic pathway in which the body becomes theoretical expansion. According to Denise Ferreira da Silva, anticolonial art must be a confrontation.  Bodies of Water: A Hybrid Cartography Flowing into an etnographyWE is part of my doctoral research, built from the memory, orality, and ancestry of Black women who turn their daily notes into the reinvention of life in community. This reinvention constitutes their intellectual production, naming everything that inhabits space or emerges from the ways they experience the world—one that is crossed by race, class, and the environmental. It concerns the relationship between water and Black women-mothers as a way of thinking existence, both as a site of escape and of resistance.  Here, I propose to reflect from a fieldwork perspective that traverses my own self—not through an autoethnographic method, but immersed in an etnographyWE, a new theory in development for thinking about the women and the waters that came before me.  Thus, Bodies of Water brings forth tension, discomfort, and a critiCREATIVE approach developed alongside my research on my grandmother, my mother, my aunt, and the waters—and on environmental racism (Costa 2022; Pacheco 2012). Bodies of Water is body–time–space poetry. Above all, it is confrontation. It is a profound dialogue between the Black body and water—living elements, yet constrained by the violence imposed by temporality.  Ancestrality and Afropessimism are both present here, navigating together in the same vessel through the currents of the Black body (Pinho 2021; Smith 2015); between these two concepts, ecology emerges as the missing link that connects them.About the Ostrom Workshop Research Series  Learn about innovative research by Graduate Students and Visiting Scholars as they advance the mission of the Ostrom Workshop, building upon the theme of governance to understand and address major societal problems.

title: Artist Diploma Recital – Nayeon Kim, piano
Wiles Lecture: "A Rebellious Generation: Solidarity Activism in Poland and the Diaspora" with Mary Erdmans
location: Indiana Memorial Union - Room Number: Federal Room
description: This talk looks at activism among political refugees from the Solidarność movement. The social-historical context created a political generation and radical disposition that impelled activism over time and space. Factors that explain activism in the diaspora include biographical availability, geographic location, and transnational political networks.  Mary Erdmans is Professor of Sociology at Case Western Reserve University. A Fulbright Scholar and past president of the Polish American Historical Association, she authored three award-winning books: Opposite Poles, The Grasinski Girls, and On Becoming a Teen Mom. Her articles have appeared in Sociological Quarterly, Journal of American Ethnic History, and Studia Migracynjne.Reception to follow.Sponsored by the Polish Studies Center with additional support from The Byrnes Institute (REEI), the Department of Slavic and East European Languages and Cultures, the Department of Sociology, and the Institute for European Studies.

title: Mindful Makers
description:
Join us for Mindful Makers, a weekly event built around being creative, meeting new people and feeling more confident in yourself and your wellness. Each week has a theme - come for one or come for all! We're here to have fun and make cool things.
January 21: Semester Vision Boards
Picture your new semester! Create a vision board that will motivate you to achieve this semester. Learn some tips on how to set great goals to help you achieve that vision.

January 28: Meditation and Aromatherapy
Ever been curious about aromatherapy and how it works? Here's your opportunity to learn more! While you're here, we'll also practice deep breathing techniques, progressive relaxation and guided visualization, and other simple techniques that don't require any special skills or equipment

February 4: Crochet the Stress Away
Whether you're a crochet expert or have never picked up a hook in your life, we have a place for you! Join us, learn basic crochet stitches, and talk about ways to have fun on campus and in the community.

February 11: Origami Ornaments and Overcoming
Sometimes life throws us curve balls. In this Mindful Makers, we'll go through the process of folding origami hearts or cranes, and using those to make beautiful ornaments that reflect challenges we've overcome. These cranes carry hope on their wings!

February 18: Air Clay Emotions
What does your angry monster look like? What about your joy? Use air clay to sculpt mini-monsters representing both supportive and challenging emotions, and learn ways of taming your more wild ones. Remember - not all monsters are scary. Sometimes they're pretty great!

February 25: Love Letters to Your Body and Cookie Decorating
Sometimes we need reminders of how great our bodies can be. In this week's MM, you'll learn simple bookbinding skills to make an accordion book, and fill those pages with what we like about ourselves and our bodies. Join our nutritionist, Katie Shepherd, for cookie decorating while you're here!

March 4: Watercolor Your Values
One of the most powerful motivators we have is our personal values. Paint a values portrait, and discuss how you can tie your goals into your values to keep yourself moving forward.

March 11: Sleep Vision Boards
Getting a good night's sleep is critical towards functioning at your best - but truthfully, most of us don't get nearly the quantity or quality of sleep we need. Create a sleep vision board as you learn ways to improve your sleep habits and why sleep is so important!

March 25: Create Your Own Journal
And we do mean from scratch! Learn simple Japanese stitchwork bookbinding to make your own book, decorate it to suit your tastes, and create a journal you can use however you like! (And if you're new to journaling, we can work on that as well!)

April 1: Perler Beads and Mindfulness
Being mindful doesn't take anything special. Sometimes it can be found in the smallest of things - like the little perler beads! Make yourself an ornament while discovering ways mindfulness can be a part of the everyday things you already do!

April 8: Gaming with Friends
Do you know that the oldest known game board is 9000 years old? We've been playing games for a long time! Well, not us personally. We're just going to play for a couple of hours. Come and join us!

April 15: Resinate
No, that's not a typo. Learn the basics of resin crafting and make your own ornaments or magnets. While here, share your favorite hobbies and why they matter to you - maybe you'll inspire someone else!

April 22: Affirmation Jars
At the end of the semester, sometimes we need reminders of what motivates us, makes us special, or just what matters to us. Create your own affirmation jar and fill it up with all those good things. When you're feeling low, pull one out and give yourself a boost!

April 29: Keep Growing
Whether you're looking forward to graduation, a summer off, or one filled with activity, summer is a great time for growth. Share your upcoming opportunities as you decorate a pot and select a new plant to nurture. Like plants, we need care to keep growing, so share your ideas for how you're going to care for yourself!

Hosted by: Health Promotion

Additional Information can be found at: https://beinvolved.indiana.edu/event/11977506

title: Talking Conservation: Janet McCabe
location: LOWELL E. BAIER HALL/IU MAURER SCHOOL OF LAW - Room Number: 123
description: Janet McCabe, former Deputy Administrator of the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency and current Professor of Practice at Indiana University, will deliver a lecture titled "Connections and Consequences: Federal Environmental Policy and State-Level Impacts." She will discuss how federal environmental policy is interpreted, implemented, and enforced at the state level. Drawing on her experience in both federal and state government, she will explore the opportunities and challenges of translating national environmental laws into meaningful protections on the ground, with particular attention to Indiana and the Midwest.About "Talking Conservation"  Talking Conservation is a public education series from Conservation Law Center that explores pressing issues in environmental and conservation law, with an emphasis on Indiana and the Midwest. Hosted in Bloomington and recorded for online viewing, each event features expert voices from across the conservation landscape—legal advocates, scientists, policymakers, and community leaders—sharing insights on topics ranging from clean water and habitat protection to land conservation and public interest lawyering for the environment.  Through these engaging, accessible lectures, Conservation Law Center aims to inform, inspire, and empower the public to better understand and influence the legal and policy frameworks that shape the natural world we share. About the speaker:Janet McCabe is an environmental policy expert with a distinguished career in public service, law, and academia. From April 2021 to October 2024, she served as the deputy administrator of the United States Environmental Protection Agency (EPA), where she played a key role in shaping the nation's environmental policies and oversaw all aspects of management of the agency and its workforce.From 2017 to 2021, McCabe was a professor of practice at the Indiana University McKinney School of Law and directed the IU Environmental Resilience Institute (ERI), where she began as assistant director for policy and implementation in 2017. She rejoined the Indiana University faculty in summer 2025 with a joint appointment at McKinney Law, O'Neill School of Public and Environmental Affairs, and the ERI.McCabe also served at the EPA starting in 2009, as principal deputy to the assistant administrator of the Office of Air and Radiation (OAR). She served as acting assistant administrator for OAR from 2013 through 2016. Her leadership at the EPA was instrumental in advancing critical air quality and climate change initiatives during President Obama's two terms.Before her work at the EPA, McCabe was executive director of Improving Kids' Environment, Inc., an environmental health advocacy organization focused on children's health. From 1993 to 2005, McCabe held various leadership roles in the Indiana Department of Environmental Management's Office of Air Quality, including assistant commissioner for that office.Before to moving to Indianapolis in 1993, McCabe was assistant attorney general for environmental protection in Massachusetts and the assistant secretary for environmental impact review, managing Massachusetts' environmental review statute. She began her legal career clerking for Justice Neil L. Lynch of the Massachusetts Supreme Judicial Court.She was raised in Washington, D.C., and attended Harvard College and Harvard Law School.RSVP below to attend.

title: Open Mic Night at The Comedy Attic
location: The Comedy Attic,123 S Walnut St, Bloomington, IN 47408, USA
description:
Every Wednesday at 8PM is Comedy Attic Open Mic Night. Bloomington has one of the best local comedy scenes in the country, and it all starts with our open mic night!

Each comedian is asked to do no more than five minutes of completely original material. While we do not care about foul language in your set, we do not allow racist, homophobic, transphobic or rape/female bashing jokes. If you have to ask if it's racist, homophobic, transphobic or a rape joke, it probably is.

Comedians that wish to participate are asked to e-mail comedyattic@gmail.com mailto:comedyattic@gmail.com . Newcomers and any participants who are not paid professionals will be required to bring audience members to see them. This is non-negotiable. So if you plan on signing up, be sure to invite as many friends as you can. Good luck!

title: Local News Day
description:
Local News Day - A nonpartisan day of action supporting local journalism.

Sponsored by Automattic, Montana Free Press, Press Forward, American Journalism Project, Newspack, and WordPress.com.

Nearly 3,500 U.S. newspapers have disappeared since 2005, leaving 1 in 4 Americans with limited access to local print news. Local News Day was created by John S. Adams, investigative reporter and founder of Montana Free Press, to connect people with trusted local outlets.

Ways to get involved: partner with Local News Day, amplify the impact with your voice, add your newsroom to the Newsroom Locator Tool.

Learn more: https://localnewsday.com

title: Homeschool Day: Map Making
location: SIDNEY AND LOIS ESKENAZI MUSEUM OF ART
description: Students will explore paintings of landscapes and landmarks in the galleries, then imagine themselves as travelers journeying to new lands. Back in the studio, we'll ask questions about territories, terrains, and landmarks, as we design and create our own imaginative maps.Know Before You Go:  Meet us on the 1st floor of the museum at 10 a.m. Together, we'll drop off personal items in the Simpson Center for Education before exploring the galleries. After seeing artworks that will inspire our project, we'll head back to the Roehm Family Art-Making Studio to create our own masterpieces! Don't forget to dress for a mess!  Strollers are welcome. Chaperones required. We ask that chaperones be 18 or older, with one adult for every 6 children.Parking Information:  The museum is in the pedestrian district of campus with several parking options nearby. The Indiana Memorial Union lot (900 E. 7th Street) is just a 5-minute walk diagonal to the museum, and visitors can receive 40% off parking validation at the museum's guest services desk. The East Garage (105 N. Eagleson Ave.) and Fee Lane Garage (709 N. Fee Lane) are also convenient, about an 8–10 minute walk. Learn more about parking on the Bloomington campus here (https://parking.indiana.edu/facilities/index.html).

title: CACR Speaker Series talk | Ben Zhao-Professor of Computer Science at University of Chicago.
location: LOWELL E. BAIER HALL/IU MAURER SCHOOL OF LAW - Room Number: 335
description: Title: TBA  Abstract: TBA  Ben Zhao is Neubauer Professor of Computer Science at University of Chicago. He completed his Ph.D. at U.C. Berkeley (2004), and B.S. from Yale (1997). He is a Fellow of the ACM, TED/AI speaker, and a recipient of the NSF CAREER award, MIT Technology Review's TR-35 Award (Young Innovators Under 35), TIME100/AI (100 Influential people in AI), USENIX Internet Defense Prize, Concept Art Association's Community Impact Award, ComputerWorld Magazine's Top 40 Technology Innovators award, IEEE ITC Early Career Award, and Faculty awards from Google, Amazon, and Facebook. His work has been covered by many media outlets including New York Times, CNN, NBC, ABC, BBC, MIT Tech Review, Wall Street Journal, Forbes, and New Scientist. His current research focuses on mitigating the harms of misused AI. He has published over 190 articles in areas of security and privacy, machine learning, networking, and HCI. He served as TPC (co-)chair for the World Wide Web conference (WWW 2016) and ACM Internet Measurement Conference (IMC 2018), and on the Steering Committee of IEEE Conference on Secure and Trustworthy Machine Learning (SaTML).
source: IU Kelley School of Business, IU Maurer School of Law

title: What Really was the Original Understanding of Freedom of the Press and Speech?
location: LOWELL E. BAIER HALL/IU MAURER SCHOOL OF LAW - Room Number: 125
description: Professor David Forte of Cleveland State University College of Law, joined by Professor Joseph Tomain, will discuss the origins of the protections the First Amendment affords the freedom of the press and speech. For generations, scholars have been exercised over whether the First Amendment forbids laws criminalizing seditious libel–that is, laws that punish criticism of the government. But the First Amendment meant much more than that issue to the Framers. When we go back to the first principles underlying freedom of speech, we can at last determine what the Framers meant to protect and what they would have allowed the government to regulate. Join us for a conversation on the first principles of the freedom of speech and press, and the future of the First Amendment.

Reproductive Rights Expo
description:
The Theta Chapter of Sigma Lambda Beta International Fraternity Inc. is planning a reproductive rights expo, a small-scale, interactive event designed to educate and engage the university community on critical issues affecting women’s rights, gender equity, and reproductive choice. The event will feature themed tables staffed by volunteers or organizational representatives, where attendees can explore topics, ask questions, and participate in hands-on activities. Think of it as a college-style fair focused on women’s issues, with each table serving as a mini-workshop or information hub. It will not be a protest nor a political demonstration, rather it will be an informational event. There will also be a philanthropic element, as one of the tables will be constructing care packages (containing pads, soap, toothbrush/toothpaste, socks, gloves, protein bars, water, nuts, trail mix, etc.) which will be donated to Middle Way House.

Hosted by: Sigma Lambda Beta Fraternity, Inc.

title: SILC BridgeWork Series - "Impact and Action"
description:
Join us for the Student Involvement and Leadership Center’s Bridgework Series, a monthly leadership and organizational development series designed to help students strengthen their team-building skills, understand group dynamics, and become agents of change within their organizations and communities. Each session will center around a key aspect of how groups work—from communication and collaboration to culture-building and creating sustainable impact—through interactive activities and real-world leadership scenarios.
This 8-event series (spanning the academic year) provides an engaging space for students to connect across campus, learn the foundations of effective teamwork, and explore how they can contribute to meaningful change.
Whether you're leading a student org, supporting a team, or just getting started, Bridgework offers practical tools and powerful insights to help you build better bridges—between people, ideas, and action.

📅 April 9th, 2026⏰ 6:00 PM – 8:00 PM📍 IMU Frangipani Room
💡 Leadership isn’t just about ideas—it’s about turning vision into action. In "Impact and Action," the final event in the Bridgework Series, we'll wrap up our exploration of the Group Dynamics domain of the Student Leadership Competencies, equipping you with the strategies 🌍 to lead with purpose, drive meaningful change, and ensure your actions leave a lasting impact on your organization and community.
🔥 Leadership is about making a difference—let’s explore how to move from intention to impact!
All Indiana University events, groups, activities, and educational, cultural, and historical observances welcome all IU students and other members of the Indiana University community.

title: The Shadow Scholars w/filmmaker | In Light Human Rights Documentary Film Festival
description: In The Shadow Scholars, Oxford's youngest Black professor, Patricia Kingori, delves into a multibillion-dollar underground industry where 40,000 educated Kenyans ghostwrite essays for students often in American, British, or Australian institutions. Filmed across three continents amid crackdowns and AI threats, it probes into the colonization of knowledge, racial inequities, and the exploitation of African labor. [97 min; documentary; English]A Q&A with filmmaker Eloïse King and IU Museum of Archaeology and Anthropology Director Brandie Macdonald will follow the screening.

title: Voice Studio Recital – Students of Paulina Villarreal
Below the Clouds/Sotto le novole w/Q&A | In Light Human Rights Documentary Film Festival
description: In Below the Clouds, Gianfranco Rosi's monumental black-and-white documentary captures Naples' hidden, unglamorous side—focusing on everyday lives, municipal routines, and subterranean historical layers shadowed by Vesuvius and the simmering Phlegraean Fields. Amid quakes and fumaroles, archaeologists excavate ancient ruins, firefighters calm fears, law enforcement hunts tomb robbers, and locals—from worshippers at Madonna dell'Arco to Japanese diggers at Villa Augustea—navigate daily life. Blending past and present, the film evokes a volatile time machine of history, anxiety, and resilience. Winner of the Special Jury Prize at the Venice Film Festival. [115 min; documentary; Italian with English subtitles]A Q&A will follow the screening.

title: 1984 - Radio Play
location: Brown County Playhouse, 70 S. Van Buren St, Nashville, IN, 47448, United States
description:
In a world where every thought is monitored and the Party controls truth itself, Winston Smith dares to question the system. When forbidden love awakens him to the possibility of freedom, he risks everything in a chilling battle against a regime where even thinking differently is a crime.

Disclaimer: This production explores themes of government control, surveillance, and personal freedom within a fictional world. While it addresses sensitive political subject matter, it is not intended as commentary on current events, political parties, or today’s social climate. Its purpose is to tell a compelling story and encourage thoughtful reflection, not to promote a particular political viewpoint.

Tickets - Friday, April 10, 7:30pm

Tickets - Saturday, April 11, 7:30pm

title: 2nd Weekend Work Day Trail Building
description:
Knobstone Hiking Trail Association of Indiana Meetup
Would you to spend a day in some beautiful woods with a delightful group of people building a connecting section of Indiana’s premier long distance hiking trail? Come join us as we continue work on the Tecumseh Trail extension portion of the KHT in MMSF. We will be building the new trail including clipping green briar.
Please bring a lunch and water. We will have large hand tools, but please bring any small tools you have such as clippers or a pruning saw. And a backpack is handy to carry everything in. Instructions will be provided for those who need them. We finish about 3:30.
Meet at the Hatfield Ridge Road gate. The gate is locked but we have a key. Go to the big sign on Rosenbaum/Catholic Cemetry Rd.
Join us on our website at http://www.knobstonehikingtrail.org/, on Facebook at [www.facebook.com/groups/2255586117953484](http://www.facebook.com/groups/2255586117953484), and Substack [https://khta.substack.com/](https://khta.substack.com/)

title: SILC Leadership Compass Series - "Learning and Reasoning"
description:
Join us for the Student Involvement and Leadership Center's Leadership Compass Series, a monthly leadership development series designed to help students grow across the 8 Student Leadership Competency domains. Each session will focus on one core leadership area—ranging from self-awareness to civic responsibility—through interactive workshops and hands-on activities. This 8-workshop series (spanning the academic year) provides a dynamic space for students to build confidence, sharpen their skills, and connect with peers who are passionate about personal and community growth.
Whether you're new to leadership or already involved on campus, the SILC Leadership Compass offers practical tools and meaningful conversations to elevate your leadership journey—one domain at a time.

***Lunch is provided for participants from 12:00pm-1:00pm on all dates***

📅 April 11th, 2026⏰ 10:00 AM – 3:00 PM📍 IMU Club Hub
💡 Strong leadership requires adaptability—but how do leaders make informed decisions and continuously grow? In "Learning and Reasoning," the final event in the Leadership Compass Series, we’ll explore the Learning and Reasoning domain of the Student Leadership Competencies, equipping you with the tools 🧠 to think critically, evaluate information, and approach problem-solving with confidence and clarity.
🔥 Great leaders never stop learning—let’s refine how you process information and make strategic decisions!
All Indiana University events, groups, activities, and educational, cultural, and historical observances welcome all IU students and other members of the Indiana University community.

title: Rubber Duck Race 2026
description:
Rubber Duck Race! All funds will be distributed equally among two local humane societies and the Mu Chapter of APO. These organizations are dedicated to supporting animal welfare in Brown and Monroe Country. For every dollar, purchase one duck to be raced at during the Rubber Duck Race!
At the race, we will hear from local organizations addressing animal welfare with IU student organizations, play lawn games, do fun activities, buy merch, and of course, race ducks! This event is open and free to all!
The Mu Chapter of Alpha Phi Omega at Indiana University has had a long tradition of conducting a spring service project that gives back to our community and develops these pillars in our chapter and campus. Alpha Phi Omega is a national co-ed service fraternity dedicated to the pillars of leadership, friendship, and service. In the spring, we hold an event called the Rubber Duck Race. At this race, donors can purchase a rubber duck for one dollar to be thrown into the Campus River by the Indiana Memorial Union. We additionally have food and tables for local student and community organizations to do outreach.
In 2025, all funds raised will go directly to supporting efforts to reduce food insecurity in Monroe County. Specifically to two local organizations; Brown County Humane Society and Monroe County Humane Association. We are hoping that by opening the opportunity to the larger IU and Bloomington community, we can engage citizens and students in the cause of food insecurity. Join us in supporting the local Bloomington and IU community with a donation today!

Hosted by: Alpha Phi Omega

Additional Information can be found at: https://beinvolved.indiana.edu/event/12282148

title: Color Drawing and Painting Workshop
location: SIDNEY AND LOIS ESKENAZI MUSEUM OF ART - Room Number: Check in with Guest Services on the first floor
description: Discover the joy of drawing in an inspiring setting! Begin by sketching directly from artwork in the collection to sharpen your eye and strengthen your skills. Then, move into the studio to learn about color theory and add color to your artwork.Strengthen fundamental drawing skills  Explore color theory in action  Experiment with color media  Expand your creative expression in a hands-on way  Reserve your spot. If this page says, "Registration has closed" that means the waitlist is full.SuppliesBring your favorite drawing pencil (e.g. 4B), a sketchbook or drawing paper, and a phone or device to photograph artwork (without flash) as we sketch. Additional supplies are provided.  Know Before You GoArrive early to find parking, check in, and get your drawing supplies ready.  At 1:55 p.m. any unclaimed reservations will be released to guests who are standing by waiting for a spot.  Where to find us, where to park, accessibility, and museum guidelines (https://artmuseum.indiana.edu/visit/your-visit.html).  This workshop is recommended for ages 12 and up. Anyone under 18, who is not an IU student, must be accompanied by an adult.  When You ArriveCheck in with Guest Services at the information desk on the first floor, even if you come often and even if you are late. We appreciate having an accurate attendance count.  Store all backpacks and large bags before entering the galleries. A small handbag may be carried at your side.  Get your supplies ready. Attach an 18x24" piece of newsprint to a drawing board.  Cancellations  This workshop often has a waitlist. Please use the cancellation link included with your confirmation email if your plans change.About the Instructor  John Cheek has taught credit and non-credit classes for eight years in portrait painting, landscape painting, palette knife painting, life drawing, beginning drawing, and advanced drawing, as well as in design. He volunteers his time and expertise to facilitate drawing workshops at the Eskenazi Museum of Art.
source: Eskenazi Museum of Art, IU Arts & Humanities Institute

title: Migratory Bird Feeders
location: Allison Jukebox Community Center, 351 S Washington St, Bloomington, IN 47401, USA
description:
This hands-on workshop has a registration fee, and pre-registration is required.
Register online by April 8 at https://webtrac.bloomington.in.gov/webtrac/web/search.html?Primarycode=140010
$15/in-city, $17/non-city. For all ages.

Help support our winged friends as the great northern bird migration peaks the first of May. In this program, we will talk about some of the more unique bird species that will be moving through the area, before constructing bird feeders to support them in their travels north. All materials are provided. Come with a constructive, hands-on attitude, and leave with a one-of-a-kind bird feeder. Cost is per project, and up to two people can work on a project together. Only one person needs to register per pair. Be sure to register for the number of feeders you would like to create.

title: Black Snow w/filmmaker | In Light Human Rights Documentary Film Festival
description: Siberian homemaker-turned-journalist Natalia Zubkova uncovers a toxic mine fire poisoning her remote coal town's homes with deadly gases. As her viral reporting exposes government corruption and the coal mafia, she faces surveillance, harassment, and threats from an authoritarian regime. This courageous mother battles for environmental justice in a polluted world of black snow. [96 min; documentary; Russian with English subtitles]A Zoom Q&A with filmmaker Alina Simone and Dr. Andy Bruno (Stephen F. Cohen Chair of Russian History, IU Dept. of History) will follow the screening.

title: AsianFest 2026 Mosaic Traditions: Piecing a Common Ground
description: The Asian Culture Center In collaboration with the City of Bloomington Community and Family Resources Department presents 2026 Asianfest, an annual campus-community partnership featuring cultural and information tables, performances, and video clips/slides about Asian American and Pacific Islander heritage.   Mosaic Traditions: Piecing a Common Ground highlights the enduring legacy of asian art forms evolving through generations.      The tales we tell and the stories we share all bear our past, present, and future—across time and space.  And thus, we meet together, where cuisines merge in fusion, attire becomes fashion, music becomes memories, and trends become traditions.      We all possess both ancestral wisdom and foresight that converge in the present, reminding us to learn from the past while remaining prudent in facing the future.       Welcome to AsianFest 2026, where we celebrate our shared histories and unique heritages. Through this year's theme, Mosaic Traditions: Piecing a Common Ground, we invite one and all to find peace on common ground.    All Indiana University events, groups, activities, and educational, cultural, and historical observances welcome all IU students and other members of the Indiana University community.For more information, view this event in BeInvolved (https://beinvolved.indiana.edu/event/12011874).

How Deep is Your Love w/filmmaker | In Light Human Rights Documentary Film Festival
description: In How Deep Is Your Love, marine biologists venture into Earth's last wilderness—the deep sea—to study "alien-like" creatures and ancient ecosystems thriving in darkness. As they race to map undiscovered species, the film reveals the urgent threat of deep-sea mining, poised to devastate these fragile habitats. Blending awe-inspiring exploration with a call to action, it questions humanity's commitment to preserving an unseen and mysterious world. [101 min; documentary; English]A Q&A will follow the screening with filmmaker Eileen Mortimer, Dr. Teresa Kovacs (Germanic Studies; Theatre, Drama and Contemporary Dance), and Dr. R. Andrés Guzmán (Spanish and Portuguese; Cultural Studies Program) will follow the screening.

title: 1984 - Radio Play
location: Brown County Playhouse, 70 S. Van Buren St, Nashville, IN, 47448, United States
description:
In a world where every thought is monitored and the Party controls truth itself, Winston Smith dares to question the system. When forbidden love awakens him to the possibility of freedom, he risks everything in a chilling battle against a regime where even thinking differently is a crime.

Disclaimer: This production explores themes of government control, surveillance, and personal freedom within a fictional world. While it addresses sensitive political subject matter, it is not intended as commentary on current events, political parties, or today’s social climate. Its purpose is to tell a compelling story and encourage thoughtful reflection, not to promote a particular political viewpoint.

BYOB @ IU welcomes all students to showcase their businesses at our bi-monthly events, currently held at Dunn Meadow or indoors in the IMU. While sellers must be IU students, our events are open to the wider Bloomington community. You’ll find a diverse range of student-run businesses offering everything from vintage clothing and jewelry to art prints, plants, and crochet items. Set up your table, set your prices, and engage with various shoppers.
Our events highlight the creativity and entrepreneurial spirit of our members while providing invaluable real-world experience in marketing, negotiating, and customer interaction. BYOB @ IU is more than just an organization—it's a movement dedicated to empowering the next generation of entrepreneurs, helping them bring their ideas to life and connect with a broader audience.

title: Basquiat - Black and White Version | Art and a Movie
description: No painter electrified the 1980s art scene like Jean-Michel Basquiat, a New York street kid whose visionary blending of expressionism, pop art, and the radical new aesthetics of hip-hop and graffiti propelled him to international fame before a tragic early demise. Julian Schnabel's stunning debut charts Basquiat's dizzying rise and fall with an insider's eye for authentic details of the downtown arts demimonde as well as a fellow postmodernist's interest in the dream-like and surreal. Jeffrey Wright's brilliant introductory performance provides trenchant insight into the artist's creative passions and personal demons. Cemented by David Bowie as a spot-on Andy Warhol, Basquiat's supporting cast is a murderers' row of Hollywood legends and art lovers (Dennis Hopper, Gary Oldman, Benicio del Toro, Parker Posey, Christopher Walken, Willem Dafoe) lending their talents to one of the high points of '90s independent cinema. [107 min; drama, biography; English]  After the screening, join Eskenazi Museum Arts Events Coordinator Jada Collins for a guided tour of the Museum's Icons & Identity program. The tour begins in the museum's Martha and David Moore Prints, Drawings, and Photographs Study, with a special viewing of a selection of Andy Warhol prints focused on portraiture. Guests are invited to stay for an optional art-making activity, creating their own Pop Art self-portraits in the Roehm Family Art-making Studio. Those who are interested in participating should meet Jada outside the Cinema after the screening.

title: Family Art Lab: America 250
location: SIDNEY AND LOIS ESKENAZI MUSEUM OF ART - Room Number: Roehm Family Art-making Studio
description: Join us this month to celebrate America's 250th Anniversary as we look at American artists and explore the theme "What America Means to Me."  Designed for K-12 students and their families, this event offers a fun and flexible way to spark creativity and connect with each other. All ages and experience levels are welcome!  Come and go at your own pace. We recommend planning for at least an hour to explore the gallery and make art.  Start in the Art Studio inside the Kimberly and John Simpson Center for Education on the museum's 2nd floor to pick up your Gallery Challenge. Then head to the galleries to find the artwork we'll use as inspiration for our project! When you're ready, head back to the studio for our hands-on art-making activity that everyone will love.  This program takes place every Second Sunday of the month, excluding holidays and museum closures. Each month features new artworks and materials, so there's always something fresh to explore and create!  All children must be chaperoned.Know Before You Go:Read about where to find us, where to park, accessibility, and museum guidelines.Strollers are welcome in the galleries. When carrying small children, please keep them in front of you.  To save time at the lockers, avoid bringing backpacks or large bags.  When You Arrive:If you parked in the IMU parking lot, Guest Services can validate your parking voucher for a discount.  Store any large bags in the lockers behind the Guest Services desk.  Head upstairs to the 2nd Floor and into the Simpson Center for Education to start.  When in the galleries, do not touch the artwork. Be mindful to keep body parts and anything you're carrying at least 18" away from the art.  Photography is welcome! Just no flash or selfie sticks.

title: The DaVinci Code- Live on Stage
location: Waldron Auditorium,112 South Walnut Street, Bloomington, IN 47404, USA
description:
Constellation Stage & Screen presents “The DaVinci Code- Live on Stage,” on stage March 26 - April 12 at the Waldron Auditorium.

Dan Brown’s international best-seller comes to life on stage in this thrilling, high-stakes adventure! When the curator of the Louvre is found murdered under mysterious circumstances, symbologist Robert Langdon and cryptologist Sophie Neveu are thrust into an investigation that quickly unravels into a deadly conspiracy. As they follow a trail of cryptic puzzles and ancient codes, they discover a secret that could change the course of history. Packed with pulse-pounding suspense and stunning visuals, The Da Vinci Code is an exhilarating journey into the unknown. Recommended for ages 13+.

See Constellation’s website for more information, including a detailed performance schedule. Learn more and find tickets at seeconstellation.org/mainstage/da-vinci-code/ https://seeconstellation.org/mainstage/da-vinci-code/ https://seeconstellation.org/mainstage/da-vinci-code/

title: 1984 - Radio Play
location: Brown County Playhouse, 70 S. Van Buren St, Nashville, IN, 47448, United States
description:
In a world where every thought is monitored and the Party controls truth itself, Winston Smith dares to question the system. When forbidden love awakens him to the possibility of freedom, he risks everything in a chilling battle against a regime where even thinking differently is a crime.

Disclaimer: This production explores themes of government control, surveillance, and personal freedom within a fictional world. While it addresses sensitive political subject matter, it is not intended as commentary on current events, political parties, or today’s social climate. Its purpose is to tell a compelling story and encourage thoughtful reflection, not to promote a particular political viewpoint.

title: Plato's Closet presents the 17th Annual Trashion Refashion Show!
location: Buskirk-Chumley Theater,114 East Kirkwood Avenue, Bloomington, IN 47408, USA
description:
The 17th Annual Trashion Refashion Runway Show, presented by Plato’s Closet, brings eco-conscious couture back to the Buskirk-Chumley Theater on Sunday, April 12, 2026, at 7 p.m. (doors open at 6:30 p.m.). This beloved event blends high fashion with sustainability, showcasing over 50 original, upcycled designs modeled by a diverse cast of community members.A highlight of Bloomington’s arts scene since 2010, the show promotes environmental sustainability and artistic creativity while challenging conventional notions about beauty and consumerism. Each of the juried show’s designs consists of at least 90% discarded materials, either rescued from the waste stream (Trashion) or transformed from underutilized garments (Refashion). The show is also committed to cultivating diversity among its models with regard to gender, age, race, body type and other categories.

This fundraising event to support the Center for Sustainable Living features locally made couture sure to inspire the fashion creatives lurking inside us all.

Tickets are $22 in advance, $24 day-of, and $10 for children/students—available at the BCT box office or online:buskirkchumley.org/events/2026-trashion-refashion-runway-show-april-12-2026/ https://buskirkchumley.org/events/2026-trashion-refashion-runway-show-april-12-2026/

Proudly sponsored by: Plato’s Closet, Bikesmiths, Hickory Audiology, Friends of Gail Kincaid, Alibi Boutique, and Olympus Properties.

For event details and updates, visit http://bloomingtontrashion.org.

title: Let Them Make Contracts: Rehabilitating Lochner
location: LOWELL E. BAIER HALL/IU MAURER SCHOOL OF LAW - Room Number: 125
description: Professor David Bernstein of George Mason University's Antonin Scalia Law School, joined by Professor Steve Sanders, will discuss the rise, fall, and influence of Lochner. How far should the liberty of contract extend against the police power of the State? What is the role of courts in protecting that liberty? How did Lochner influence judicial recognition of individual rights in the 20th century? Was Lochner truly an aberration from liberty of contract doctrine? Join us for a conversation on the history, merits, pitfalls, and impact of Lochner in the American constitutional scheme.  Noodles & Company will be provided for all attendees!

title: Spring 2026 Colloquium Series with Anke Birkenmaier
description: The Center for Latin American and Caribbean Studies is proud to present the continuation of its Colloquium Series for Spring 2026.  We welcome Dr. Anke Birkenmaier as she presents Circulating Latin America: Ethical Interventions from the Global South.     Recent Latin American literature is circulating widely in translation, through independent and mainstream publishers, and is winning international awards. This presentation focuses on two novels, Karla Suarez's Objetos perdidos and Claudio Piñeiro's Elena sabe, to assess both the formal strategies and the ethical import of recent writing by Latin American writers, and the ways in which they build bridges between local and global political engagement.Anke Birkenmaier is currently serving as chair of the Spanish and Portuguese department in the College of Arts and Sciences at Indiana University. Birkenmaier is a scholar of modern Caribbean and Latin American literature and culture, and in studying these areas she has two major foci. She studies literature in relation to other discourses such as anthropology, looking at the ways in which ideas about culture and race have evolved over time. Her interest in media and sound studies has also led her to explore the ways in which the novel has entered in competition with other communication media.     The CLACS Spring 2026 Colloquium meets Mondays from February through April, with faculty and guest speakers engaging Latin America and the Caribbean through research on history, culture, language, politics, and the arts. Check the CLACS Calendar (https://events.iu.edu/clacs/) for details.
source: IU Hamilton Lugar School, IU La Casa Latino Cultural Center

title: Jazz @ the BCT: Natalie Boeyink Jazz Ensemble; Plummer Jazz Group – Greg Ward, director
description: About the DirectorsNatalie Boeyink, bassist and educator, serves as associate professor of music jazz studies at the Indiana University Jacobs School of Music. After earning her doctorate in music education at the Jacobs School in 2015, Boeyink spent eight years as a jazz lecturer and senior lecturer in the University of North Carolina system. She earned an M.M. in Jazz Performance at the University of Louisville and a B.M. in Jazz Studies at Jacobs. Her interest in the educational experiences and careers of jazzwomen has launched a series of research projects, conference presentations and publications, including her dissertation, "An Analysis and Description of Female Jazz Instrumentalists." An advocate for jazzwomen, Boeyink served as chair of the Jazz Education Network's Women in Jazz Committee and worked to reestablish the Sisters in Jazz Collegiate Combo Competition. Batuquê Trio is Boeyink's seasoned collaboration with drummer Andy Smith and pianist Jamaal Baptiste. Focusing on contemporary Brazilian, Afro-Cuban, and Caribbean jazz, the trio frequently appears at jazz festivals as performers and clinicians. Their first CD, Transparency, was released in 2015. A second album is forthcoming in 2024. Some of Boeyink's notable performances include appearances with Tammy McCann, Jamie Baum, Ingrid Jensen, Terri Lyne Carrington, Christine Jensen, Leni Stern, Jovino Santos Neto, Joe Piscopo, David "Fathead" Newman, Jon Hendricks, Jeff Coffin, David Liebman, and Lorraine Feather.Greg Ward is associate professor of music in jazz studies at the Indiana University Jacobs School of Music. A saxophonist and composer born in Peoria, Illinois, Ward has performed and recorded with a varied group of artists, such as Prefuse 73, Lupe Fiasco, Tortoise, William Parker, Makaya McCraven, Linda Oh, and Mike Reed. As a bandleader, Ward has produced four recordings, including Fitted Shards: South Side Story, Phonic Juggernaut, Touch My Beloved's Thought, and Rogue Parade: Stomping Off from Greenwood. As a composer, he has received commissions from the Jazz Gallery in New York City, Chicago Jazz Institute, City of Chicago's Made in Chicago: World Class Jazz Series, and the Peoria Ballet Company. He was awarded the New Music USA Van Lier Fellowship in 2012 and an Individual Artists Program grant by the Chicago Department of Cultural Affairs and Special Events in 2017. Ward maintains an active international touring schedule with various ensembles.

description:
Recovery Ally Training is an interactive educational workshop designed to increase awareness, reduce stigma, and build practical skills to support individuals in recovery from substance use disorders. Participants will learn about recovery pathways, common challenges faced by people in recovery, and how language, policies, and everyday interactions can either support or hinder recovery efforts.
This training emphasizes empathy, inclusivity, and actionable strategies, empowering participants to serve as visible, informed allies within their communities, workplaces, or campuses. By the end of the training, attendees will have a stronger understanding of recovery-oriented practices and how to create safer, more supportive environments for people in recovery.

title: Guest Music Theory Lecture – Elizabeth Medina-Gray (Ithaca College)
description: Five Friends Master Class Series – Honoring Robert SamelsThe Five Friends Master Class Series honoring the lives of five talented Jacobs School of Music students—Chris Carducci, Garth Eppley, Georgina Joshi, Zachary Novak, and Robert Samels—was established in 2012 with a gift of $1 million from the Georgina Joshi Foundation, Inc. This annual series of lectures, master classes, and residencies by a number of the world's leading musicians and teachers focuses on areas of interest most relevant to the lives of the five friends—voice performance, choral conducting, early music, music theory, composition, and opera. The Georgina Joshi Foundation was established in 2007 as the vision of Georgina Joshi's mother, Louise Addicott-Joshi, to provide educational and career development opportunities for young musicians and to encourage and support public performance of music. The gift to the school establishes a permanent way for the world to learn about each of the five friends, their musical talents and passions, and to encourage the development of similar talents and passions in current and future music students. The establishment of this endowment by the families is administered by the IU Foundation.  Bass-baritone and composer Robert Samels was born on June 2, 1981, and died in a plane crash on April 20, 2006. He was a doctoral student in choral conducting at the Indiana University Jacobs School of Music and had studied voice with Giorgio Tozzi and Costanza Cuccaro. He began his vocal studies with Alfred Anderson at the University of Akron and Andreas Poulimenos at Bowling Green State University. Samels had recently appeared as Mr. Gibbs in the world premiere of Our Town by Ned Rorem, as Marco in the collegiate premiere of William Bolcom's A View from the Bridge, and as Joseph and Herod in the collegiate premiere of El Nino by John Adams. In September 2005, he conducted the premiere of his own opera, Pilatvs. As a member of the Wolf Trap Opera Company for 2006, he would have added three roles that summer, including Bartolo in Le Nozze di Figaro, Friar Laurence in Roméo et Juliette, and Pluto in Telemann's Orpheus. Other opera credits included the title roles of Don Pasquale and Il Turco in Italia, as well as Leporello in Don Giovanni, Falstaff in TheMerry Wives of Windsor, and Bottom in A Midsummer Night's Dream. In the summer of 2004, he performed Creon in the New York premiere of John Eaton's Antigone. Samels also frequently performed in the oratorio repertoire. In the spring of 2005, he was selected as a semi-finalist in the annual competition of the Oratorio Society of New York. He was an announcer with public radio station WFIU, as well as the host and producer of its Cantabile program. A soloist with Aguavá New Music Studio, he had recently performed a concert at the Library of Congress. Samels was an associate instructor in the Jacobs School's Music Theory Department, where was loved and admired by his students.

title: A Conversation with Caleb Hearon
description:
Union Board, in collaboration with the IU Arts and Humanities Council, is excited to announce a Conversation with Caleb Hearon as part of the yearly Granfalloon festival.
Purchase a ticket through this link:https://www.iuauditorium.com/events/detail/calebhearon-2026
Students can reserve up to one free ticket.
Caleb Hearon is a comedian, writer, and actor known for his clever humor and commentary. He is the host of the popular podcast So True with Caleb Hearon, where he combines personal storytelling with comedic insight, and he has built a national reputation through stand-up performances, television writing, and live shows.
Originally from the Midwest, Hearon began performing comedy at a young age and has since appeared at major comedy festivals while touring extensively across the United States. He also recently came out with his first HBO comedy special, which is deeply personal and comments on identity, grief, and growing up queer in the Midwest.
This event will be in partnership with the IU Arts and Humanities Council to kick off and promote Granfalloon 2025:
Presented by the IU Arts and Humanities Council and inspired by legendary Hoosier author Kurt Vonnegut Jr., Granfalloon brings together musicians, artists, thinkers, and good people from all walks of life for a celebration of art, ideas, and community.
The festival highlights the creative spirit of our region by tapping into the enduring ethos of Vonnegut’s life and work: promoting civic engagement, encouraging independent thought, and championing art and creativity as essential to the spiritual life of individuals and the health of our democracy.

Hosted by: Indiana Memorial Union Board

Additional Information can be found at: https://beinvolved.indiana.edu/event/12145323

description:
Tuesday, April 14
7pm – Doors open at 6:30
Lotus Firebay
105 S Rogers

About Cécilia
Cécilia is a trio of fine Canadian musicians — Timi Turmel on accordion, Erin Leahy on piano, and Louis Schryer on fiddle. They bring fresh spirit to Quebecois and Celtic traditions that have been part of Canadian music for generations. Individually, they have cred for miles: Timi was Quebecois Champion on accordion three years running; with her siblings in the band LEAHY, Erin is a multiple JUNO Award winner; and Louis is the only eight-time Canadian Fiddle Champion (and a four-time Grand Masters Fiddle Champion). As Cécilia, they invite audiences to share in the unmistakable, irresistible vitality of their music and culture.

title: Concert Band – Tiffany J. Galus, conductor
description: Repertoire  Alexandra Gardner: Time Unfolding (2024, consortium premiere)Bonds/Ehrlich: Spiritual Suite (1962)Benson: Solitary Dancer (1966)About the ConductorTiffany J. Galus is assistant professor of music in bands and director of the Marching Hundred at the Indiana University Jacobs School of Music. Previously, she was assistant director of bands at The University of Texas at Austin, where she served as assistant director of the Longhorn Band, conducted the university concert and athletic bands, and taught courses in undergraduate conducting and wind band literature. She has also served as a public-school band director in northern Indiana. Under her direction, the program received consistent superior ratings at band festivals, including two state titles (2014). In 2013, she was a quarterfinalist for the first Music Educator Grammy Award. With a comprehensive background in the arts as an educator, conductor, performer, producer, arranger, editor, and media creator, Galus has led a multifaceted career, serving a variety of arts communities. At The University of Texas, she established the UTWE Live! livestream series, which reached over a half-million viewers between 2018 and 2020. She also is cofounder and owner of Keep Making Music, an ensemble media creation company based in Austin, Texas, that produced virtual performances for ensembles nationwide, including a virtual production of Mozart's Die Zauberflöte. A freelance transcriber, editor, and arranger, she has written for wind ensembles, marching/pep bands, show choir, and chamber ensembles. Her major works include a wind band transcription of John Adams' Saxophone Concerto and Charles Ives' Variations on America, critical editions of Fred Junkin's setting of Respighi's Fountains of Rome, HR Kent/Mayhew Lake's setting of Overture to "La Forza del Destino," and Michael Martin's Lontano. Galus has served on the staff for Ultimate Drill Book, Blue Lake Fine Arts Camp, Rhythmic Force Indoor Percussion, Madison Scouts Drum and Bugle Corps, Blue Stars Drum and Bugle Corps, and the National Youth Wind Ensemble. She holds memberships in the College Band Directors National Association, Texas Music Educators Association, Indiana Bandmasters Association, National Band Association, National Association for Music Education, and Kappa Psi, and is an honorary sister of Tau Beta Sigma.

description: Bio  Mahdi Taraghi is a third-year Ph.D. student in Environmental Science at the O'Neill School of Public and Environmental Affairs, Indiana University Bloomington. He holds a Master's degree in Water Resources Engineering and is passionate about the intersection of environmental policy, socio-environmental systems, and complex systems modeling. title: Music Theory Colloquium Series: Guest Lecture – Elizabeth Medina-Gray (Ithaca College)
description: Five Friends Master Class Series – Honoring Robert SamelsThe Five Friends Master Class Series honoring the lives of five talented Jacobs School of Music students—Chris Carducci, Garth Eppley, Georgina Joshi, Zachary Novak, and Robert Samels—was established in 2012 with a gift of $1 million from the Georgina Joshi Foundation, Inc. This annual series of lectures, master classes, and residencies by a number of the world's leading musicians and teachers focuses on areas of interest most relevant to the lives of the five friends—voice performance, choral conducting, early music, music theory, composition, and opera. The Georgina Joshi Foundation was established in 2007 as the vision of Georgina Joshi's mother, Louise Addicott-Joshi, to provide educational and career development opportunities for young musicians and to encourage and support public performance of music. The gift to the school establishes a permanent way for the world to learn about each of the five friends, their musical talents and passions, and to encourage the development of similar talents and passions in current and future music students. The establishment of this endowment by the families is administered by the IU Foundation.  Bass-baritone and composer Robert Samels was born on June 2, 1981, and died in a plane crash on April 20, 2006. He was a doctoral student in choral conducting at the Indiana University Jacobs School of Music and had studied voice with Giorgio Tozzi and Costanza Cuccaro. He began his vocal studies with Alfred Anderson at the University of Akron and Andreas Poulimenos at Bowling Green State University. Samels had recently appeared as Mr. Gibbs in the world premiere of Our Town by Ned Rorem, as Marco in the collegiate premiere of William Bolcom's A View from the Bridge, and as Joseph and Herod in the collegiate premiere of El Nino by John Adams. In September 2005, he conducted the premiere of his own opera, Pilatvs. As a member of the Wolf Trap Opera Company for 2006, he would have added three roles that summer, including Bartolo in Le Nozze di Figaro, Friar Laurence in Roméo et Juliette, and Pluto in Telemann's Orpheus. Other opera credits included the title roles of Don Pasquale and Il Turco in Italia, as well as Leporello in Don Giovanni, Falstaff in TheMerry Wives of Windsor, and Bottom in A Midsummer Night's Dream. In the summer of 2004, he performed Creon in the New York premiere of John Eaton's Antigone. Samels also frequently performed in the oratorio repertoire. In the spring of 2005, he was selected as a semi-finalist in the annual competition of the Oratorio Society of New York. He was an announcer with public radio station WFIU, as well as the host and producer of its Cantabile program. A soloist with Aguavá New Music Studio, he had recently performed a concert at the Library of Congress. Samels was an associate instructor in the Jacobs School's Music Theory Department, where was loved and admired by his students.

title: Mindful Makers
description:
Join us for Mindful Makers, a weekly event built around being creative, meeting new people and feeling more confident in yourself and your wellness. Each week has a theme - come for one or come for all! We're here to have fun and make cool things.
January 21: Semester Vision Boards
Picture your new semester! Create a vision board that will motivate you to achieve this semester. Learn some tips on how to set great goals to help you achieve that vision.

January 28: Meditation and Aromatherapy
Ever been curious about aromatherapy and how it works? Here's your opportunity to learn more! While you're here, we'll also practice deep breathing techniques, progressive relaxation and guided visualization, and other simple techniques that don't require any special skills or equipment

February 4: Crochet the Stress Away
Whether you're a crochet expert or have never picked up a hook in your life, we have a place for you! Join us, learn basic crochet stitches, and talk about ways to have fun on campus and in the community.

February 11: Origami Ornaments and Overcoming
Sometimes life throws us curve balls. In this Mindful Makers, we'll go through the process of folding origami hearts or cranes, and using those to make beautiful ornaments that reflect challenges we've overcome. These cranes carry hope on their wings!

February 18: Air Clay Emotions
What does your angry monster look like? What about your joy? Use air clay to sculpt mini-monsters representing both supportive and challenging emotions, and learn ways of taming your more wild ones. Remember - not all monsters are scary. Sometimes they're pretty great!

February 25: Love Letters to Your Body and Cookie Decorating
Sometimes we need reminders of how great our bodies can be. In this week's MM, you'll learn simple bookbinding skills to make an accordion book, and fill those pages with what we like about ourselves and our bodies. Join our nutritionist, Katie Shepherd, for cookie decorating while you're here!

March 4: Watercolor Your Values
One of the most powerful motivators we have is our personal values. Paint a values portrait, and discuss how you can tie your goals into your values to keep yourself moving forward.

March 11: Sleep Vision Boards
Getting a good night's sleep is critical towards functioning at your best - but truthfully, most of us don't get nearly the quantity or quality of sleep we need. Create a sleep vision board as you learn ways to improve your sleep habits and why sleep is so important!

March 25: Create Your Own Journal
And we do mean from scratch! Learn simple Japanese stitchwork bookbinding to make your own book, decorate it to suit your tastes, and create a journal you can use however you like! (And if you're new to journaling, we can work on that as well!)

April 1: Perler Beads and Mindfulness
Being mindful doesn't take anything special. Sometimes it can be found in the smallest of things - like the little perler beads! Make yourself an ornament while discovering ways mindfulness can be a part of the everyday things you already do!

April 8: Gaming with Friends
Do you know that the oldest known game board is 9000 years old? We've been playing games for a long time! Well, not us personally. We're just going to play for a couple of hours. Come and join us!

April 15: Resinate
No, that's not a typo. Learn the basics of resin crafting and make your own ornaments or magnets. While here, share your favorite hobbies and why they matter to you - maybe you'll inspire someone else!

April 22: Affirmation Jars
At the end of the semester, sometimes we need reminders of what motivates us, makes us special, or just what matters to us. Create your own affirmation jar and fill it up with all those good things. When you're feeling low, pull one out and give yourself a boost!

April 29: Keep Growing
Whether you're looking forward to graduation, a summer off, or one filled with activity, summer is a great time for growth. Share your upcoming opportunities as you decorate a pot and select a new plant to nurture. Like plants, we need care to keep growing, so share your ideas for how you're going to care for yourself!

Hosted by: Health Promotion

Additional Information can be found at: https://beinvolved.indiana.edu/event/11977507

title: Open Mic Night at The Comedy Attic
location: The Comedy Attic,123 S Walnut St, Bloomington, IN 47408, USA
description:
Every Wednesday at 8PM is Comedy Attic Open Mic Night. Bloomington has one of the best local comedy scenes in the country, and it all starts with our open mic night!

Each comedian is asked to do no more than five minutes of completely original material. While we do not care about foul language in your set, we do not allow racist, homophobic, transphobic or rape/female bashing jokes. If you have to ask if it's racist, homophobic, transphobic or a rape joke, it probably is.

Comedians that wish to participate are asked to e-mail comedyattic@gmail.com mailto:comedyattic@gmail.com . Newcomers and any participants who are not paid professionals will be required to bring audience members to see them. This is non-negotiable. So if you plan on signing up, be sure to invite as many friends as you can. Good luck!

title: 21JPSI Speaker Series: "Japan's Caring Capitalism: Business Reinvention and the Economic Importance of Guardrails" (Prof. Ulrike Schaede, University of California - San Diego)
location: SPEA /PUBLIC AND ENVIRONMENTAL AFFAIRS, PAUL H. O'NEILL GRADUATE CENTER - Room Number: Room A221
description: This public event is open to all students, faculty, staff, and members of the community. Please join us! Professor Schaede's prepared remarks will be followed by a discussion/audience Q&A.   About the talk:Japan's Caring Capitalism: Japan's business reinvention and what a street racing manga teaches us about the economic importance of guardrails)  Japan's economy is much stronger than most people think. And in these times of fast-changing technologies and rising geopolitical transitions, Japan's system of slower, more balanced growth offers an attractive alternative to America's increasingly reckless capitalism. Yet, since the collapse of the bubble, Japan has fallen into a cycle of negativity, fueled by benchmarking Japan against what America considers a "good" company or economy. This pessimism has legitimated the introduction of American business practices, almost without modifications. The resulting financialization and short-termism are most visible in the rise of shareholder activism, an innovation system focused on startups and generating unicorns, and increased job mobility.  To some extent, American-style market discipline has helped Japan's ongoing business reinvention and the shift from "Made in Japan" to "Japan Inside", i.e., a strategic pivot into upstream materials and components to become the technology anchor of Northeast Asian supply chains. However, Japan can do better than a naïve adoption of American business practices. Japan presents an alternative system of capitalism that has guardrails against uncertainties and is more caring of society. This system entails different tradeoffs and cost-benefit calculations, and is anchored on more balance. These result in a different type of strong economy, with different processes of innovation.This event is made possible thanks to generous financial support from the Japan Foundation.About the speaker:Ulrike Schaede (ウリケ・シェー) is professor of Japanese business at the UC San Diego School of Global Policy and Strategy (GPS). She is the founding director of the Japan Forum for Innovation and Technology (http://jfit.ucsd.edu/) (JFIT), and the faculty coordinator of the International Management track at GPS.                      Schaede's research focuses on Japanese business organization, strategy and management. Her main interest is to explore Japan's business system and political economy, with the goals to analyze different systems of capitalism and ways of organizing business, and to identify the social and economic efficiency consequences of these differences.                      Her 2020 book, "The Business Reinvention of Japan: How to Make Sense of the New Japan and Why It Matters (https://www.sup.org/books/title/?id=32247)" (Stanford University Press 2020), won the 2021 Masayoshi Ohira Memorial Prize from Japan, as well as the 2021 U.S. Axiom Business Book Award (silver medal, "Economics"). In 2022, it was published in Japanese as 再興 THE KAISHA 日本のビジネス・リインベンション (https://www.amazon.co.jp/-/en/%E3%82%A6%E3%83%AA%E3%82%B1%E3%83%BB%E3%82%B7%E3%82%A7%E3%83%BC%E3%83%87/dp/4296113607/ref=tmm_hrd_swatch_0?_encoding=UTF8&qid=&sr=) by Nikkei BP.                      In 2022, Schaede also published a monograph titled "The Digital Transformation and Japan's Political Economy (https://www.cambridge.org/core/elements/abs/digital-transformation-and-japans-political-economy/282E0769CB887747A42369F4B78973C9)" (with Kay Shimizu), at Cambridge University Press.                      She is also the author of "Choose and Focus: Japanese Business Strategies for the 21st Century (https://www.amazon.com/Choose-Focus-Japanese-Business-Strategies/dp/0801447062/ref=sr_1_1?dchild=1&keywords=schaede+choose+and+focus&qid=1632357699&sr=8-1)" (Cornell UP), and co-author of "Creating Ambidextrous Organizations: Exploration and Exploitation for Overcoming Inertia (https://www.amazon.co.jp/%E4%B8%A1%E5%88%A9%E3%81%8D%E3%81%AE%E7%B5%84%E7%B9%94%E3%82%92%E3%81%A4%E3%81%8F%E3%82%8B%E2%80%95%E2%80%95%E5%A4%A7%E4%BC%81%E6%A5%AD%E7%97%85%E3%82%92%E6%89%93%E7%A0%B4%E3%81%99%E3%82%8B%E3%80%8C%E6%94%BB%E3%82%81%E3%81%A8%E5%AE%88%E3%82%8A%E3%81%AE%E7%B5%8C%E5%96%B6%E3%80%8D-%E5%8A%A0%E8%97%A4%E9%9B%85%E5%89%87/dp/4862762867/ref=sr_1_6?__mk_ja_JP=%E3%82%AB%E3%82%BF%E3%82%AB%E3%83%8A&dchild=1&keywords=schaede&qid=1595526565&sr=8-6)" (両利きの組織をつくる: 大企業病打破する攻めと守りの経営, Eiji Shuppan 2020, in Japanese). In addition, she has published over 50 peer-reviewed articles, book chapters and company case studies. Her website, www.thejapanologist.com (http://www.thejapanologist.com/), features a blog on contemporary topics in Japanese business, society and political economy.                      At JFIT, Schaede runs a weekly webinar series called JAPAN ZOOMINAR @ UC SAN DIEGO (https://jfit.ucsd.edu/zoominar/index.html), where she discusses contemporary Japan with global scholars, business leaders and industry experts. In the classroom, she teaches GPS's international management capstone class ("Strategy and Negotiation") and a management class titled "Managing Business Reinvention in a Disrupted World." For the Japan Focus of the MIA degree, she has taught the required "Business and Management in Japan," as well as "Innovation and Entrepreneurship in Japan."          This event is made possible thanks to generous financial support from the Japan Foundation.All 21JPSI events are 100% in-person unless otherwise noted.
source: IU Hamilton Lugar School, IU Kelley School of Business
