title: Spring 2026 Colloquium Series with Elena Guzmán, Beatrice Capote, Solimar Otero, Maria Hamilton Abegunde
description: The Center for Latin American and Caribbean Studies is proud to present the continuation of its Colloquium Series for Spring 2026.  We welcome Elena Guzmán, Beatrice Capote, Solimar Otero, and Maria Hamilton Abegunde as they present Oríkì Oshun.Oríkì Oshun is an experimental short film that honors the Orisha Oshun, a central figure in Yoruba cosmology and spirituality. Rooted in the Yoruba tradition of oríkì—a performative act of praise that invokes the essence, attributes, and destiny of a person, place, or deity—this film reimagines the possibilities of storytelling through a multisensory, synesthetic cinematic experience. Through a series of interconnected vignettes, Oríkì Oshun weaves together sacred stories (pataki) that reveal the multidimensionality of Oshun. While often celebrated as the Orisha of beauty, love, and wealth, this film ventures beyond these familiar portrayals, offering a deeper and more complex vision of Oshun's identity. It explores her sacrifices, her struggles, her resilience, and her transformation into a fierce warrior. By blending traditional Yoruba performance aesthetics with experimental film techniques, Oríkì Oshun creates a polyrhythmic and immersive space where sound, image, and movement converge to honor the sacred. This project is not just a celebration of Oshun but a meditation on the power of storytelling to expand our understanding of divine multiplicity, human imperfection, and sacred possibility.     The CLACS Spring 2026 Colloquium meets Mondays from February through April, with faculty and guest speakers engaging Latin America and the Caribbean through research on history, culture, language, politics, and the arts. Check the CLACS Calendar (https://events.iu.edu/clacs/) for details.
source: IU La Casa Latino Cultural Center

title: ACC Book Club features "To Save and Destroy" by Viet Nguyen
location: Asian Culture Center (807 E 10th street, Bloomington, IN)
description: What does it mean to be an "other"? How can writing shift the boundaries between insider and outsider for both author and audience? All are welcome to join the ACC in discussing these questions over dinner through Viet Thanh Nguyen's essay collection To Save and to Destroy: Writing as an Other. Originally delivered as the Norton Lecture series at Harvard in 2023-24, the six chapters span Nguyen's reflections on war, solidarity, and power, drawing from sources ranging from classics of American literature to Nguyen's own lived experience as a refugee. Digital copies of the eBook are freely available to access through IU Libraries, so all attendees are welcome to read ahead! Pizza will be served at each meeting. For more information, please reach out to ACC volunteer and book club moderator Cheryl Nong, PhD student in social psychology, at acc@iu.edu. The next book club meeting is on March 26, 2026.  All Indiana University events, groups, activities, and educational, cultural, and historical observances welcome all IU students and other members of the Indiana University community.For more information, view this event in BeInvolved (https://beinvolved.indiana.edu/event/11950465).

title: Society
description: $8.00 - Tickets for the 7 PM screening of SOCIETY 1989 • USA • 99 minutes • dir. Brian Yuzna Thursday, April 16th at The Orbit Room (107 N College) $8 dollars | 18+ Showtime: 7pm T RAILER Synopsis : After producing Re-Animator , Brian Yuzna turned his hand to directing with SOCIETY —one of the ickiest, most original body horror shockers of all time. Teenager Bill Whitney has always felt like the odd one out in his wealthy, upper-class Beverly Hills family. But his sense of alienation takes a sinister turn when he hears an audio recording of his sister’s coming-out party, which seems to implicate his family in a bizarre, ritualistic orgy. Packing stomach-churning grue courtesy Screaming Mad George ( A Nightmare on Elm Street 4 ) and thought-provoking social commentary in equal measure, SOCIETY is a biting satire that culminates in the most gag-inducing “climax” in all of horror history. *Cicada Cinema is dedicated to curating a wide variety of films while ensuring a safe and inclusive moviegoing experience for all. Our mission to program underrepresented films can lead to screening films that may contain uncomfortable or otherwise insensitive imagery and content. We encourage you to research the films we program beforehand to determine if they could be upsetting to you. Please feel free to contact us for guidance on any film you may have questions about.

title: CANCELED: REEI Colloquium: "Radio Free Europe and Romanian Jazz in Exile" with Grace Pechianu
location: GLOBAL & INTERNATIONAL STUDIES BUILDING - Room Number: 4067
description: This talk explores how Radio Free Europe's broadcast 1964 interview and performance featuring defected jazz pianist Eugen Ciceu presented an alternative, aggressive paradigm to jazz diplomacy during the Cold War. Come learn how Romanian exiles undermined political and cultural isolationism while advocating for individual agency and creativity within a democratic, free market society.  Grace Pechianu is a doctoral candidate in musicology at Indiana University, Bloomington. Her research considers networks of Romanian exiles and defected musicians, their role as contributors to Radio Free Europe, and role in facilitating communication with Romanians behind the Iron Curtain.
source: IU Hamilton Lugar School, IU Jacobs School of Music

title: LCIU Colloquium Speaker Spring 2026
description:

title: Doctoral Recital – Nelson Rodriguez Garza, guitar
Symphonic Band – Eric M. Smedley, conductor; Kyung Sun Lee, violin soloist
description: Repertoire  Don Freund: Beyond the Grass Gates for violin and wind ensemble (1998)Cindy McTee: Circuits (1990/2011)Persichetti/Westover-Muñoz: Symphony No. 7 ("Liturgical"), Op. 80 (1958)About the ArtistsEric M. Smedley is associate professor of music in bands at the Indiana University Jacobs School of Music, where he directs the Symphonic Band, teaches undergraduate and graduate instrumental conducting courses, and serves as the director of the Big Red Basketball Band. Smedley earned a Doctor of Musical Arts degree from the University of Washington and Master of Music and Bachelor of Music Education degrees from the Jacobs School of Music. He previously served on the music faculties of Boise State University and Western Kentucky University and as a public-school music educator in Cocoa Beach, Florida; Santa Monica, California; and Kenosha, Wisconsin. He has conducted throughout North America and in Vietnam, Poland, Russia, and Japan. Smedley maintains an active schedule as a clinician, guest conductor, adjudicator, and trumpet performer. His Symphonic Band was selected to perform at the 2018 College Band Directors National Association North Central Conference. He was a finalist at the 2023 Warsaw Wind Band Conducting Competition in Warsaw, Poland. In 2025, Navona Records released the album Origins and Expansions, featuring a composition by Kevin Bobo, world-renowned steel pan soloist Liam Teague, and the IU Wind Ensemble. Smedley's research interests are varied and include projects in the areas of contemporary wind band literature, wind band orchestration, and conducting pedagogy. Smedley is a member of the Gamma chapter of the international band fraternity Phi Beta Mu and is an honorary member of Kappa Kappa Psi, Tau Beta Sigma, and Phi Mu Alpha Sinfonia. He is a member of the College Band Directors National Association and the National Association for Music Education. He lives in Bloomington, Indiana, with his wife, Jessica, their daughter, Ella, and their Bedlington Terriers, Piper and Finn.  Violinist Kyung Sun Lee is professor of music in violin at the Indiana University Jacobs School of Music. She captured a bronze medal in the 1993 Queen Elizabeth Competition, sixth prize in the 1994 Tchaikovsky Competition, first prizes at the Washington and D'Angelo International Competitions, and third prize in the Montreal International Competition, where she also won the Audience Favorite and Best Performance of the Commissioned Work prizes. Subsequent to winning these awards, Lee has enjoyed ever-increasing popularity as a performer and received high critical acclaim. The Strad praised her "exceptional tonal suavity and expressive intensity in equal measure," while Harris Goldsmith declared, "Godard's Concerto Romantique could not have had a more outstanding soloist than Kyung Sun Lee" (New York Concert Review Inc.). In addition to being in demand as a soloist and chamber musician, Lee is an accomplished teacher and clinician. After becoming assistant professor of violin at Oberlin Conservatory in fall 2001, then associate professor at the University of Houston in fall 2006, she has been professor at Seoul National University since 2009. Lee taught for two summers at the Aspen Music Festival and has also been on faculty at the Bowdoin Music Festival and the Heifetz International Institute. In recent, years she has been in demand as a judge of violin competitions including the Joachim International Violin Competition Hannover, Seoul International Competition, and Singapore International Violin Competition. She studied at Seoul National University, Peabody Conservatory, and The Juilliard School. Her teachers have included Sylvia Rosenberg, Robert Mann, and Dorothy Delay. Lee plays a Joseph Guarnerius violin from 1723 and is music director of Changwon International Chamber Music Festival and Seoul Virtuosi Chamber Orchestra.

title: Curator-led Tour
location: SIDNEY AND LOIS ESKENAZI MUSEUM OF ART - Room Number: Check in with Guest Services on the first floor
description: Step into the world of acclaimed photographer Rania Matar (b. 1964, Lebanon), whose striking portraits document young women persisting in uncertain times. Matar's lens highlights women's creativity, strength, dignity, and resilience—offering a powerful counter-narrative to the challenges of her country and region. Read more about this featured exhibition (https://artmuseum.indiana.edu/exhibitions/upcoming/_feature-exhibitions/2026-03-05-rania-matar-where-do-i-go.html).  Join us for a rare opportunity to explore the artist's work in depth, guided by the scholar who researched and curated the exhibition. Read Leila Reichert's bio. (http://Elliot%20Josephine%20Leila%20Reichert%20is%20an%20American%20curator,%20critic,%20and%20editor.%20She%20is%20the%20inaugural%20Curator%20of%20Contemporary%20Art%20at%20the%20Eskenazi%20Museum%20of%20Art%20at%20Indiana%20University%20and%20was%20a%202023-24%20Propel%20Fellow%20with%20the%20Association%20of%20Art%20Museum%20Curators%20Foundation.%20She%20was%20formerly%20Art%20Editor%20of%20Newcity%20and%20Assistant%20Curator%20at%20the%20Block%20Museum%20of%20Art,%20Northwestern%20University.%20She%20has%20curated%20and%20assisted%20in%20developing%20critically%20celebrated%20exhibitions%20in%20the%20United%20States%20and%20abroad.%20Her%20critical%20writing%20has%20been%20published%20in%20the%20Journal%20of%20Visual%20Studies,%20Hyperallergic,%20the%20Brooklyn%20Rail,%20Newcity%20Art,%20and%20The%20Seen,%20among%20others.%20She%20holds%20dual%20masters%E2%80%99%20degrees%20in%20Modern%20and%20Contemporary%20Art%20History%20and%20Arts%20Administration%20and%20Cultural%20Policy%20from%20the%20School%20of%20the%20Art%20Institute%20of%20Chicago%20and%20a%20Bachelors%20of%20Arts%20with%20Honors%20in%20Art%20History%20from%20Northwestern%20University,%20where%20she%20graduated%20summa%20cum%20laude.)  Reserve your spot today for a chance to connect with Rania Matar's striking photographs in a personal, meaningful way. Leila will share the stories, research, and insights behind the exhibition, making this experience as memorable as the photography itself.   Know Before You GoSpace is limited to 15 participants. RSVP to reserve a space. Walk-ins are welcome if space is available. If this page says "registration has closed", this means that registration and the waitlist have filled for this date and time.Plan to arrive early to check in. At 1:25 p.m., any unclaimed spaces may be released to guests who are standing by waiting for a space.Admission and tours are free.A small handbag may be carried at your side. Larger bags and backpacks will need to be stored before touring.Read about where to find us, where to park, accessibility, and museum guidelines.When You ArriveCheck in with Guest Services staff on the first floor.If you park in the IMU parking lot, Guest Services can validate your parking voucher for a discount.Be sure to store backpacks and large bags before touring. Lockers and coat racks are available.Keep space (18") between yourself, including anything you're carrying, and the art.
source: Eskenazi Museum of Art, IU Arts & Humanities Institute

title: Intermediate/Advanced Drawing Workshop
location: SIDNEY AND LOIS ESKENAZI MUSEUM OF ART - Room Number: Check in with Guest Services on the first floor
description: Reserve your spot. If this page says, "Registration has closed" that means the waitlist is full.SuppliesBring your favorite drawing pencil (e.g. 4B), a sketchbook or drawing paper, and a phone or device to photograph artwork (without flash) as we sketch.  Know Before You GoArrive early to find parking, check in, and get your drawing supplies ready.  At 1:55 p.m. any unclaimed reservations will be released to guests who are standing by waiting for a spot.  Where to find us, where to park, accessibility, and museum guidelines (https://artmuseum.indiana.edu/visit/your-visit.html).  This workshop is recommended for ages 12 and up. Anyone under 18, who is not an IU student, must be accompanied by an adult.  When You ArriveCheck in with Guest Services at the information desk on the first floor, even if you come often and even if you are late. We appreciate having an accurate attendance count.  Store all backpacks and large bags before entering the galleries. A small handbag may be carried at your side.  Get your supplies ready. Attach an 18x24" piece of newsprint to a drawing board.  Cancellations  This workshop often has a waitlist. Please use the cancellation link included with your confirmation email if your plans change.About the Instructor  John Cheek has taught credit and non-credit classes for eight years in portrait painting, landscape painting, palette knife painting, life drawing, beginning drawing, and advanced drawing, as well as in design. He volunteers his time and expertise to facilitate drawing workshops at the Eskenazi Museum of Art.

The Natural | 5X Robert Redford
description: Robert Redford stars in this inspiring fable that begins when 14-year-old Roy Hobbs fashions a powerful bat from a fallen oak tree. He soon impresses major league scouts with his ability, fixing his extraordinary talent in the mind of sportswriter Max Mercy (Robert Duvall), who eventually becomes instrumental in Hobbs' career. But a meeting with a mysterious woman shatters his dream. Years pass and an older Hobbs reappears as a rookie from the New York Knights. Overcoming physical pain and defying those who have a stake in seeing the Knights lose, Hobbs, with his boyhood bat, has his chance to lead the Knights to the pennant and to finally fulfill his dream. [138 min; drama; English]

title: ABRA Cadabra
location: Buskirk-Chumley Theater,114 East Kirkwood Avenue, Bloomington, IN 47408, USA
description:
Attention all ABBA fans! Get ready for a night of glittering costumes, irresistible melodies, and pure joy when ABRA Cadabra – the internationally acclaimed ABBA tribute band – takes the stage at Burkirk-Chumley Theater in Bloomington on April 19, 2026 @ 7:30pm.

For 25 years, ABRA Cadabra has been captivating audiences across the globe with faithful yet fresh performances of ABBA’s timeless hits. From “Dancing Queen” to “Mamma Mia” and “Take a Chance on Me,” the band blends meticulous musical detail with colorful visuals and an atmosphere designed to get audiences on their feet.

The result is a high-energy, family-friendly spectacle featuring a live band, dancers, dazzling costumes, and multiple wardrobe changes. Audiences are encouraged to sing, clap, and dance along, creating a shared moment of nostalgia and celebration.

Connect with ABRA Cadabra on Facebook & Instagram @ATributeToABBA and online at http://www.iloveabba.com http://www.iloveabba.com/ www.iloveabba.com

Here’s what our fans are saying.

“Best show we’ve ever seen!” – Gilberte Nowlan – Moncton, NB

“I’ve seen tributes to ABBA before, they were great, but you are the best. If you come back East we will be there!” –Jack Hayward – Fredericton, NB

“Just saw your show in Regina. WOW! You guys are amazing – I took my (80+) mom and she loved it! The entire group is stunning, never seen any performers acknowledge their entire crew, go into the audience and so obviously love what you do! Thank you…we will come again!” – Rhonda Lamb – Regina, SK

title: Book Talk: "The Permanence of anti-Roma Racism: (Un)uttered Sentences" With Margareta Matache
location: GLOBAL & INTERNATIONAL STUDIES BUILDING - Room Number: 4067
description: This talk draws on Margareta Matache's new book,The Permanence of anti-Roma Racism: (Un)uttered Sentences, to examine anti-Roma racism, which she frames in Romani as ofitsialo telćhudipen (structural or top-down oppression). The focus will include the origins, protectors, legacies, and continuities of anti-Roma racism, as well as its place within global frameworks and theories related to oppression, racism, racialization, and slavery.Margareta Matache, PhD, is a Harvard Lecturer, co-founder, and Director of the Roma Program at Harvard University. Matache is the co-editor of Time for Reparations: A Global Perspective (2021) and Realizing Roma Rights (2017). Her most recent book,The Permanence of Anti-Roma Racism: (Un)uttered Sentences (Routledge), examines anti-Roma racism and links to other oppressions.   This event is cosponsored by The Byrnes Institute, the Institute for European Studies, and the Department of Anthropology

description: The Center for Latin American and Caribbean Studies is proud to present the continuation of its Colloquium Series for Spring 2026.  We welcome Dr. Eduardo Herrera as he presents Argentina's Soccer Chanting Tradition, from Neighborhood Stadiums to Global Stages.   Eduardo Herrera is an associate professor in the department of Folklore and Ethnomusicology and the director of the Ethnomusicology Institute at Indiana University Bloomington. With a PhD from University of Illinois Urbana-Champaign, His research comprises topics from sound studies to Latinx cultural practices and coloniality/postcoloniality/decoloniality.  The CLACS Spring 2026 Colloquium meets Mondays from February through April, with faculty and guest speakers engaging Latin America and the Caribbean through research on history, culture, language, politics, and the arts. Check the CLACS Calendar (https://events.iu.edu/clacs/) for details.

title: Latin Jazz Ensemble – Wayne Wallace, director; Andy Miller, rhythm section coach
description: About the DirectorsWayne Wallace is professor of practice in jazz studies and jazz trombone at the IU Jacobs School of Music. A seven-time Grammy nominee, he is one of the most respected exponents of African American Latin music in the world today. Wallace is known for his use of traditional forms and styles in combination with contemporary music and has earned wide critical acclaim, including placement in both the trombone and producer categories of the DownBeat Critics Poll. An accomplished arranger, educator, and composer with compositions for film and television, he has received grants from the Creative Work Fund, the National Endowment for the Arts, the Lila Wallace Foundation, and the San Francisco Arts Commission. Wallace has performed, recorded, and studied with many acknowledged masters of the Afro-Latin and jazz idioms, such as Aretha Franklin, Bobby Hutcherson, Earth Wind and Fire, Pete Escovedo, Santana, and many more. This experience has provided a solid foundation for Wallace's current explorations of the intersections of a wealth of cultural styles and rhythmic concepts. Born and raised in San Francisco, Wallace was exposed to blues, country and western, R&B, jazz, and Afro-Caribbean music at an early age. The fertile musical environment of the San Francisco Bay Area shaped his career. His studies of Afro-Latin music and jazz include trips to Cuba, New York City, and Puerto Rico. Widely respected as a teacher and historian, Wallace has taught at San José State University, Stanford University, and the Jazzschool in Berkeley. He has conducted lectures, workshops, and clinics in the Americas and Europe since 1983. Head of the critically acclaimed Patois Records, Wallace has created a unique record label with a passionate mission of developing and chronicling the multilingual styles of the San Francisco Bay Area music scene. Under his direction the label has released 13 recordings to critical acclaim, including recordings by Wallace, Marc and Paul van Wageningen, and vocalists Kat Parra, Alexa Weber-Morales, and Kristina. He is an endorser of Conn-Selmer trombones.Andy Miller is lecturer in music in percussion at the Indiana University Jacobs School of Music, with an emphasis in Latin jazz and Afro-Latin percussion music. An engaging performer and passionate educator, he navigates the diverse world of percussion with technical and musical precision across a broad range of artistic practices. Fostering a deep interest in the percussion music of the African diaspora, Miller has studied with master percussionists in the United States and internationally. A Fulbright grant recipient, Miller studied Afro-Colombian currulao, cumbia, and bullerengue in Colombia with La Wey Segura, Francis Lara, and Emilsen Pacheco, later returning on a Presser Graduate Music Award to study joropo music with Fernando Torres Ramirez. Miller conducted field research in Brazil with Jorge Alabê for his doctoral project, "The Language of Drumming in Afro-Brazilian Candomblé Ketu." His study of bomba percussion, song, and dance took him to Puerto Rico to study with Beto Torrens, Marién Torres, Rafa Maya, and Hector "Coco" Baréz. He studied Guinean mande drumming with Bolokada Conde and Afro-Cuban folkloric and popular percussion with Michael Spiro. Miller also champions percussion art music by Latin American composers, including a performance of Ricardo Lorenz's Venezuelan maracas concerto Pataruco with the Jacobs School's University Orchestra. Commissions include Maria José by Luis Fernando Sanchez Gooding, Silent Surface by Rafael Llanos, and Monologue IV: Bilingüismo by José Martinez. His commitment to new music has led to residencies with the Bang on a Can Summer Music Festival and the Atlantic Music Festival Contemporary Ensemble. His collaborations with choreographers as a composer/performer have premiered in New York City, throughout the Midwest, and internationally. Miller served as music director of the University of Wisconsin–Milwaukee Dance Department (2017-24), accompanying classes in styles including modern, ballet, West African, and Afro-Latin dance. He previously taught percussion at the Lawrence University Conservatory of Music, Olivet Nazarene University, and Bembé Drum and Dance. Miller earned degrees from IU (D.M.), the University of Illinois Urbana-Champaign (M.M.), and Wright State University (B.M.).

title: The Alchemy of Confidence
location: STEPHEN L. & CONNIE J. FERGUSON INTERNATIONAL CENTER - Room Number: 421/423
description: This salon series at Indiana University features leading thinkers exploring how to build better digital and natural worlds.Beyond the Web / After Analog Speaker Series.  Deception and honesty have long been locked in evolutionary arms races. When false signals become common, they lose their power, favoring honest signals whose reliability is enforced by cost. This principle, central to signaling theory, explains many costly biological traits and offers insight into human communication and disinformation.  This insight lies at the heart of signaling theory, which explains many costly biological traits and behaviors once thought to be useless. The theory also sheds light on human culture and has become increasingly relevant for understanding disinformation and identity deception in the digital age. However, human reliability depends on more than costly signals alone. Trust between individuals, along with third-party enforcement through social bonds, surveillance, and institutions, plays a crucial role.  This talk explores how signaling costs, affective trust, and technological oversight interact, with particular attention to when technologies support trust and when they undermine it. As rapid technological change reshapes the incentives for deception and detection, understanding the foundations of trust has never been more urgent.About the Speaker  Judith is a writer, designer and artist whose work focuses on the co-evolution of technology and society. She has published numerous articles about social media, AI, ethics and anonymity, and is the author of The Social Machine: Designs for Living Online (http://amzn.to/NXFIFt) (MIT Press). As the former director of the MIT Media Lab's Sociable Media Group, she and her students designed innovative interfaces for on-line communities; their art projects examining the future of identity, privacy and mediated life have been exhibited in museums and galleries worldwide.

title: T-Break Workshop
description:
Curious about taking a break from cannabis or wanting to better understand your relationship with it? Join us for an interactive T-Break Workshop based on the T-Break Guide by Tom Fontana. This session explores what a 21-day cannabis tolerance break can look like and offers practical tools for navigating common experiences like cravings, sleep changes, mood shifts, and stress.
Participants will learn about the physical, emotional, and reflective themes that often come up during a T-Break, engage in guided discussion, and explore strategies for creating balance — whether your goal is to reset your tolerance, take a short pause, or simply reflect on your use. This workshop is supportive, educational, and judgment-free. No prior experience with a T-Break is required.

Hosted by: Collegiate Recovery Community

title: Crash (1996) | Additional Films and Guests
description: Based on the equally infamous J.G. Ballard novel, David Cronenberg's 1996 film Crash is built to rattle cultural cages, spark heated debated and discussion, and draw you into a cinematic world like no other. Aestheticizing the erotic allure of auto collisions and prodding at the human fascination with death and danger, Cronenberg crafts a utterly singular film that—at the time of its release—addressed head-on the social fears of the swiftly approaching millennium. 30 years later, the film takes an a different, but still immediate, resonance in a world living through one "unprecedented" event after another. [100 min; drama, thriller; English]

description: After mesmerizing audiences across Europe and the UK, The Rock Orchestra by Candlelight is set to electrify the USA once again. description:

title: Center for Survey Research Consultations
description:
Make an appointment now! https://iub.libcal.com/appointments/csr

You have the option to schedule on Zoom or in-person.

The Center for Survey Research consults with faculty, staff, and students across a wide range of substantive disciplines spanning business, education, the social sciences, and the health sciences. We are happy to assist with any qualitative or quantitative research project by providing consultation on:
research design
questionnaire development and review
sample design
online interface and reporting portal development
database and data collection instrument programming including Qualtrics online survey software
usability testing
sample management including high-volume email campaigns
data collection using online, telephone, mail, and face-to-face administration
qualitative methods (semi-structured interviews, focus groups, observations, etc.)
data processing
data analysis

Takes place in the Wells Library Scholars' Commons, first floor of the east tower. No question too big or too small!

title: Frontlines & Futures: Graduate Foreign Policy Salon Series: Reforming the United Nations: Can Multilateral Governance Evolve?
description: This session invites graduate students, alumni, faculty, staff, and the general public to explore how the United Nations can evolve in response to emerging global powers, technological innovation, and shifting alliances. Participants will examine challenges like climate-induced migration, artificial intelligence, cyber threats, and the rise of new economic blocs, assessing how these trends impact U.S. interests and global governance.  Through guided discussion, reflection, and visual prompts, participants will debate bold policy strategies, consider ethical trade-offs, and propose actionable reforms to strengthen multilateral institutions. Questions will include: Which emerging trend or power could most reshape global politics? How should the U.S. respond to protect both national and global priorities? What bold policy steps could strengthen multilateral governance, and how can allies be coordinated to build trust? How should ethical principles guide decisions around emerging technologies and global crises?  This safe, structured space emphasizes candid dialogue, critical thinking, and real-world policy analysis. Lunch will be provided. Join us for the final session of the academic year to sharpen your foreign policy instincts and engage with peers on the pressing issues shaping tomorrow's global landscape.
source: IU Hamilton Lugar School, IU Kelley School of Business

Responsibility | Earth Connection Film Festival
description: How do we instill a sense of responsibility to our environment? This program dives into guiding philosophies of stewardship, moral obligation, and ecological knowledge. [78 min. total runtime]There will be a post-film Q&A with Earth Connection Film Festival directors Dr. Jessica Eise (Indiana University) and Prof. Sarah Lasley (California State Polytechnic University, Humboldt). Following the screening, there will be a plant propagation party in the lower lobby from 3-3:45pm.Decoding Ancestral Knowledge(dir. Ruth Lichtman & Sharon Shattuck, 2023, USA): Enter the captivating world of scientific exploration through the lens of Kiana Frank who is a proud Native Hawaiian and microbiologist. Her unique journey intertwines traditional wisdom with the latest molecular techniques, casting light on the intricate interactions between microorganisms and the environment they inhabit. By embracing her Native Hawaiian heritage and its oral traditions, Kiana broadens our concepts of who is a scientist and how science unfolds. [10 min; English]Peixinho/Little Fish(dir. Kaushiik Subramaniam, 2024, United Kingdom): In a coastal village where the sea gives and takes, Peixinho follows the story of one young man defying the tide of generational fear. Once unable to swim, he now teaches local children to float, dive, and reclaim their relationship with the ocean—not as something to be feared, but to be understood and protected. As he inspires the next generation with water safety and ocean literacy, he also works with elders to encourage more sustainable practices. [7 min; Portuguese with English subtitles]The Last Observers(dir. Maja K. Mikkelsen, 2024, Sweden): For decades, Karin and Lennart have worked side by side doing weather observations and managing Falsterbo Bird Observatory, a ringing station for migrating birds. Their inspiring life story invites us to reflect on what matters most in life, and what beauty there is to be found in everyday life. [24 min; Swedish with English subtitles]Guardian of the Well(dir. Bentley Brown & Tahir Ben Mahamat Zene, 2025, Chad): In mid-2024, Chad experienced its worst drought in decades. Most of the livestock died, people moved away, and the city vanished. In the desolate Batha Prefecture, a chance encounter at a water well opens a window into the reality of a Central African country deeply affected by climate change. [5 min; Arabic with English subtitles]Planetwalker(dir. Dominic Gill & Nadia Gill, 2024, USA): In 1971, John Francis witnessed an oil tanker collision in the San Francisco Bay. The sight of oiled birds on the shoreline caused him to give up motorized transport and speaking for 17 years, convinced that listening was the way forward. Planetwalker recounts much of his internal journey, exploring the web-like and deeply personal consequences of his decades of pilgrimage. [31 min; English]

title: Mindful Makers
description:
Join us for Mindful Makers, a weekly event built around being creative, meeting new people and feeling more confident in yourself and your wellness. Each week has a theme - come for one or come for all! We're here to have fun and make cool things.
January 21: Semester Vision Boards
Picture your new semester! Create a vision board that will motivate you to achieve this semester. Learn some tips on how to set great goals to help you achieve that vision.

January 28: Meditation and Aromatherapy
Ever been curious about aromatherapy and how it works? Here's your opportunity to learn more! While you're here, we'll also practice deep breathing techniques, progressive relaxation and guided visualization, and other simple techniques that don't require any special skills or equipment

February 4: Crochet the Stress Away
Whether you're a crochet expert or have never picked up a hook in your life, we have a place for you! Join us, learn basic crochet stitches, and talk about ways to have fun on campus and in the community.

February 11: Origami Ornaments and Overcoming
Sometimes life throws us curve balls. In this Mindful Makers, we'll go through the process of folding origami hearts or cranes, and using those to make beautiful ornaments that reflect challenges we've overcome. These cranes carry hope on their wings!

February 18: Air Clay Emotions
What does your angry monster look like? What about your joy? Use air clay to sculpt mini-monsters representing both supportive and challenging emotions, and learn ways of taming your more wild ones. Remember - not all monsters are scary. Sometimes they're pretty great!

February 25: Love Letters to Your Body and Cookie Decorating
Sometimes we need reminders of how great our bodies can be. In this week's MM, you'll learn simple bookbinding skills to make an accordion book, and fill those pages with what we like about ourselves and our bodies. Join our nutritionist, Katie Shepherd, for cookie decorating while you're here!

March 4: Watercolor Your Values
One of the most powerful motivators we have is our personal values. Paint a values portrait, and discuss how you can tie your goals into your values to keep yourself moving forward.

March 11: Sleep Vision Boards
Getting a good night's sleep is critical towards functioning at your best - but truthfully, most of us don't get nearly the quantity or quality of sleep we need. Create a sleep vision board as you learn ways to improve your sleep habits and why sleep is so important!

March 25: Create Your Own Journal
And we do mean from scratch! Learn simple Japanese stitchwork bookbinding to make your own book, decorate it to suit your tastes, and create a journal you can use however you like! (And if you're new to journaling, we can work on that as well!)

April 1: Perler Beads and Mindfulness
Being mindful doesn't take anything special. Sometimes it can be found in the smallest of things - like the little perler beads! Make yourself an ornament while discovering ways mindfulness can be a part of the everyday things you already do!

April 8: Gaming with Friends
Do you know that the oldest known game board is 9000 years old? We've been playing games for a long time! Well, not us personally. We're just going to play for a couple of hours. Come and join us!

April 15: Resinate
No, that's not a typo. Learn the basics of resin crafting and make your own ornaments or magnets. While here, share your favorite hobbies and why they matter to you - maybe you'll inspire someone else!

April 22: Affirmation Jars
At the end of the semester, sometimes we need reminders of what motivates us, makes us special, or just what matters to us. Create your own affirmation jar and fill it up with all those good things. When you're feeling low, pull one out and give yourself a boost!

April 29: Keep Growing
Whether you're looking forward to graduation, a summer off, or one filled with activity, summer is a great time for growth. Share your upcoming opportunities as you decorate a pot and select a new plant to nurture. Like plants, we need care to keep growing, so share your ideas for how you're going to care for yourself!

Hosted by: Health Promotion

Additional Information can be found at: https://beinvolved.indiana.edu/event/11977508

title: Open Mic Night at The Comedy Attic
location: The Comedy Attic,123 S Walnut St, Bloomington, IN 47408, USA
description:
Every Wednesday at 8PM is Comedy Attic Open Mic Night. Bloomington has one of the best local comedy scenes in the country, and it all starts with our open mic night!

Each comedian is asked to do no more than five minutes of completely original material. While we do not care about foul language in your set, we do not allow racist, homophobic, transphobic or rape/female bashing jokes. If you have to ask if it's racist, homophobic, transphobic or a rape joke, it probably is.

Comedians that wish to participate are asked to e-mail comedyattic@gmail.com mailto:comedyattic@gmail.com . Newcomers and any participants who are not paid professionals will be required to bring audience members to see them. This is non-negotiable. So if you plan on signing up, be sure to invite as many friends as you can. Good luck!

title: Pillion
description: $8.00 - Tickets for the 8 PM screening of PILLION 2026 • UK • 107 minutes • dir. Harry Lighton Wednesday, April 22nd at The Back Door (207 S College Ave) $8 | 21+ Showtime: 8 pm TRAILER Synopsis : In his unorthodox queer romance, first-time feature director Harry Lighton has fashioned something highly improbable: a film about a sadomasochistic relationship that is both transgressive and disarming. Harry Melling plays the mild Colin, whose gayness is happily accepted by his parents (Lesley Sharp and Douglas Hodge) and who is most extroverted when singing in a barbershop quartet. When this sweet-natured square catches the eye of strapping, leather-clad biker Ray (Alexander Skarsgård) at the local pub, he finds himself entering a submissive role—both domestic and erotic—for the inscrutable beauty who brings him both satisfaction and confusion, pain and pleasure. As their affair continues, Colin must decide what he wants and needs—and who he really is. Comic without ever descending into judgment of its characters, Pillion is a beguiling inquiry into the social and sexual performances of masculinity, featuring fearless work from Melling and Skarsgård.

title: Making Indiana University: A Reading, Conversation, and Signing with Jim Capshew
description: Blending institutional and environmental history, Making Indiana University: History, Landscape, and a Sense of Place traces how the university's identity has been shaped—materially and imaginatively—by Dunn's Woods, the evolving campus landscape, and the narratives through which generations have understood this place.  In a setting central to IU's early history, Capshew will introduce the book, read from it, and take questions from the audience. The event will be introduced by John Summerlot, Director of Enterprise Risk & Incident Management at the IU Foundation. It will conclude with a signing of a specially produced booklet featuring the Wylie House chapter, along with commemorative image cards drawn from the volume. Following the program, guests are invited to enjoy a self-guided tour of the Wylie House.  Light refreshments will be served.  The event also celebrates IU Libraries Publishing (https://libraries.indiana.edu/libraries-publishing), the university's new open-access publisher of long-form scholarly, creative, and public-facing works. By making publications freely available worldwide at no cost to readers or authors, IU Libraries Publishing advances Indiana University's public mission and ensures that its scholarship remains accessible to all.  This event will be of particular interest to IU alumni, Bloomington residents, faculty and staff, and anyone interested in university history, environmental studies, and the evolving story of Indiana University.  Doors will open at 5 p.m. This event is free and open to the public.  Free parking options are available for visitors:Neighborhood Street Parking: Free street parking is available after 5 p.m., including in areas normally restricted to Neighborhood Zone permit holders.  Wylie House Museum Parking: Complimentary parking (no permit required) is available behind the Wylie House Museum and adjacent to its Education Center (the gray barn). This lot includes one accessible parking space. Access these spaces via the alley behind the museum, from Grant Street and Lincoln Street.

title: Concert Orchestra – Thomas Wilkins, conductor
description: Repertoire  Bonds: The Montgomery Variations (1964)Barnes: Kinsfolknem (2024)Dawson: Negro Folk Symphony (1934/53)  About the ConductorThomas Wilkins is principal conductor of the Hollywood Bowl Orchestra, artistic partner for education and external engagement and Germeshausen Family and Youth Concerts Conductor Chair with the Boston Symphony Orchestra, and professor of music (conducting) at the Indiana University Jacobs School of Music, where he holds the Henry A. Upper Chair of Orchestral Conducting, established by the late Barbara and David Jacobs as a part of Indiana University's Matching the Promise Campaign. He is also music director laureate of the Omaha Symphony, where he was music director from 2005 through 2021, and principal guest conductor of the Virginia Symphony Orchestra. Past positions have included resident conductor of the Detroit Symphony and the Florida Orchestra (Tampa Bay) and associate conductor of the Richmond (Virginia) Symphony. He also has served on the music faculties of North Park University (Chicago), University of Tennessee in Chattanooga, and Virginia Commonwealth University in Richmond. Devoted to promoting a life-long enthusiasm for music, Wilkins brings energy and commitment to audiences of all ages. He is hailed as a master at communicating and connecting with audiences. He has guest conducted orchestras throughout the United States. Wilkins' commitment to community has been demonstrated by his participation on several boards of directors, including the Greater Omaha Chamber of Commerce, the Charles Drew Health Center (Omaha), the Center Against Spouse Abuse in Tampa Bay, and the Museum of Fine Arts as well as the Academy Preparatory Center both in St. Petersburg, Florida. Currently, he serves as chairman of the board for the Raymond James Charitable Endowment Fund and as National Ambassador for the non-profit World Pediatric Project, headquartered in Richmond, Virginia, which provides children throughout Central America and the Caribbean with critical surgical and diagnostic care. A native of Norfolk, Virginia, Wilkins is a graduate of the Shenandoah Conservatory of Music and the New England Conservatory of Music (Boston). He and his wife, Sheri-Lee, are the proud parents of twin daughters, Erica and Nicole.

title: 2026 Conference on Community Engaged Learning
location: Cook Center, Maxwell Hall, 750 E. Kirkwood Ave., Bloomington, IN 40705
description: The conference convenes faculty, staff, community partners, and students from IU Bloomington to celebrate and recognize achievements in community engaged learning. We explore best practices in CEL and teaching and how community-engagement elevates professional growth and research. Connect with peers, share insights, and shape the future of education.  The structure of the conference is a half-day with structured space for networking, conversation, and workshops. Lunch is included.  Conference goals are toRecognize the community engagement efforts of faculty, community partners, students, and staff  Support best practices in CEL, community engagement, and teaching  Help faculty connect community-engaged teaching to professional development and research    The conference is seeking proposals for presentations, roundtables, and academic posters from faculty, community partners, students, and staff.Submit a Proposal (https://iubovpue-fireform.eas.iu.edu/online/form/index/celconproposals)  Proposals should address the following themes:Building trust and equitable relationships in community engaged partnerships  Supporting students' civic development  CEL as a High Impact Practice (HIP) and assessing its effects on student success  Creating productive synergies between research and community-engaged teaching  Demonstrating the impact of CEL on Bloomington, state, and international communities  Other areas of interest

title: Arbor Day Tree Planting at Rogers Family Park
location: Rogers Family Park, 2000 E Winslow Rd, Bloomington, IN 47401, USA
description:
Celebrate Arbor Day in Indiana on the last Friday in April and continue Bloomington's tree-planting tradition!
Friday, April 24 from 9 a.m.-noon at Rogers Family Park, 2000 E. Winslow Rd. in Bloomington
Assist Parks and Recreation urban forestry staff as they plant trees along the Jackson Creek Trail, and take home a free tree seedling while supplies last. Tree planting volunteers will help dig and widen holes to plant balled and burlapped trees, move trees into planting position, and spread mulch. Volunteers ages 14 yrs. and older are welcome!
Gloves and shovels are provided; meet at the red barn at Rogers Family Park. Vehicle parking is available inside the entrance to Rogers Family Park south of the Winslow-High-Rogers roundabout. Additional vehicle parking is available at Sherwood Oaks Park and at Olcott Park.

description: Spend a day tromping through wetlands during our Beanblossom Bottoms Nature Preserve Weed Wrangle as we work together to remove invasive cattails. Over the past couple of years, storms and tornado damage have opened the canopy in parts of the preserve, allowing more sunlight to reach the wetlands, which causes aggressive species like cattails to spread.

location: Brown County Playhouse, 70 S. Van Buren St, Nashville, IN, 47448, United States
description:
The Brown County Playhouse in Nashville, Indiana, welcomes blues icons John Primer & The Real Deal Blues Band with special guests Paul Holdman and Gordon Bonham.
As a young man, guitarist, vocalist, and bandleader John Primer learned Chicago blues with the masters. Primer performed regularly with legends such as Muddy Waters, Willie Dixon, and was a long-term band leader for Magic Slim & The Teardrops. Primer is one of the few truly-authentic Chicago bluesmen touring and recording today. He was inducted into the Blues Hall of Fame in 2023.
Primer’s long list of honors and accolades speak to his experience and reputation for engaging a live audience, including: Three-time Grammy® Award nominee; Blues Artist of the Year — Living Blues critic’s poll award; Traditional Blues Artist of the Year — Blues Music Award; Lifetime Achievement Award, Live Album of the Year, and Traditional Blues Album of the Year — Blues Blast Awards

title: Wind Ensemble – Donald McKinney, conductor
description: Repertoire  Shuying Li: In This Breath (2025, consortium premiere)John Corigliano: Gazebo Dances (1972/73)Quinn Mason: A Joyous Trilogy (2019/22)Erik Santos: The Seer (2019)*  *Voice soloist to be announced  About the ConductorDonald J. McKinney is professor of music in bands, director of bands, and chair of the Bands Department at the Indiana University Jacobs School of Music. He was previously director of bands, professor of conducting, and chair of the conducting area at the University of Colorado Boulder College of Music. Prior to his 2013 appointment at CU Boulder, he was the director of wind ensembles and associate professor at Louisiana State University. McKinney has held additional faculty positions at Interlochen Arts Academy and Duquesne University Mary Pappert School of Music. From 2010 to 2015, he was the coordinator of bands for the renowned Interlochen Arts Camp, where he conducted the World Youth Wind Symphony and administered the summer band program. As a guest conductor, he has appeared with the Dallas Winds, Concordia Santa Fe, Boulder Philharmonic Orchestra, Greater Boulder Youth Orchestra, and numerous honor ensembles. His performances with the Dallas Winds have been featured on National Public Radio's Performance Today on numerous occasions. As a teacher of conducting, he has presented conducting master classes at a host of colleges and universities. His recording credits include projects with the Dallas Winds, University of Michigan Symphony Band, University of Texas Wind Ensemble, University of North Texas Wind Symphony, Keystone Wind Ensemble, and the Duquesne University Wind Symphony. He was nominated for a 2019 Grammy Award for producing the Dallas Winds recording John Williams at the Movies. He has published articles in numerous conducting resources, including five volumes of Teaching Music through Performance in Band and The Conductor's Companion. He has also authored a chapter about Pulitzer Prize-winning composer Jennifer Higdon for the book Women of Influence in Contemporary Music. McKinney earned a Doctor of Musical Arts in Conducting degree from the University of Michigan. His primary conducting teachers include Michael Haithcock, Jack Stamp, and Robert Cameron as well as additional study with H. Robert Reynolds and Frank Battisti.

title: Declaration of Independence Curator Talks
description: The Declaration of Independence: The Motives, The Moment is an exhibition exploring the journey taken by the nation's founders. How colonial leaders go from loyal subjects of the British empire to risking everything by becoming revolutionaries? Take an insightful tour of the exhibition from the curator, with up close looks at Revolutionary War era correspondence, books and manuscripts that influenced the revolutionaries, and media that spread the word about the declaration.  Light refreshments will be offered. Come learn something new about the nation's founding and have a snack in Indiana University's rare book library!  Tours are offered at noon, 1 p.m., and 2 p.m. RSVP requested for planning of refreshments and staffing.

description:

title: Drop-in Tours and Crafting
location: SIDNEY AND LOIS ESKENAZI MUSEUM OF ART - Room Number: Featured Exhibitions Gallery, First Floor
description: An Immersive ExperienceInspired by Indonesia's spectacular coral reefs, acclaimed fiber artist Mulyana (b. 1984, Bandung, Indonesia) invites you to wander through a handcrafted seascape where art and ecology meet.  Explore colorful coral reefs, playful fish, and fantastical sea creatures — all meticulously hand-knit and crocheted.  Pause to reflect on the delicate balance between human wellbeing and the vitality of our oceans.  Chat with a Museum Guide to deepen your understanding.  Create Your Own Sea CreatureEnjoy a hands-on activity in the gallery.  No prior experience is needed — a volunteer can guide you step by step.  Choose from a fish, octopus, lobster, sea urchin, or crab.  All supplies are provided, so you can dive right in!  Why Visit? This exhibition is more than art — it's a living conversation about beauty, fragility, and our shared responsibility to protect the ocean. Whether you're an art lover, a family looking for a creative activity, or simply curious, Vital Ecosystems offers a memorable experience for all ages.
source: Eskenazi Museum of Art, IU Arts & Humanities Institute

title: University Orchestra – Jeffery Meyer, conductor; Tichina Vaughn, mezzo-soprano
description: Repertoire  Quinn Mason: Serenade (2025)Jessie Montgomery: Five Freedom Songs (2018)John Adams: Shaker Loops (1978/83)About the Artists  Distinguished for insightful performances, visionary programming, and wide-ranging collaborations, conductor Jeffery Meyer has captivated audiences around the globe. He is professor of orchestral conducting at the Indiana University Jacobs School of Music as well as artistic partner with the Northwest Sinfonietta, one of the northwest United States' most vibrantly creative ensembles. Artistic director of the St. Petersburg Chamber Philharmonic for two decades, his work with the orchestra was noted for its breadth and innovation. His programming has earned three ASCAP Awards for Adventurous Programming and two Vytautas Marijošius Memorial Awards. A champion of contemporary music, Meyer imagines the orchestra experience as a forum for story, emotion, and civic dialogue. His projects have paired symphonic works with multimedia visionaries and diverse cultural collaborators. His recordings span repertoire from baroque to the present; his most recent, Illuminations: Music of Narong Prangcharoen, was released by Albany Records in 2023. Recent highlights include a multimedia Rite of Spring and Petrushka with the Thailand Philharmonic; Carlos Simon's Graffiti with renowned graffiti artists; a theatrical symphonic concert developed in collaboration with Daniel Bernard Roumain and Marc Bamuthi Joseph; Laura Kaminsky's Piano Concerto with Ursula Oppens (Cedille Records); a semistaged production of Nkeiru Okoye's Invitation to a Die-In; and multiple collaborations with the American Composers Orchestra's EarShot program. Recent and upcoming engagements include the City Chamber Orchestra of Hong Kong, Los Angeles Ballet, Xalapa Symphony, Orquesta Juvenil Universitaria Eduardo Mata, American Composers Orchestra, Texas Festival Orchestra at Round Top, and returns to the Grossman Ensemble, Sichuan Symphony, and Thailand Philharmonic. Passionate about working with young musicians and music education, Meyer is an active adjudicator, guest clinician, and master class teacher. Prior to his appointment at the Jacobs School, he led acclaimed orchestral programs at Arizona State University and Ithaca College. He has given master classes throughout the United States, Canada, Europe, and Asia. Meyer holds degrees in piano and composition and earned his Doctor of Musical Arts in Piano Performance with Gilbert Kalish at Stony Brook University.Tichina Vaughn is associate professor of music in voice at the Indiana University Jacobs School of Music. A Grammy Award-winning American mezzo-soprano, celebrated for her "superb, rich singing" and serious "theatrical presence," Vaughn began her international operatic career as a member of the Metropolitan Opera's Lindemann Young Artist Program. Recent successes have included her debut at the Welsh National Opera as Zia Principessa and Zita (Il Trittico), her Royal Opera House Covent Garden debut as the Witch (Hansel and Gretel), Azucena (Il Trovatore) for Piedmont Opera, as well as returning to the Metropolitan Opera as the Hostess of the Inn (Boris Gudonow), and roles in Dialogues de Carmélites, Elektra, and Porgy and Bess. Upcoming engagements include her return to the Semperoper as the Old Lady in Candide, mezzo soloist in Verdi's Requiem with the Flagstaff Symphony Orchestra, and Maria in Porgy and Bess with the Metropolitan Opera. Further international engagements have included appearances with Alan Gilbert at the Lucerne Festival in Porgy and Bess and her debut at the Opéra National de Lyon as Old Lady (Candide). In North America, Vaughn recently appeared as Amneris (Aida) at Fort Worth Opera and Cincinnati Opera and made her Opera Theatre of Saint Louis debut as Monisha in Scott Joplin's Treemonisha. From 2010 to 2018 she was an ensemble member at the Semperoper Dresden, where she was heard as Herodias (Salome), Azucena (Il Trovatore), Venus (Tannhäuser), Ulrica (Un Ballo in Maschera), and Ortrud (Lohengrin), among others. From 1996 to 2006 she was a principal artist at the Stuttgart State Opera, where she was awarded the title of Kammersängerin for her successes in such roles as Eboli (Don Carlo), Widow Begbick (Mahagonny), Venus (Tannhäuser), Giulietta (Tales of Hoffmann), Arnalta (L'incoronazione di Poppea), Fricka (Die Walküre), Waltraute (Götterdämmerung), and Erda (Siegfried and Das Rheingold). Her discography includes the Grammy-winning Metropolitan Opera recording of Porgy and Bess, Stuttgart State Theater's Der Ring des Nibelungen, and her solo CD, Christmas at My House.

title: BYOB (bring your own business)
description:
Calling all savvy shoppers and student vendors!
BYOB @ IU welcomes all students to showcase their businesses at our bi-monthly events, currently held at Dunn Meadow or indoors in the IMU. While sellers must be IU students, our events are open to the wider Bloomington community. You’ll find a diverse range of student-run businesses offering everything from vintage clothing and jewelry to art prints, plants, and crochet items. Set up your table, set your prices, and engage with various shoppers.
Our events highlight the creativity and entrepreneurial spirit of our members while providing invaluable real-world experience in marketing, negotiating, and customer interaction. BYOB @ IU is more than just an organization—it's a movement dedicated to empowering the next generation of entrepreneurs, helping them bring their ideas to life and connect with a broader audience.

title: Spring 2026 Colloquium Series with Lucia Guerra Reyes
description: The Center for Latin American and Caribbean Studies is proud to present the continuation of its Colloquium Series for Spring 2026.  We welcome Dr. Lucia Guerra Reyes as she presents Circulating Latin America: Ethical Interventions from the Global South.Lucia Guerra-Reyes is an associate professor in applied health science. She is a medical anthropologist and interdisciplinary researcher. Guerra-Reyes investigates the complexities of access to sexual and reproductive health and care for marginalized communities. She is interested in understanding how national policies of culturally appropriate care are enacted at the local level; how health care providers view their role in extending care to under-served communities; and how women make decisions about their reproductive lives.      The CLACS Spring 2026 Colloquium meets Mondays from February through April, with faculty and guest speakers engaging Latin America and the Caribbean through research on history, culture, language, politics, and the arts. Check the CLACS Calendar (https://events.iu.edu/clacs/) for details.
source: IU Hamilton Lugar School, IU La Casa Latino Cultural Center

title: Preview Screening of "Creating Resolve"
description: This film explores the transformative power of creative resolve in global health. From the frontlines of Rwanda's HIV fight to the historic eradication of smallpox, we go behind the scenes with the activists and experts who refused to accept the status quo. It's more than a history of medicine; it's a blueprint for a future where health is a right, not a privilege, and international solidarity is our strongest vaccine.

title: ASIU Meeting
description:
Advoates for Science at IU will host monthly in-person meetings for all undergraduate and graduate student members. During these meetings, we will facilitate discussions about current club events, updates, and opportunities. Additionally, beyond these discussions, the respective committees within ASIU (political action, media outreach, event planning, and written journalism) will meet in order to collaborate on organization action items and plan for future initiatives. This meeting is going to be a necessary and consistent opportunity for all student members of ASIU to meet in person, discuss the most pressing environmental and scientific policy issues at the time, and work together on building new ideas for both personal development and broader action. title: Workshop on Measuring International Development, Advancing Equity
description: Humanity stands at a crossroads. While we have made incredible strides in human development, we also face existential threats—from climate change and pandemics to growing polarization and inequality. To overcome these threats, we must work together to advance a coherent vision of human development centered on guaranteeing every person a dignified, or at least minimally good, life. This workshop addresses the conceptual and practical gaps in how we measure social progress. A narrow focus on GDP is no longer sufficient; it is distribution-insensitive and fails to capture the non-market values essential for mutual flourishing. This workshop will explore how to think about development, develop better metrics, and put them into practice.

Denim Day
location: 10th St and Fee at Arboretum - Table 1
description:
Denim Day is recognized on the last Wednesday in April in honor of Sexual Assault Awareness Month. The campaign began after a ruling by the Italian Supreme Court to overturn a rape conviction because the justices felt that since the victim was wearing tight jeans she must have helped the person who raped her remove them, thereby implying consent. Following the reversal, the women in the Italian Parliament came to work wearing jeans in solidarity with the victim.
Join the Office for Sexual Violence Prevention and Victim Advocacy and sexual assault survivors worldwide in wearing jeans and learning about more you can do to provide a safe and supportive environment.

Hosted by: Office for Sexual Violence Prevention and Victim Advocacy

title: Mindful Makers
description:
Join us for Mindful Makers, a weekly event built around being creative, meeting new people and feeling more confident in yourself and your wellness. Each week has a theme - come for one or come for all! We're here to have fun and make cool things.
January 21: Semester Vision Boards
Picture your new semester! Create a vision board that will motivate you to achieve this semester. Learn some tips on how to set great goals to help you achieve that vision.

January 28: Meditation and Aromatherapy
Ever been curious about aromatherapy and how it works? Here's your opportunity to learn more! While you're here, we'll also practice deep breathing techniques, progressive relaxation and guided visualization, and other simple techniques that don't require any special skills or equipment

February 4: Crochet the Stress Away
Whether you're a crochet expert or have never picked up a hook in your life, we have a place for you! Join us, learn basic crochet stitches, and talk about ways to have fun on campus and in the community.

February 11: Origami Ornaments and Overcoming
Sometimes life throws us curve balls. In this Mindful Makers, we'll go through the process of folding origami hearts or cranes, and using those to make beautiful ornaments that reflect challenges we've overcome. These cranes carry hope on their wings!

February 18: Air Clay Emotions
What does your angry monster look like? What about your joy? Use air clay to sculpt mini-monsters representing both supportive and challenging emotions, and learn ways of taming your more wild ones. Remember - not all monsters are scary. Sometimes they're pretty great!

February 25: Love Letters to Your Body and Cookie Decorating
Sometimes we need reminders of how great our bodies can be. In this week's MM, you'll learn simple bookbinding skills to make an accordion book, and fill those pages with what we like about ourselves and our bodies. Join our nutritionist, Katie Shepherd, for cookie decorating while you're here!

March 4: Watercolor Your Values
One of the most powerful motivators we have is our personal values. Paint a values portrait, and discuss how you can tie your goals into your values to keep yourself moving forward.

March 11: Sleep Vision Boards
Getting a good night's sleep is critical towards functioning at your best - but truthfully, most of us don't get nearly the quantity or quality of sleep we need. Create a sleep vision board as you learn ways to improve your sleep habits and why sleep is so important!

March 25: Create Your Own Journal
And we do mean from scratch! Learn simple Japanese stitchwork bookbinding to make your own book, decorate it to suit your tastes, and create a journal you can use however you like! (And if you're new to journaling, we can work on that as well!)

April 1: Perler Beads and Mindfulness
Being mindful doesn't take anything special. Sometimes it can be found in the smallest of things - like the little perler beads! Make yourself an ornament while discovering ways mindfulness can be a part of the everyday things you already do!

April 8: Gaming with Friends
Do you know that the oldest known game board is 9000 years old? We've been playing games for a long time! Well, not us personally. We're just going to play for a couple of hours. Come and join us!

April 15: Resinate
No, that's not a typo. Learn the basics of resin crafting and make your own ornaments or magnets. While here, share your favorite hobbies and why they matter to you - maybe you'll inspire someone else!

April 22: Affirmation Jars
At the end of the semester, sometimes we need reminders of what motivates us, makes us special, or just what matters to us. Create your own affirmation jar and fill it up with all those good things. When you're feeling low, pull one out and give yourself a boost!

April 29: Keep Growing
Whether you're looking forward to graduation, a summer off, or one filled with activity, summer is a great time for growth. Share your upcoming opportunities as you decorate a pot and select a new plant to nurture. Like plants, we need care to keep growing, so share your ideas for how you're going to care for yourself!

Hosted by: Health Promotion

Additional Information can be found at: https://beinvolved.indiana.edu/event/11977509

title: New Music Ensemble – David Dzubay, director; Emma Cardon-Wake, composer
description: Repertoire  Julia Wolfe: Tell me everything (1994)Yuting Tan: Amnesia (2023)Emma Cardon-Wake: GIRLHOOD (2025, premiere)*  *Georgina Joshi Composition CommissionAbout the ConductorDavid Dzubay's compositions have been performed in the United States, Europe, Canada, Mexico, and Asia by the symphony orchestras of Aspen, Atlanta, Baltimore, Boston, Cincinnati, Detroit, Fort Wayne, Guangxi, Guiyang, Honolulu, Kansas City, Louisville, Memphis, Minnesota, Oregon, Oakland, St. Louis and Vancouver; the American Composers Orchestra, National Symphonies of Ireland and Mexico, New World Symphony, National Repertory Orchestra, and New York Youth Symphony; and ensembles including the Grossman Ensemble, Le Nouvel Ensemble Moderne, Onix, Manhattan and St. Louis Brass Quintets, Voices of Change, the Alexander, Orion and Pacifica string quartets, the League/ISCM, Earplay, and the San Francisco Contemporary Music Players. His music has been championed by conductors including James DePreist, George Hanson, Keith Lockhart, David Loebel, Michael Morgan, Eiji Oue, Richard Pittman, Iván del Prado, Mark Russell Smith, Lawrence Leighton Smith, Michael Stern, Carl Topilow, David Wiley, Kirk Trevor, Thomas Wilkins, and David Zinman. Honors include two Fromm Music Foundation commissions; Guggenheim, MacDowell, Yaddo, Copland House, and Djerassi fellowships; the Kuhmo Chamber Music Festival Composition Competition, Indianapolis Chamber Orchestra Composition Competition, Utah Arts Festival Commission, William Revelli and Walter Beeler Memorial Prizes, Wayne Peterson Prize; and grants from the Aaron Copland Fund for Music for two portrait CDs. Currently professor of music and director of the New Music Ensemble at the Indiana University Jacobs School of Music in Bloomington, Dzubay was previously on the faculty of the University of North Texas in Denton. Since 2011, he has taught composition for three weeks each summer at the Brevard Music Center. From 1995 to 1998, he served as composer-consultant to the Minnesota Orchestra and during the 2005-06 season, he was Meet the Composer/American Symphony Orchestra League Music Alive Composer-in-Residence with the Green Bay Symphony Orchestra. Dzubay has conducted at the Tanglewood, Aspen, and June in Buffalo festivals as well as the National Symphony of Columbia, Grossman Ensemble, League of Composers Orchestra, Pittsburgh New Music Ensemble, Music from China, and Voices of Change.The Georgina Joshi Composition Commission Award  A vision of Louise Addicott-Joshi and made possible by a gift from The Georgina Joshi Foundation, the annual Georgina Joshi Composition Commission is awarded to a student composer in support of a new work for solo voice and instrumental ensemble to be premiered and recorded by the New Music Ensemble. The award is presented in conjunction with the Composition Department's annual Dean's Prizes.Georgina Joshi earned her bachelor's degree in music (with honors) from the Royal College of Music in London and pursued a master's degree in the Jacobs School of Music. While at IU, Joshi appeared as a soloist in major choral performances and IU Opera Theater productions. An enthusiastic performer, she was featured in a variety of regional ensembles and festivals, and appeared as a soloist in England, Wales, Romania, and Greece.

title: Open Mic Night at The Comedy Attic
location: The Comedy Attic,123 S Walnut St, Bloomington, IN 47408, USA
description:
Every Wednesday at 8PM is Comedy Attic Open Mic Night. Bloomington has one of the best local comedy scenes in the country, and it all starts with our open mic night!

Each comedian is asked to do no more than five minutes of completely original material. While we do not care about foul language in your set, we do not allow racist, homophobic, transphobic or rape/female bashing jokes. If you have to ask if it's racist, homophobic, transphobic or a rape joke, it probably is.

Comedians that wish to participate are asked to e-mail comedyattic@gmail.com mailto:comedyattic@gmail.com . Newcomers and any participants who are not paid professionals will be required to bring audience members to see them. This is non-negotiable. So if you plan on signing up, be sure to invite as many friends as you can. Good luck!

title: U.S. Economic Cooperation with Nations in the Middle East and Central Asia: Working Towards Regional Stabilities
description: Nations of the Middle East and Central Asia have emerged over the last decade as important partners to the United States both economically and strategically. Economic ties have expanded with the United States through two-way direct investments, third country cooperation, collaboration in innovation technologies and supply chain diversifications. At the same time, the economic integration of these two regions has also increased, with both state and private investment from the GCC countries into Turkey and Central Asia. In the geopolitical sphere, Middle Eastern nations seek to maintain good relationships with China and Russia, while enhancing ties with Washington. Central Asian countries seek to expand political and economic links with the United States to diversify from Russia and China. This discussion will focus on the business and economic ties between America and these regions as they look toward regional stability and global economic competitiveness.     Khush Choksy is senior vice president of International Member Relations at the U.S. Chamber of Commerce. In this capacity, he oversees member engagement and fundraising across the International Division and its over 30 business councils and programs. He also leads the Chamber's programs in the Middle East, Türkiye, and Central Asia.  As the head of International Member Relations, Choksy is responsible for member services across all the U.S. Chamber's companies that are part of its International Division.  As senior vice president of the Middle East and Türkiye Department, Choksy's responsibilities include oversight of several bilateral business councils and programs. Under his leadership, the Middle East portfolio has expanded to become the U.S. Chamber's largest international program and represents over 250 companies. Choksy also led the effort to integrate the U.S.-India Business Council into the U.S. Chamber.  Before joining the Chamber, Choksy was a director at Chemonics International. There, he led teams developing economic ties between the United States and the Middle East. He lived and worked in Jordan for seven years, and also lived and worked in India, Sri Lanka, and the U.K. Prior to that, Choksy worked for the Parsons Corporation. He is a recognized thought leader on the role of international business in the global economy.  Choksy received his M.B.A. in strategic management from the Wharton School of the University of Pennsylvania and his B.A., magna cum laude, from Hamilton College. He lives with his wife and daughter in Chevy Chase, Maryland.

title: Global Competencies Skills Development Series: Off-the-Record Dinner on Careers in Intelligence & National Security
location: GLOBAL & INTERNATIONAL STUDIES BUILDING - Room Number: 1122
description: What does it actually take to build a career in intelligence or the White House?  Join a small, off-the-record dinner with Judd Devermont, former National Intelligence Officer and senior National Security Council official who helped shape U.S. policy at the highest levels of government.  This is not a speech. It is a direct conversation.  He has worked inside the intelligence community, coordinated across agencies, and advised senior decision-makers on complex global crises. He has seen how power moves, how policy is negotiated, and how careers are built or stalled.  We will talk about:      Breaking into intelligence and national security          Moving between government, analysis, and policy roles          Negotiation and coalition building in real-world settings          The risks, trade-offs, and personal decisions behind public service          What early-career professionals get wrong      If you are serious about a future in intelligence, national security, or global affairs, this is a rare chance to ask unfiltered questions in a closed-door setting.  Space is limited.
source: IU Hamilton Lugar School, IU Kelley School of Business

title: You (probably) Haven't Seen This Before
description: Dive deep into the archival collections of IU Libraries Moving Image Archive through a new monthly screening series of short films! Each month, rare and unique archival films will be presented focusing on underrepresented narratives and underrepresented motion-picture practices that push on and expand the spectrum of what is considered "cinema."  In an effort to keep audiences open-minded and with limited pre-conceptions of these rare works of celluloid, descriptions of films to be shown will not be shared prior to screenings. Join us based on announced themes and the expectation that the works you see will fall outside of Hollywood tropes, outside of mainstream commercial expectations, and outside of conventional filmmaking practices. Trust us, you (probably) haven't seen these films before!  This program is curated by Dominick Rivers and hosted by the IU Libraries Moving Image Archive Screening Room.  Please remember to register below.
